Author: Holistic Bravo
Recipe Card Ingredients:
**Dough:**
- 2½ cups all-purpose flour
- 1 stick (8 tbsp) unsalted butter, cold and cut into small cubes
- 1 large egg, room temperature
- 1 tsp fine salt
- ½ cup cold water (you may not need all of it)

**Filling:**
- Guava paste (pasta de guayaba), sliced into pieces approximately 1½-2 inches long and ¼ inch thick
- Parmesan cheese, cut into thick slices similar in size to guava pieces (or substitute cream cheese for creamier filling)

**For Finishing:**
- 1 egg, beaten (for egg wash - optional but recommended for golden shine)
Recipe Card Instructions:
**Making the Dough:**
1. In large bowl, whisk together 2½ cups flour and 1 tsp salt to distribute salt evenly.
2. Add cold butter cubes to flour. Using fingertips, rub butter into flour until mixture resembles coarse crumbs with some pea-sized butter pieces remaining, about 3-5 minutes. Don't rush this step - those butter pieces create the flaky layers.
3. Lightly beat egg in small bowl. Add beaten egg to flour-butter mixture and stir with fork to begin incorporating.
4. Slowly add cold water a few tablespoons at a time, mixing with hands after each addition. Stop when dough starts to come together into cohesive ball - you may not need all the water. Dough should be slightly tacky but not sticky.
5. Form dough into smooth ball with your hands - don't overwork it, just bring it together.
6. Wrap tightly in plastic wrap or place in covered container. Refrigerate at least 30 minutes, up to 1 hour (or up to 2 days ahead).

**Assembly:**
1. Preheat oven to 375°F. Line large baking sheet with parchment paper.
2. Remove dough from refrigerator and divide into 10 equal pieces. Roll each piece between palms into smooth ball.
3. On lightly floured surface, use rolling pin to flatten each ball into circle about 4-5 inches in diameter and roughly 1/8 inch thick.
4. Place one slice of guava paste in center of each circle, then place one slice of parmesan cheese directly on top of guava.
5. Fold dough circle in half to create half-moon shape, bringing one edge over to meet the other. Press edges together firmly to seal.
6. Crimp sealed edges with fork tines, pressing down along entire edge. This ensures filling won't leak and creates pretty pattern.
7. Place formed empanadas on prepared baking sheet, spacing about 1 inch apart.

**Baking:**
1. Optional but recommended: Brush tops of empanadas with beaten egg using pastry brush for golden, shiny finish.
2. Bake at 375°F for 20-25 minutes until golden brown and crispy. Edges should be deeply golden.
3. Remove from oven and let cool about 5 minutes before serving - filling will be molten hot!
4. Serve warm for best experience. Store leftovers in airtight container up to 3 days at room temperature or 5 days refrigerated. Reheat in 325°F oven for 5-7 minutes to re-crisp.
Nutrition:
Per empanada (1 of 10):
- Calories: Approximately 200-250
- Protein: Approximately 5-6g
- Carbohydrates: Approximately 28-32g
- Fat: Approximately 9-11g
- Fiber: Approximately 1-2g

Name: Halloween High Protein Sweet Treat
Short Description: These 4-ingredient frozen treats taste like pumpkin cheesecake and are perfect for satisfying your sweet tooth while hitting your protein goals.
Introduction:
Happy Halloween! This festive treat is what I make when my sweet tooth comes out to play - which tends to be every day, honestly. It's one of those magical recipes where cottage cheese completely transforms into something that tastes like pumpkin cheesecake when blended smooth. This was actually my first time using cottage cheese in this recipe, and I couldn't believe how well it worked.

These frozen treats are perfect for meal prepping at the beginning of the week. I love having a batch in the freezer so I can grab one whenever I need something sweet without derailing my protein goals. With just four simple ingredients, they come together quickly and satisfy that dessert craving while sneaking in a solid protein boost. I created this recipe as a Halloween-themed treat, but honestly, I make these year-round because they're that good.

Whether you want just a light drizzle of chocolate on top or you're going full chocolate-dipped indulgence, these treats adapt to your mood. Plus, they're customizable with toppings like mini M&Ms, crushed nuts, or coconut flakes. It's sweet treat o'clock somewhere!

The best part about these is how they completely transform once frozen. The texture becomes almost ice cream-like, creamy and smooth with that classic pumpkin spice flavor that reminds you of all the best fall desserts. I've served these to people who claim they hate cottage cheese, and they couldn't even tell it was in there. The pumpkin and maple syrup do such a good job of masking any cottage cheese flavor that you truly just taste pumpkin cheesecake.
Why You'll Love This:
- Only 4 ingredients needed and ready in 40 minutes including freeze time - so simple
- Packed with protein from cottage cheese so you can indulge guilt-free while hitting your macros for the day
- Tastes like pumpkin cheesecake when you blend it - I promise you won't even know there's cottage cheese in it
- Perfect for meal prep - I make a batch on Sunday and enjoy throughout the week whenever I need something sweet
- Completely customizable chocolate coating from light drizzle to thick chocolate shell depending on your mood and goals
Tips:
- Blend the cottage cheese very smooth before adding other ingredients to eliminate any graininess - this is crucial
- Use 100% pure pumpkin puree, not pumpkin pie filling which contains added sugar and spices that will throw off the flavor
- The mixture should taste like pumpkin cheesecake before freezing - if it doesn't, adjust sweetness with more maple syrup
- Pour chocolate on top for a thinner coating or dip the entire treat for a thick chocolate shell - your choice
- Add toppings immediately after chocolate coating before it sets - once chocolate hardens, toppings won't stick
- Store in an airtight container in the freezer for up to several weeks - they actually get better over time
- Make these in silicone molds for easy removal without breaking - I learned this the hard way with ice cube trays
Ingredients Overview:
The magic of this recipe starts with cottage cheese as your protein powerhouse. When you blend it completely smooth, it creates this incredibly creamy, cheesecake-like base without any of that typical cottage cheese texture or taste. You'll want to use full-fat or low-fat cottage cheese depending on your preference - both work beautifully. I usually go with low-fat to keep the calories down, but full-fat creates an even creamier result if you're not counting macros.

Next comes the pumpkin puree, and this is important: make sure you're using 100% pure pumpkin puree, not the pumpkin pie filling that comes pre-sweetened and spiced. The pure puree gives you that authentic fall pumpkin flavor plus fiber and nutrients. You'll use about half a can, which is perfect because you can save the other half for another batch or add it to smoothies. I always buy a few extra cans in fall so I have them on hand year-round.

For sweetness, maple syrup is the perfect natural choice that complements the pumpkin flavor without overpowering it. Just two tablespoons is enough to make the mixture taste like dessert, but you can adjust to your preference. I sometimes add a tiny bit of vanilla extract too for extra depth. And finally, chocolate for coating - use whatever kind you love, whether that's dark, milk, or even white chocolate. The amount depends entirely on your chocolate preference and whether you want to drizzle or fully dip. I'm a dark chocolate person myself, but milk chocolate creates more of that traditional Halloween candy vibe.
Instructions Overview:
The process is incredibly simple - blend the cottage cheese until smooth, add the pumpkin and maple syrup, blend again, pour into molds, and freeze. The most important step is really blending that cottage cheese well. You want zero graininess, completely smooth and creamy like a milkshake. This usually takes about 60-90 seconds in a good blender.

Once you pour the mixture into molds, it needs about 30 minutes in the freezer to firm up enough to hold its shape for the chocolate coating. You don't want them rock-solid at this point - just firm enough to handle. If they're too frozen, the chocolate won't stick properly.

The chocolate coating is where you get to customize these to your preference. For a light coating, just drizzle melted chocolate over the top - this is the lower-calorie option that still gives you that chocolate fix. For full indulgence, dip the entire frozen treat into melted chocolate so it gets coated on all sides. I like to hold them with a fork and let the excess chocolate drip off for a smooth finish.

If you're adding toppings like mini M&Ms or nuts, work quickly and add them immediately after coating while the chocolate is still wet. Once the chocolate hardens, toppings won't stick. Then back in the freezer for a final 10-15 minutes to set everything, and you're done. I usually make these while I'm cooking dinner so they're ready by the time I'm done eating and want dessert.
FAQ:
**Can you actually taste the cottage cheese?**
No! This is the magic of this recipe. When you blend the cottage cheese completely smooth and combine it with the pumpkin and maple syrup, it genuinely tastes like pumpkin cheesecake. The cottage cheese just provides protein and creaminess without any detectable cottage cheese flavor. I've served these to cottage cheese haters who couldn't tell at all.

**How long do these last in the freezer?**
They'll keep in an airtight container in the freezer for several weeks - probably up to a month or even longer. The combination of being frozen and coated in chocolate helps preserve them perfectly. That said, they're so delicious they usually don't last more than a week in my house! I can't resist grabbing one every time I open the freezer.

**Can I use Greek yogurt instead of cottage cheese?**
You could try it, but cottage cheese is really the star here because it provides more protein per serving and creates a better texture when frozen. Greek yogurt might result in a slightly icier texture and won't have quite the same protein punch. I haven't tested it personally, so I can't guarantee the results, but if you try it, let me know how it goes!

- High Protein Peanut Butter Chocolate Truffles
- Chocolate Chip Pumpkin Muffins
- Gluten-Free Pumpkin Cake Pops
Author: Holistic Bravo
Recipe Card Ingredients:
**Base:**
- 1 cup cottage cheese (full-fat or low-fat - both work great)
- 1/2 can (approximately 1/2 cup) 100% pure pumpkin puree (NOT pumpkin pie filling)
- 2 tbsp pure maple syrup (adjust to taste - add more if you like things sweeter)

**Coating:**
- Chocolate chips or chocolate bar (amount as desired depending on coating preference - approximately 1/2 cup for drizzling, 1-1.5 cups for full dipping)
- Mini M&Ms, chopped nuts, or coconut flakes (optional, for decoration)
Recipe Card Instructions:
1. Add 1 cup cottage cheese to blender. Blend on high for 60-90 seconds until completely smooth with zero graininess - texture should be like thick cream. Scrape down sides if needed.
2. Add pumpkin puree (1/2 cup) and maple syrup (2 tbsp) to blended cottage cheese. Blend on high for another 30-60 seconds until everything is uniformly combined and color is even throughout.
3. Taste the mixture at this point - it should taste like pumpkin cheesecake! If you want it sweeter, add 1-2 tsp more maple syrup and blend again.
4. Pour mixture into silicone molds, ice cube trays, or small paper cups. Fill each cavity about 3/4 full. Silicone molds work best for easy removal.
5. Place filled molds in freezer for approximately 30 minutes. Check them - you want them firm enough to hold shape when handled, but not rock-solid frozen yet.
6. While treats are freezing, melt chocolate in microwave-safe bowl using 30-second intervals, stirring thoroughly between each interval, until completely smooth (usually 2-3 intervals total). Or use double boiler for more gentle melting.
7. Remove treats from molds. If they're sticking, let them sit at room temperature for 30-60 seconds or run warm water on bottom of mold very briefly.
8. Now for chocolate coating - two options:
   - Light coating: Use spoon to drizzle melted chocolate over top of each treat (uses less chocolate, fewer calories)
   - Thick shell: Hold treat with fork and dip entire thing into melted chocolate, letting excess drip off (more indulgent, full coverage)
9. If adding decorations (mini M&Ms, nuts, coconut), sprinkle them on immediately after coating while chocolate is still wet and sticky.
10. Place coated treats on parchment-lined plate or baking sheet. Return to freezer for 10-15 minutes until chocolate is completely set and hardened.
11. Transfer to airtight container or freezer bag and store in freezer up to several weeks. Enjoy straight from freezer whenever sweet tooth strikes - they're perfect grab-and-go treats!
Nutrition:
Per treat (approximate, based on 10 servings):
- Calories: 80-120 (depending on chocolate amount)
- Protein: 4-5g
- Carbohydrates: 10-15g
- Fat: 2-6g
- Fiber: 1-2g
- Sugar: 6-8g

Name: High Protein Bagels
Short Description: These customizable 3-ingredient base bagels can transform into matcha cranberry, double chocolate, or everything bagels - all ready in 26 minutes.
Introduction:
These high-protein bagels are about to revolutionize your breakfast routine. With a simple base of just three ingredients - Greek yogurt, flour, and baking powder - you can transform them into whatever flavor your heart desires. Sweet, savory, matcha-infused, chocolate-loaded, or classic everything seasoning, these bagels do it all.

What I love most about this recipe is how meal-prep friendly it is. You can store the uncooked bagels in the fridge for up to a week and just pop them in the air fryer whenever you're feeling like a fresh, warm, high-protein snack. I created multiple variations, and the double chocolate version turned out so delicious I actually forgot to press record on the original ending because I was too busy eating them!

The base dough comes together in minutes, and from there you can get creative with inclusions. The matcha cranberry version is refreshing and beautiful with that vibrant green color, the double chocolate is basically dessert in bagel form, and the everything bagel is a classic savory option. Eating them warm is definitely my favorite way to enjoy them, especially with some salted butter and honey on the sweet versions.

I've experimented with so many variations of these beyond the three shown here - blueberry with lemon zest, cinnamon raisin, jalapeño cheddar, even pumpkin spice. The base is so versatile that once you master it, you can truly make any flavor combination you can imagine. They've become my go-to breakfast when I want something more substantial than just Greek yogurt but don't want to spend a lot of time cooking.
Why You'll Love This:
- Only 3 base ingredients create a high-protein foundation thanks to Greek yogurt - it's genius in its simplicity
- Store uncooked bagels in the fridge for a week for easy grab-and-go breakfast options throughout the week
- Completely customizable from sweet to savory with endless flavor combinations limited only by your imagination
- Each bagel provides 12-15g protein from the Greek yogurt base alone, more with protein powder additions
- Air fryer makes them perfectly crispy outside and soft inside in just 16 minutes - no boiling required like traditional bagels
Tips:
- Use non-fat Greek yogurt for maximum protein with minimal fat - this gives you the best macro profile
- Bread flour is not required but all-purpose flour works perfectly fine - I use AP flour exclusively
- For the chocolate variation, add extra Greek yogurt one spoonful at a time until dough forms - protein powder makes it drier than you'd expect
- Store uncooked bagels in the fridge and air fry fresh whenever you want one - game changer for busy mornings
- Best enjoyed warm right out of the air fryer - that's when the texture is absolute perfection
- If you don't have an air fryer, bake at 350°F for 20-25 minutes, flipping halfway through baking time
- Sweet versions are incredible with butter and honey or cream cheese and jam
- Savory versions are perfect for breakfast sandwiches - I make egg and cheese sandwiches with the everything bagels
Ingredients Overview:
The genius of these bagels lies in the Greek yogurt. This isn't just adding protein - the Greek yogurt actually replaces both eggs and traditional leavening agents while creating a tender, slightly tangy base that tastes remarkably like real bagels. You'll want to use non-fat Greek yogurt for the best protein-to-calorie ratio, though full-fat works too if that's what you have on hand. The protein content in Greek yogurt is what makes these so satiating.

All-purpose flour creates the structure of the bagels. You'll need equal parts Greek yogurt and flour by weight (100g each for the base recipe), which makes it super easy to remember and scale up or down. The combination of yogurt and flour creates what's known as a "2-ingredient dough" that's become popular for good reason - it works! No yeast, no long rise times, no complicated techniques.

Baking powder and salt round out the base with just a teaspoon of each. The baking powder helps create a bit of lift and chewiness that makes these feel more like traditional bagels, while the salt enhances flavor and balances the tanginess of the yogurt. From there, the variations get fun. Matcha powder adds a beautiful green color and earthy flavor without overwhelming the bagel - just 5g is perfect. Dried cranberries add pops of tartness and chewiness in the matcha version. For the double chocolate bagels, chocolate protein powder amps up the protein even more while creating a rich chocolate flavor, and mini chocolate chips make them legitimately feel like dessert for breakfast. And for the everything bagel, well, everything bagel seasoning is all you need to sprinkle on top before air frying - it's the easiest variation.
Instructions Overview:
The process is incredibly straightforward - mix the dry ingredients, add the Greek yogurt, knead until it comes together, then customize with your chosen flavors. The dough should be slightly sticky but workable. If it's too dry, add a tiny bit more yogurt. If it's too sticky, add a bit more flour. You'll get a feel for the right consistency after making these once.

For the chocolate variation, the protein powder absorbs a lot of moisture, so you'll need to add extra Greek yogurt gradually until the dough comes together properly. Every protein powder is different in how much liquid it absorbs, so this is something you adjust by feel rather than exact measurements.

Shaping the bagels is simple - divide the dough in half, roll each piece into a ball, poke a hole through the center with your finger, and gently stretch it into a bagel shape. The hole will close up slightly during cooking, so make it bigger than you think it needs to be.

The air fryer is perfect for these because it creates that crispy exterior while keeping the interior soft and tender. Eight minutes per side at 350°F is the magic formula. They come out golden brown and perfect every time. If you're using an oven instead, the baking time is slightly longer but the results are still great - they just won't be quite as crispy on the outside.
FAQ:
**Can I store these bagels?**
Absolutely! In fact, that's one of the best parts of this recipe. You can store the uncooked, shaped bagels in an airtight container in the fridge for up to a week. Just pull one out and air fry it fresh whenever you want a warm bagel. This makes meal prep so easy! I usually make a big batch on Sunday and have fresh bagels all week long.

**Why does the chocolate version need more Greek yogurt?**
Protein powder absorbs a lot of moisture, which makes the dough dry and crumbly if you don't compensate for it. By adding extra Greek yogurt a spoonful at a time, you're rehydrating the dough until it comes together into a cohesive ball. Every protein powder is different in terms of absorption, so add yogurt gradually until you get the right consistency. Start with one tablespoon and add more as needed.

**Can I bake these instead of using an air fryer?**
Yes! If you don't have an air fryer, bake them in a regular oven at 350°F for about 20-25 minutes total, flipping them halfway through at the 10-12 minute mark. They won't get quite as crispy on the outside, but they'll still be delicious and perfectly cooked through. I made these in the oven for months before I got an air fryer.

- High Protein Veggie Wraps
- McGriddle Breakfast Sandwich
Author: Holistic Bravo
Recipe Card Ingredients:
**Base Recipe (makes 2 bagels):**
- 100g all-purpose flour (about 3/4 cup, but weighing is most accurate)
- 100g non-fat Greek yogurt (about 1/3 cup - use thick, high-protein yogurt)
- 1 tsp baking powder
- 1 tsp fine salt

**Matcha Cranberry Variation:**
- Base recipe ingredients
- 5g matcha powder (about 1 tsp - use culinary grade matcha)
- 30g dried cranberries (about 3 tbsp)

**Double Chocolate Variation:**
- Base recipe ingredients
- 1 scoop (approximately 30g) chocolate protein powder
- 3/4 cup mini chocolate chips
- Extra Greek yogurt (add 1 spoonful at a time as needed - usually 2-4 tbsp total)

**Everything Bagel Variation:**
- Base recipe ingredients
- 2-3 tbsp everything bagel seasoning (for topping - don't skimp, be generous!)
Recipe Card Instructions:
1. In large bowl, whisk together 100g flour, 1 tsp baking powder, and 1 tsp salt until evenly distributed.
2. Add 100g Greek yogurt to dry ingredients. Use spoon to start mixing, then switch to hands when it gets too thick. Knead in bowl for 2-3 minutes until dough comes together. It should be slightly sticky but workable.
3. Now customize your flavor:
   - **Matcha Cranberry:** Add 5g matcha powder to dough and knead until color is evenly distributed and vibrant green throughout, about 1-2 minutes. Fold in 30g dried cranberries, distributing evenly.
   - **Double Chocolate:** Add 1 scoop chocolate protein powder and mix thoroughly. Dough will become very dry and crumbly. Add extra Greek yogurt 1 tbsp at a time, mixing after each addition, until mixture forms cohesive ball (usually needs 2-4 tbsp). Fold in 3/4 cup mini chocolate chips throughout dough.
   - **Everything:** Proceed with plain base dough (seasoning goes on top before cooking).
4. Divide dough into 2 equal pieces. Roll each piece between palms into smooth ball first.
5. Use index finger to poke hole through center of each ball. Gently stretch and rotate to create bagel shape with decent-sized hole (at least 1.5 inches diameter - it will shrink during cooking).
6. If making everything bagels, generously sprinkle everything seasoning on top of each bagel now, pressing gently so it adheres well.
7. Preheat air fryer to 350°F for 3-5 minutes.
8. Place shaped bagels in air fryer basket with space between them (don't crowd - cook in batches if needed).
9. Air fry for 8 minutes on first side without opening air fryer - let them develop nice golden crust.
10. Carefully flip bagels using tongs or spatula. Air fry for another 8 minutes on second side until both sides are golden brown and bagels are cooked through with no doughy center.
11. Remove from air fryer and let cool 1-2 minutes, but these are absolute best enjoyed warm!
   - **Sweet versions:** Slice and slather with butter and drizzle with honey, or spread with cream cheese and jam
   - **Everything bagels:** Classic with cream cheese, or make breakfast sandwich with eggs, cheese, and your favorite toppings

**Oven Method (if no air fryer):**
Preheat oven to 350°F. Place bagels on parchment-lined baking sheet. Bake 20-25 minutes total, flipping at 10-12 minute mark, until golden brown on both sides.
Nutrition:
Per bagel (base recipe):
- Calories: 180-220 (plain/everything), 250-280 (chocolate)
- Protein: 12-15g (base), 18-22g (with protein powder)
- Carbohydrates: 30-35g
- Fat: 2-4g (base), 8-10g (chocolate)
- Fiber: 1-2g

Name: High Protein Cookie Dough
Short Description: This plant-based, no-bake edible cookie dough is safe to eat and ready in 5 minutes - the perfect guilt-free sweet treat.
Introduction:
You know those days when you come home from work and just need a little pick-me-up? That's exactly when this recipe was born. I had one of those longer days and wanted to make something sweet and comforting that would come together quickly - and this edible cookie dough was the answer.

This is one of those recipes that feels like a hug in a bowl. It's completely plant-based, vegan, non-dairy, and here's the best part: there's absolutely no chance of getting salmonella because there are no eggs or raw flour involved. Almond flour is totally safe to eat uncooked, which means you can go from craving to eating in about five minutes flat.

The recipe uses PB powder (powdered peanut butter) which keeps the calories and fat lower while maintaining that classic peanut butter cookie dough flavor, but you can absolutely use regular nut butter if that's what you have or prefer. This is your sweet treat - you get to make it exactly how you like it. And the best part? You don't have to share it with anybody. Well, don't share it with your dog if you add chocolate chips - chocolate is toxic to dogs, as I always remind people!

What I love most about this recipe is how it satisfies that cookie dough craving without any of the guilt or food safety concerns. We've all snuck bites of raw cookie dough while baking, but this is made specifically to be eaten raw, which means you can make a whole bowl and just go to town with a spoon. It's become my go-to comfort food for stressful days.
Why You'll Love This:
- Takes only 5 minutes from start to finish with no cooking required - perfect for instant gratification
- Completely safe to eat raw with no eggs or flour that needs to be cooked - zero food safety concerns
- High in protein from almond flour and PB powder while still tasting like dessert - you're getting nutrition
- Plant-based and vegan-friendly for those following dairy-free diets or just wanting cleaner ingredients
- Totally customizable with your favorite inclusions like candy pieces, sprinkles, or M&Ms - make it your own
Tips:
- Mix the PB powder with water in a separate bowl before adding to almond flour for the smoothest consistency - this prevents clumps
- Add water gradually - you want the peanut butter mixture thick, not runny, almost like natural peanut butter
- Use regular peanut butter, almond butter, or cashew butter if you don't have PB powder - all work great
- The recipe is easily doubled if you want to share (or just want more for yourself later!) - I usually make a double batch
- Store leftovers in the fridge for a firmer, fudgier texture that's almost like eating frozen cookie dough
- Add whatever inclusions make you happy - this is YOUR treat and you deserve to customize it
- No sharing required unless your roommate or partner looks really sad - then maybe share a spoonful
Ingredients Overview:
Almond flour is the base of this cookie dough, and it's what makes the whole thing work. Almond flour provides healthy fats, protein, and that slightly nutty flavor that tastes like actual cookie dough. Because it's essentially just finely ground almonds, it's completely safe to eat raw, which is what makes this recipe possible without any baking. You'll need 100g, which is about one cup. Make sure you're using almond flour (finely ground) not almond meal (coarser grind) - the texture is much better with flour.

Next comes the PB powder - I prefer PB Fit brand specifically for the macros. PB powder is made from peanuts that have had most of the oil pressed out, so you get all that peanut butter flavor and protein with significantly fewer calories and fat than regular peanut butter. A serving has about 5g protein and only 1.5g fat compared to regular peanut butter's 8g protein and 16g fat. That said, if you prefer regular peanut butter, almond butter, or cashew butter, go for it! The taste will be amazing either way, just note that your calories and fat will be higher. You'll use 50g of PB powder.

For sweetness, monk fruit sweetener is optional but recommended. Just one teaspoon of this zero-calorie natural sweetener enhances all the flavors without adding sugar or spiking your blood sugar. It has no bitter aftertaste like some stevia products. Of course, you can adjust the sweetness to your preference or skip it entirely if you like things less sweet. You could also use regular sugar, coconut sugar, or any sweetener you prefer.

And finally, chocolate chips! About one heaping tablespoon is the starting point, but honestly, add as much or as little as you like. This is the fun part where you can customize with candy pieces, M&Ms, sprinkles, or even crushed cookies if you're feeling extra. I usually add way more than one tablespoon because I'm a chocolate fiend.
Instructions Overview:
The key to getting the perfect texture is mixing the PB powder with water separately before adding it to the almond flour. This ensures you get a smooth, creamy peanut butter consistency without any dry pockets or clumps. You want it thick like natural peanut butter - spreadable but not runny.

Once you've got your PB mixture at the right consistency, you just mix it into the almond flour and watch it transform into cookie dough right before your eyes. It's honestly kind of magical how quickly it comes together. The almond flour absorbs the moisture from the peanut butter mixture and suddenly you have this perfect cookie dough texture.

Adding the monk fruit sweetener is optional but I highly recommend it - just one teaspoon makes everything taste more dessert-like and enhances the cookie dough flavor. Then comes the fun part: folding in whatever inclusions you want. Chocolate chips are classic, but I've done this with everything from peanut butter cups to dried fruit to crushed pretzels.

You can eat it immediately at room temperature, or pop it in the fridge for 20-30 minutes to firm it up into more of a fudgy texture. I personally love it cold from the fridge because it becomes almost truffle-like in texture. Either way, grab a spoon and enjoy straight from the bowl. This is not a recipe that needs to look pretty - it's pure comfort food.
FAQ:
**Is this actually safe to eat raw?**
Yes, absolutely! There are no eggs in this recipe, and almond flour is simply ground almonds which are perfectly safe to eat without cooking. Unlike recipes that use raw wheat flour (which can carry bacteria and should be heat-treated), this has zero food safety risks. Dig in with confidence! I've been making and eating this for years with no issues.

**Can I use regular peanut butter instead of PB powder?**
Totally! I just prefer PB Fit powder for the macros - it's lower in calories and fat while still providing protein and flavor. But regular peanut butter, almond butter, or cashew butter all work beautifully. Just note that your calorie and fat content will be higher, but it will taste amazing. You'll need about 1/3 cup of regular nut butter to replace the PB powder.

**How do I know how much water to add to the PB powder?**
Add water very gradually, about a tablespoon at a time, and mix thoroughly after each addition. You're looking for a thick, creamy consistency similar to natural peanut butter - spreadable but not runny. If you accidentally add too much water and it becomes runny, just mix in a bit more PB powder to thicken it back up. It's very forgiving.

Name: High Protein Tofu Pasta
Short Description: This two-ingredient homemade pasta uses tofu instead of eggs for a vegan, high-protein alternative that you genuinely cannot taste.
Introduction:
This recipe is an absolute game-changer for anyone who's vegan or plant-based and misses the protein boost that eggs provide in traditional pasta. I tried making tofu pasta for the first time while filming this video, and it immediately went into the rotation - that's how good it is.

The concept is brilliant in its simplicity: tofu replaces eggs as both the protein source and the binder for the flour. If you've made homemade pasta before with the classic flour-and-eggs method, this follows the same general process but swaps tofu for the eggs. The result? You get pasta that's significantly higher in protein than egg-free dried pasta, and once it's cooked and covered with your favorite sauce, you legitimately cannot taste the tofu at all.

What I love about this recipe is how it opens up the world of fresh homemade pasta to vegans who can't do eggs. Fresh pasta has this incredible tender texture that dried pasta just can't match, and now everyone gets to enjoy it. I kept the sauce simple in the video with just garlic, olive oil, salt, pepper, and some greens I didn't want to go bad, but you can sauce this pasta however you like - marinara, alfredo, pesto, whatever makes you happy.

The first time I made this, I was genuinely skeptical that you wouldn't taste the tofu. But after cooking it and tossing it with the sauce, I took a bite and was completely blown away. It just tastes like pasta. Good, fresh, tender pasta with a beautiful texture and none of that tofu flavor. It's one of those recipes that proves vegan cooking doesn't have to be complicated or involve specialty ingredients - just tofu and flour, and you're making fresh pasta.
Why You'll Love This:
- Only 2 ingredients create fresh homemade pasta that's vegan and egg-free - it's so simple it almost feels like cheating
- Provides 15-18g of protein per serving from tofu, much higher than regular pasta and even comparable to egg pasta
- You genuinely cannot taste the tofu once it's cooked and sauced - I was shocked by this
- Fresh pasta cooks in just 3-5 minutes compared to dried pasta's 10-12 minutes, making dinner faster
- Can be made ahead and stored in the fridge for 2-3 days or frozen for weeks for easy meal prep
Tips:
- Use firm tofu (not extra firm or soft) for the best texture and moisture content - this is the sweet spot
- Drain the tofu well before using to remove excess water, but don't press it for hours like you would for stir-frying
- The dough should feel similar to traditional pasta dough - smooth and slightly elastic, not sticky or crumbly
- Fresh pasta cooks much faster than dried (3-5 minutes), so watch it carefully and don't walk away
- Always reserve some pasta water before draining - it's essential for sauce consistency and is liquid gold
- This pasta can be cut into any shape: fettuccine, spaghetti, pappardelle, or even ravioli if you're feeling ambitious
- Freeze uncooked pasta on a baking sheet first, then transfer to a freezer bag so pieces don't stick together
Ingredients Overview:
The star ingredient here is firm tofu - not extra firm, not soft, but right in the middle with firm tofu. You'll use half a block (about 200g), which is the perfect amount to balance with 200g of flour. The tofu needs to have enough moisture to bind the flour together, but not so much that your dough becomes sticky and unworkable. Extra firm tofu is too dry and won't bind properly, while soft or silken tofu is too wet and makes the dough sticky. Firm is the Goldilocks zone. Make sure to drain it well before using. Once this pasta is cooked and sauced, you won't taste the tofu at all, which makes it perfect even for people who think they don't like tofu.

For the flour, you'll use 200g of all-purpose flour. This creates the structure and chewiness of the pasta. I love the 1:1 ratio of tofu to flour because it's so easy to remember and scale - if you want more pasta, just double everything. You can experiment with other types of flour too - whole wheat would add more fiber and a nuttier flavor, or you could try a blend. The key is the ratio of flour to tofu, which creates a workable dough that's not too dry or too wet.

For the simple sauce shown in the video, you'll need minced garlic (3-4 cloves), olive oil (about 3 tablespoons), salt, pepper, and some greens like baby arugula or spinach (a couple handfuls). Don't forget to reserve some of that starchy pasta water before draining - about 1 cup. It's what brings everything together into a cohesive, silky sauce that clings to the pasta. The starch in the pasta water acts as an emulsifier.
Instructions Overview:
Making the dough is straightforward but requires some good old-fashioned kneading. You break up the tofu in a bowl, add the flour, and then work it together with your hands for several minutes until it forms a smooth, cohesive dough. The kneading is important - it develops the structure and makes the pasta tender with a good chew.

Rolling out the dough requires getting it quite thin. You want to be able to almost see your hand through it when held up to the light, but not so thin that it tears easily. If you have a pasta roller, this is where it shines, but a regular rolling pin works perfectly fine too. I've made this countless times with just a rolling pin.

Fresh pasta cooks incredibly fast compared to dried pasta - usually just 3-5 minutes in boiling salted water. You'll know it's done when it floats to the top and has a tender bite. Don't overcook it or it becomes mushy.

The sauce in this recipe is super simple and lets the pasta shine. Garlic sautéed in olive oil with some wilted greens, seasoned with salt and pepper, and brought together with that reserved pasta water. The starchy pasta water is crucial - it helps the sauce cling to the pasta and creates that restaurant-quality silky texture. You just toss the drained pasta in the pan with the sauce, add a splash of pasta water, and let everything come together for a minute. Perfect.
FAQ:
**Can you really not taste the tofu in the pasta?**
Seriously, no! This was my first time trying it too, and I was skeptical, but once it's cooked and covered with sauce, you absolutely cannot taste the tofu. The tofu is just there for structure and protein - the flavor completely disappears into the background. It just tastes like regular fresh pasta. I've served this to non-vegan friends and they had no idea there was tofu in it.

**What type of tofu should I use?**
Firm tofu is ideal. It has the perfect moisture content to bind with the flour without making the dough too wet or too dry. Extra firm tofu might be too dry and crumbly, making it hard to form a cohesive dough. Soft or silken tofu would make the dough too wet and sticky, requiring way too much extra flour. Firm is the goldilocks zone.

**How long does homemade tofu pasta last?**
Fresh uncooked pasta can be stored in an airtight container in the fridge for 2-3 days, or you can freeze it for up to a month. To freeze, lay the cut pasta in a single layer on a baking sheet and freeze until solid (about 2 hours), then transfer to a freezer bag. This prevents the pieces from sticking together. Cook from frozen, adding just a minute or two to the cooking time.

- Mushroom Ravioli
- Spinach Ravioli
Author: Holistic Bravo
Recipe Card Ingredients:
**Pasta:**
- 200g all-purpose flour (about 1⅔ cups - weighing is most accurate)
- Half a block (approximately 200g) firm tofu (NOT extra firm or soft - firm is key for right texture)

**Simple Garlic Greens Sauce:**
- 3-4 cloves garlic, minced
- 3 tbsp extra virgin olive oil
- Salt and freshly ground black pepper to taste
- 2-3 large handfuls baby arugula and spinach (or any greens you have)
- 1 cup reserved pasta water (scoop out before draining - essential!)

**Optional additions:**
- Red pepper flakes for heat
- Parmesan or nutritional yeast for savory umami
- Cherry tomatoes for freshness
Recipe Card Instructions:
1. Drain firm tofu well - let excess water drip off for a few minutes. You don't need to press it for hours, just remove obvious excess moisture.
2. Add drained tofu to large bowl. Use hands to break it up and mash thoroughly, working it until well incorporated. Small bits are fine - doesn't need to be completely smooth.
3. Add 200g flour to bowl with tofu. This is where it gets messy and fun - roll up your sleeves! Use hands to incorporate flour and tofu together, mixing and squeezing to combine.
4. Knead dough in bowl (or on clean work surface) for 5-8 minutes, really working the flour into the tofu. Dough should come together into cohesive ball that feels smooth and slightly elastic, similar to regular pasta dough. If too dry and crumbly, add tiny bit of water (1 tsp at a time). If too wet and sticky, add bit more flour (1 tbsp at a time).
5. Once you have smooth dough ball, use pasta roller, pasta mat, or regular rolling pin to roll dough out thin. Aim for thin enough to see your hand through when held up to light, but not so thin it tears easily - about 1/16 inch thick.
6. Cut rolled dough into desired pasta shape using knife or pasta cutter. Fettuccine and spaghetti are classic, but any shape works. Dust cut pasta lightly with flour to prevent sticking.
7. Bring large pot of water to rolling boil. Add generous amount of salt - water should taste like the sea (about 2 tbsp salt per 6 quarts water).
8. Add fresh pasta to boiling water. Stir gently to prevent sticking. Fresh pasta cooks very fast - only 3-5 minutes total. You'll know it's done when it floats to top and has tender bite. Taste a piece to check doneness.
9. Before draining, use measuring cup or ladle to scoop out at least 1 cup pasta water. Set aside - this is crucial for sauce!
10. While pasta cooks, make sauce: Heat 3 tbsp olive oil in large pan over medium heat. Add minced garlic and sauté until fragrant and just starting to turn golden, about 1 minute. Don't let it burn or it becomes bitter!
11. Season with generous pinch of salt and several grinds black pepper. Add greens (arugula and spinach). Sauté until greens wilt down, 1-2 minutes. They'll reduce significantly.
12. Drain cooked pasta (remember you already reserved pasta water!).
13. Add drained pasta directly to pan with sauce. Add generous splash of reserved pasta water (start with 1/4 cup) - this helps sauce cling to pasta and creates silky consistency.
14. Toss everything together over medium heat for about 1 minute, letting pasta absorb flavors and sauce come together. Add more pasta water as needed if it seems dry - you want it glossy and well-coated.
15. Serve immediately while hot. Fresh pasta is best eaten right away! Garnish with more black pepper, red pepper flakes, or nutritional yeast if desired.

**Storage:**
Leftovers can be stored in airtight container in fridge 2-3 days. Reheat gently with splash of water or broth to rehydrate.
Nutrition:
Per serving (pasta only, approximately 1/3 of recipe):
- Calories: 320-380
- Protein: 15-18g
- Carbohydrates: 55-65g
- Fat: 4-6g
- Fiber: 3-4g
- Calcium: Significant amount from tofu
- Iron: Good amount from tofu

Name: High Protein Veggie Wraps
Short Description: These Mediterranean-inspired meal prep wraps are loaded with tofu, black beans, and bold flavors - my Sunday meal prep essential.
Introduction:
Welcome to your new weekly meal prep hero. These high-protein veggie wraps are what I make every Sunday when I want to set myself up for success throughout the week. They're packed with Mediterranean flavors - sundried tomatoes, kalamata olives, pepperoncini, and feta - that make eating healthy feel like an indulgence rather than a chore.

What makes these wraps so brilliant for meal prep is that they're baked after being assembled, which crisps up the tortilla and helps everything stay together. You're not dealing with a soggy wrap situation halfway through the week. Each wrap is loaded with an entire block of tofu and an entire can of black beans distributed across 4-6 wraps, so you're getting substantial protein in every single one.

I use two different spreads - red pepper hummus and pesto - alternating between wraps, or you can just choose your favorite and use that for all of them. The beauty of this recipe is that you make a big batch once, store them in the fridge wrapped individually, and then you have grab-and-go lunches or quick dinners ready throughout the week. They're delicious cold straight from the fridge or reheated for a few minutes.

The Mediterranean flavors in these wraps are what keep me coming back to this recipe week after week. The salty, briny combination of olives and pepperoncini with the sweet sundried tomatoes and creamy feta is just perfection. Every bite has so much flavor that you forget you're eating something healthy. These are what I reach for when I'm too busy or tired to cook but still want real food that tastes good and makes me feel good.
Why You'll Love This:
- Perfect for weekly meal prep with 4-6 wraps ready to grab throughout the week without any morning stress
- Each wrap provides 22-28g of protein from tofu, black beans, feta, and hummus - serious protein content
- Baking the wraps makes them crispy and helps everything hold together without getting soggy
- Mediterranean flavors are bold and satisfying so healthy eating doesn't feel boring or restrictive
- Can be eaten cold or reheated depending on your preference and schedule - both ways are delicious
Tips:
- Press the tofu really well to remove excess moisture - this prevents soggy wraps and is worth the effort
- Season the tofu and black beans generously since this is where most of the seasoning comes from in each bite
- Alternate between red pepper hummus and pesto for variety, or use your favorite on all wraps if you prefer consistency
- Wrap them tightly before baking to ensure they hold together and don't burst open
- Store wrapped individually in the fridge for easy grab-and-go meals throughout the week - I use foil
- These last up to 5 days in the fridge when stored properly in airtight containers or wrapped tightly
- Can be frozen individually for up to a month - thaw overnight in the fridge before eating
- Reheat in toaster oven at 350°F for 5-7 minutes to re-crisp if you prefer them warm
Ingredients Overview:
The protein foundation of these wraps comes from two sources: an entire block of tofu and an entire can of black beans. The tofu should be well-drained and pressed to remove excess moisture - this is crucial for texture and prevents watery wraps. You can crumble the tofu or cube it depending on your preference - I usually crumble it because it distributes more evenly. Season both the tofu and black beans generously with your favorite spices. This is where the flavor really builds, so don't be shy. I use garlic powder, onion powder, cumin, paprika, salt, and pepper on both.

For the Mediterranean toppings, you'll use sundried tomatoes (those oil-packed ones are perfect and add richness plus healthy fats), kalamata olives for their briny, savory bite, pepperoncini for a slight kick and tang that brightens everything up, and crumbled feta cheese for saltiness and creaminess. These ingredients transform a simple wrap into something that tastes like it came from a Mediterranean café. Don't skimp on these - they're what make the wraps special.

The spreads are what bind everything together and add moisture. Red pepper hummus brings a slightly sweet, smoky flavor plus additional protein and healthy fats, while pesto adds that classic basil-garlic punch and healthy fats from nuts and olive oil. You can use one or the other, or alternate like I do in the video. Both work beautifully, and alternating gives you variety throughout the week so you don't get bored eating the same thing every day.

And finally, you'll need large tortillas or wraps. Choose ones that are pliable and large enough to hold all these fillings without tearing. I prefer whole wheat tortillas for extra fiber, but any large tortilla works. Spinach or tomato wraps would be great too.
Instructions Overview:
The key to these wraps is proper prep and assembly. Pressing the tofu well is crucial - I can't stress this enough. Excess moisture will make your wraps soggy and sad. Once pressed, you season it generously. The tofu itself is pretty bland, so it needs that seasoning to bring flavor to every bite.

The assembly-line method is the most efficient way to make these. Lay out all your tortillas and spread them all at once, then layer on the fillings assembly-line style. This makes the whole process much faster and more efficient than making them one at a time.

Baking at 425°F is what creates that crispy exterior while warming everything through and melting the feta slightly. The high temperature is important - don't be tempted to go lower or they won't crisp up properly. Twenty minutes is the magic number.

Once baked and cooled, wrap each one individually in foil or plastic wrap. This makes them easy to grab in the morning when you're rushing out the door. I label mine with the date and type of spread (hummus or pesto) so I know which is which. They stay fresh for up to 5 days in the fridge, though they rarely last that long in my house because they're so good.
FAQ:
**How long do these wraps last in the fridge?**
They'll stay fresh for up to 5 days when stored properly in airtight containers or wrapped individually in foil or plastic wrap. The baking process really helps them maintain their structure throughout the week, so you don't end up with soggy wraps on day four. In fact, I think they're just as good on day five as they are on day one.

**Can these be eaten cold?**
Absolutely! They're delicious both ways. Cold right from the fridge is perfect when you're rushing out the door or don't have access to a microwave, and reheated for a minute in the microwave or 5-7 minutes in a toaster oven is great when you have a couple extra minutes and want them warm and crispy. I honestly eat them cold most of the time because I'm too impatient to wait for reheating.

**Can I freeze these wraps?**
Yes! Wrap each one individually in foil or plastic wrap, then place them all in a freezer bag with as much air removed as possible. They'll keep for up to a month in the freezer. Thaw overnight in the fridge before eating, then heat if desired. The texture holds up surprisingly well after freezing - I always keep a few in my freezer as backup for extra busy weeks.

- Mediterranean Veggie Wraps

Author: Holistic Bravo
Recipe Card Ingredients:
**Protein Base:**
- 1 entire block (14-16 oz) firm or extra firm tofu, well-drained and pressed
- 1 entire can (15 oz) black beans, drained and rinsed thoroughly
- Seasonings: 1 tsp garlic powder, 1 tsp onion powder, 1 tsp cumin, 1 tsp paprika, 1/2 tsp salt, 1/4 tsp black pepper (for both tofu and beans)

**Mediterranean Toppings:**
- 1/3 cup sundried tomatoes in oil, roughly chopped
- 1/2 cup kalamata olives, pitted and sliced (or left whole)
- 1/3 cup pepperoncini, sliced
- 1 cup crumbled feta cheese

**Spreads (choose one or use both):**
- 1/2 cup red pepper hummus
- 1/2 cup basil pesto (store-bought or homemade)

**Wraps:**
- 4-6 large tortillas or wraps (whole wheat, spinach, or regular - whatever you prefer)
Recipe Card Instructions:
1. Preheat oven to 425°F. Line large baking sheet with parchment paper.
2. Drain tofu and press well to remove as much excess moisture as possible. Use tofu press or wrap in paper towels and place something heavy on top for 15-20 minutes. This step is crucial for preventing soggy wraps!
3. While tofu presses, drain and rinse can of black beans thoroughly in colander. Add to medium bowl.
4. Season black beans with half of the spice mixture: 1/2 tsp garlic powder, 1/2 tsp onion powder, 1/2 tsp cumin, 1/2 tsp paprika, 1/4 tsp salt, pinch of black pepper. Mix well to coat.
5. Once tofu is pressed, crumble or cube it and add to separate bowl. Season with remaining spice mixture: 1/2 tsp garlic powder, 1/2 tsp onion powder, 1/2 tsp cumin, 1/2 tsp paprika, 1/4 tsp salt, pinch of black pepper. Mix well to coat every piece.
6. Assembly time! Lay out all 4-6 tortillas on clean work surface for assembly-line efficiency.
7. Spread red pepper hummus on some wraps and pesto on others (or use one spread for all if you prefer). Spread almost to edges, using about 2 tbsp per wrap.
8. Layer seasoned tofu and black beans down the center of each wrap, dividing equally among all wraps. Don't overfill - you need to be able to fold them. Each wrap should get roughly 1/6 of the tofu and 1/6 of the beans.
9. Add Mediterranean toppings: Sprinkle sundried tomatoes, kalamata olives, pepperoncini, and generous amount of crumbled feta cheese down center of each wrap over tofu and beans.
10. Time to wrap! Fold sides in first (left and right edges fold toward center), then roll from bottom up tightly, tucking as you go. Keep everything enclosed and roll tightly - tight wraps stay together better.
11. Place wrapped tortillas seam-side down on prepared baking sheet. This helps them stay closed during baking. Space them about 1 inch apart.
12. Bake at 425°F for 20 minutes until tortillas are crispy and golden brown on outside and everything is heated through.
13. Remove from oven and let cool 5-10 minutes before cutting or handling - they're hot!
14. For meal prep: Let cool completely, then wrap each one individually in foil or plastic wrap. Label with date and spread type if alternating.
15. Store in refrigerator in airtight containers or wrapped individually. Keep up to 5 days.
16. To eat: Enjoy cold straight from fridge, or reheat in microwave 30-60 seconds or toaster oven at 350°F for 5-7 minutes to re-crisp.

**Freezing Instructions:**
Wrap individually in foil, place all in freezer bag, freeze up to 1 month. Thaw overnight in fridge.
Nutrition:
Per wrap (approximate, based on 4-6 servings):
- Calories: 380-450
- Protein: 22-28g
- Carbohydrates: 42-50g
- Fat: 14-18g
- Fiber: 8-10g
- Sodium: Moderate to high (from feta, olives, and pepperoncini)

Name: Black Bean Patties with High Protein Buns
Short Description: These homemade black bean burgers with Greek yogurt buns are worlds better than anything store-bought - you'll never go back.
Introduction:
Say goodbye to store-bought veggie patties forever. Once you make these homemade black bean patties, you'll never go back - they're just that good. And when you pair them with these high-protein buns made from Greek yogurt and bread flour, you've got a complete vegetarian burger experience that rivals anything you could buy at a restaurant.

I realized while making this that there are actually more than four ingredients in the patties (despite initially thinking it was a four-ingredient recipe!), but it's still incredibly simple and straightforward. The patties come together in a food processor with black beans, red onion, egg whites, bread crumbs, and soy sauce. That's it. The result is a flavorful, protein-packed patty that holds together beautifully and has an actual meaty texture that doesn't fall apart when you flip it.

The high-protein buns are equally genius, using that magic Greek yogurt and flour combination that creates a tender, slightly chewy bun that's substantially higher in protein than regular burger buns. Together with the patties, toppings like pesto, shaved parmesan, arugula, and sliced onion, this is the kind of meal that makes you feel satisfied on every level - taste, nutrition, and the pride of having made it yourself from scratch.

What I love most about these burgers is how customizable they are. Sometimes I add roasted red peppers to the patty mixture, or swap the arugula for spinach, or use different cheeses. The base recipe is so solid that you can play around with variations and they always turn out great. These have become my go-to for meal prep because I can make a big batch of buns and patties on Sunday and have burgers ready all week.
Why You'll Love This:
- Homemade black bean patties are worlds better than anything store-bought in both flavor and texture
- Each burger provides approximately 30g of protein from multiple sources before you even add toppings
- High-protein buns use Greek yogurt instead of eggs and oil for a healthier alternative that's still delicious
- These pass the "upside down test" meaning the patties hold together perfectly without falling apart or crumbling
- Perfect for meal prep - make the patties and buns ahead and assemble throughout the week for quick dinners
Tips:
- Don't over-process the black beans - you want texture with visible chunks, not a smooth paste that tastes like refried beans
- The mixture should be thick enough to hold together when you turn the bowl upside down - this is the consistency test
- Use parchment paper when forming patties to prevent sticking to your hands and surfaces - makes cleanup easier
- Cook patties covered on low to medium heat to ensure they cook through without burning the outside
- Buns can be made ahead and frozen for up to a month for quick burger assembly anytime you want
- Bread flour has more protein than all-purpose and creates better structure, but AP flour works too if that's what you have
- Make a batch of roasted sweet potatoes at the beginning of the week as an easy side - they pair perfectly with these burgers
Ingredients Overview:
Let's start with those high-protein buns, which are surprisingly simple. You'll use 350g of bread flour - bread flour is ideal here because it has higher protein content than all-purpose flour (about 12-14g protein per cup versus 10-12g), which creates a chewier, more substantial bun that won't fall apart under burger toppings and sauces. Combined with 350g of Greek yogurt (notice the equal ratio - easy to remember!), plus baking powder and salt, you've got a dough that replaces traditional eggs and oil while pumping up the protein content significantly. Black sesame seeds on top are optional but add a beautiful finish and subtle nutty flavor that I love.

For the black bean patties, start with one can of thoroughly drained and rinsed black beans. Make sure you rinse them really well to remove that canned bean liquid - otherwise your patties can taste too "beany." These are your protein and fiber powerhouse. You'll also need a quarter of a red onion for flavor and moisture, egg whites (about 1/3 cup or about 2-3 egg whites) to bind everything together without adding the fat from yolks, bread crumbs (panko works great because it's lighter and crispier) to absorb moisture and create the right texture, and a tablespoon of low-sodium soy sauce for that savory umami depth that makes these taste almost meaty.

For assembly, pesto is the perfect spread - it's rich and herby without being heavy, and it adds healthy fats and flavor. Shaved parmesan adds salty, nutty notes that complement the black beans, fresh arugula brings a peppery bite and some greens to your meal, and sliced onion adds crunch and sharp flavor. And don't forget to prep some sweet potatoes for the side - they're the perfect accompaniment. I like to cube them, toss with olive oil and spices, and roast them while I'm making the burgers.
Instructions Overview:
The buns come together first since they need to bake. The Greek yogurt and bread flour get mixed and kneaded into a smooth dough, divided into portions, shaped into buns, and baked at two different temperatures - first 350°F to bake them through, then 425°F to get that golden-brown top. This two-temperature method ensures they're fully cooked without getting too dark.

For the patties, the food processor does most of the work. You pulse the black beans and onion until chunky - not smooth, you want texture here. Then you add the egg whites, bread crumbs, and soy sauce and pulse again until everything is combined and the mixture is thick enough to hold together. The "upside down test" is genius - if you can flip the bowl upside down and the mixture doesn't fall out, it's the perfect consistency for forming patties.

Forming the patties on parchment paper prevents them from sticking to everything and makes the process much cleaner. Then you cook them covered on low to medium heat, which is important. The covering helps them cook through without drying out, and the lower heat prevents burning while ensuring they're fully heated in the center.

Assembly is the best part - spreading that pesto on the warm bun, adding the crispy patty, piling on the toppings. These are substantial burgers that hold together when you bite into them, which is the mark of a really good veggie burger. No falling apart or crumbling like so many store-bought options.
FAQ:
**Why use bread flour instead of all-purpose flour for the buns?**
Bread flour contains more protein than all-purpose flour (about 12-14g per cup versus 10-12g), which creates a chewier, more substantial bun with better structure. These buns need to hold up to burger toppings and sauces without falling apart or getting soggy, and the extra protein in bread flour helps them do exactly that. The higher protein content creates more gluten, which gives the buns that nice chewy texture. That said, all-purpose flour will work in a pinch - the buns just won't be quite as sturdy.

**Can I make the patties ahead of time?**
Absolutely! Form the patties and place them on a parchment-lined baking sheet or plate. Cover and refrigerate for up to 2 days before cooking. You can also freeze them for up to a month - just place parchment paper between each patty so they don't stick together, then store in a freezer bag. Cook from frozen, adding a few extra minutes to the cooking time on each side. I always make a double batch and freeze half.

**What's the "upside down test"?**
This is my genius way to check if your patty mixture is the right consistency. If you can turn the food processor bowl upside down and the mixture stays put without sliding out or falling, it's thick enough to form into patties that will hold together during cooking. If it starts to slide out, it's too wet - add more bread crumbs a tablespoon at a time until it passes the test! This trick has never failed me.
