Author: Holistic Bravo
Recipe Card Ingredients:
**High Protein Buns:**
- 350g bread flour (about 2¾ cups - weighing is most accurate)
- 350g non-fat or low-fat Greek yogurt (about 1⅓ cups - use thick, high-protein yogurt)
- 1g baking powder (about ¼ tsp)
- Generous pinch of fine salt
- Black sesame seeds (optional, for topping - adds beautiful visual and subtle flavor)

**Black Bean Patties:**
- 1 can (15 oz) black beans, drained and rinsed very thoroughly
- 1/4 medium red onion, cut into large chunks
- 1/3 cup egg whites (approximately 2-3 large egg whites)
- 1⅓ cup bread crumbs (panko recommended for lighter texture)
- 1 tbsp low sodium soy sauce

**For Assembly:**
- 1/2 cup basil pesto (store-bought or homemade)
- 1 cup shaved parmesan cheese
- 2 cups fresh arugula
- 1 small red onion, thinly sliced
- Cooking spray or oil for pan

**Optional side:**
- Sweet potatoes, cubed and roasted
Recipe Card Instructions:
**For High Protein Buns:**
1. Preheat oven to 350°F. Line baking sheet with parchment paper.
2. In large bowl, whisk together 350g bread flour, 1g baking powder, and generous pinch of salt to distribute evenly.
3. Add 350g Greek yogurt to flour mixture. Use spoon to start mixing, then switch to hands when it gets too thick to stir. Bring dough together and knead in bowl (or on clean surface) for 3-5 minutes until it forms smooth, cohesive dough. Dough should be soft and slightly tacky but not sticky.
4. Divide dough into 4 equal pieces (about 175g each for uniformity). Roll each piece between palms to form smooth ball.
5. Gently flatten and shape each ball into bun shape - round with flat top and bottom, about 4 inches in diameter.
6. If using black sesame seeds, sprinkle generously on top of each bun now, pressing gently so they adhere.
7. Place buns on prepared baking sheet with 2-3 inches space between them.
8. Bake at 350°F for 20 minutes until buns are set and cooked through.
9. After 20 minutes, increase oven temperature to 425°F and bake for another 10 minutes until tops are golden brown with slight color. Don't skip this step - it creates that beautiful finish.
10. Remove from oven and let cool on baking sheet while you make patties. Buns can be made ahead and frozen.

**For Black Bean Patties:**
1. Ensure black beans are very well drained and rinsed - excess liquid makes patties too wet. Let them drain in colander for a few minutes.
2. Add drained black beans to food processor along with red onion chunks (no need to chop small - processor will break them down).
3. Pulse food processor 8-10 times, stopping to scrape down sides as needed. You want beans and onion broken down into small chunks with visible texture - NOT smooth paste. Mixture should look chunky.
4. Add egg whites (1/3 cup), bread crumbs (1⅓ cup), and soy sauce (1 tbsp) to food processor.
5. Pulse another 6-8 times until mixture is combined and thick. Scrape down sides and pulse a few more times if needed.
6. Time for the upside down test! Carefully turn food processor bowl upside down over sink. If mixture stays put and doesn't slide out, it's perfect consistency. If it starts sliding or falling, it's too wet - add 2-3 tbsp more bread crumbs and pulse until it passes test.
7. Lay out sheet of parchment paper on cutting board or counter to prevent sticking.
8. Divide mixture into 4 equal portions (use scale for uniformity if you have one - about 150-175g each).
9. Use hands to form each portion into patty shape about 4 inches in diameter and 3/4 inch thick. Make them size of your buns. Compact them well so they hold together.
10. Heat large skillet over low to medium heat. Spray generously with cooking oil to prevent sticking.
11. Carefully transfer patties to hot pan using spatula. Cover pan with lid - this is important for cooking through.
12. Cook covered for 3-4 minutes on first side without moving or pressing them - let them develop crust.
13. Carefully flip patties using spatula. Cover again and cook another 3-4 minutes on second side until both sides are crispy and golden brown and patties are heated through.
14. Remove from heat. Patties should be firm and hold together when handled.

**Assembly:**
1. Slice buns in half horizontally with serrated knife.
2. Spread generous amount of pesto (about 2 tbsp) on bottom half of each bun.
3. Place one black bean patty on top of pesto.
4. Add shaved parmesan cheese (about ¼ cup per burger) on top of patty.
5. Add handful of fresh arugula (about ½ cup per burger).
6. Add 2-3 slices of red onion.
7. Cap with top half of bun.
8. Serve immediately with side of roasted sweet potatoes or your favorite side dish. Enjoy this protein-packed homemade burger!

**Storage:**
Buns: Cool completely, store in airtight container at room temperature 2-3 days or freeze up to 1 month.
Patties: Refrigerate cooked patties up to 4 days, or freeze uncooked patties up to 1 month with parchment between them.
Nutrition:
Per burger (without toppings):
- Calories: 420-480
- Protein: 28-32g
- Carbohydrates: 70-80g
- Fat: 4-6g
- Fiber: 10-12g
- Iron: High (from black beans)
- Calcium: Good amount (from Greek yogurt)

Name: Homemade Dog Treats
Short Description: Simple 3-ingredient all-natural treats for your furry best friend that are safe for humans too.
Introduction:
Today we're making something special for the best friends in our lives—our dogs! Rex is about to be one very happy pup with these super simple, all-natural dog treats that come together with just three wholesome ingredients: oats, peanut butter, and pumpkin. The best part? They're ready in under 40 minutes from start to finish, and your dog will absolutely love them.

I created this recipe for my pup Rex, and watching him try them for the first time was pure joy. These treats have a Play-Doh-like consistency before baking, which makes them perfect for pressing into cute molds—think paw prints, bones, or any fun shape you can find. Everything is pre-measured like a cooking show (which never happens in my kitchen!), and they come together really quickly.

Here's something important to note: these treats are also completely safe for humans to eat! They're basically oatmeal, pumpkin, and peanut butter baked together, so if you want to make a human-friendly version, just add a couple tablespoons of maple syrup to the mixture. Your dog doesn't need the added sweetness, but we humans might appreciate it. I've been known to snack on the maple syrup version myself. And remember—everything in moderation, even for Rex!

What I love most about these treats is how much control you have over what goes into your dog's body. Store-bought treats often contain preservatives, fillers, and ingredients you can't even pronounce. With this recipe, you know exactly what's in every single treat, and you can feel good about giving them to your best friend.
Why You'll Love This:
- Only 3 natural ingredients with no preservatives or mystery additives like store-bought treats
- Ready in about 40 minutes total including baking time
- Rex approved which means your pup will probably love them too
- Makes approximately 20-30 treats depending on mold size for training rewards or special occasions
- Safe for humans to eat too with the addition of maple syrup for a sweet snack
- You'll feel like the best pet parent knowing exactly what your dog is eating
Tips:
- CRITICAL: Check peanut butter ingredients carefully—it should only contain peanuts, NO xylitol which is toxic to dogs
- I use silicone molds for easy removal and cute shapes that make treat time even more fun
- The mixture should be like Play-Doh—moldable and slightly sticky but not too wet
- Store at room temperature for up to a week, refrigerate for up to 2 weeks, or freeze for longer
- These are treats, not meals—everything in moderation for your furry friend
- Make a batch for fellow dog parent friends as DIY gifts they'll appreciate
- For the human version, I add 2 tablespoons of maple syrup to the mixture before baking
- If your dog has allergies, you can substitute sunflower seed butter for peanut butter
Ingredients Overview:
Let's start with rolled oats, which are the base of these treats. One cup is all you need. Oats are gentle on dog digestion, provide fiber, and are completely safe for dogs to eat. They'll get processed into smaller pieces when you blend everything together, creating that perfect moldable texture. I love using oats because they're a whole grain that provides sustained energy for active dogs.

Next up is pumpkin puree—you'll use a quarter cup. Make sure you're using 100% pure pumpkin puree, NOT pumpkin pie filling which contains added sugar and spices that aren't necessary for dogs. Plain pumpkin is great for dog digestion, adds natural moisture and sweetness, and dogs generally love the flavor. I've found that pumpkin also helps with digestive regularity, which is always a plus.

And finally, peanut butter—this is where you need to be careful. Use one-third cup of CREAMY peanut butter, and this is absolutely critical: check the ingredients label and make sure the ONLY ingredient is peanuts. No salt, no sugar, and especially NO XYLITOL. Xylitol is a common sweetener found in some peanut butters, and it's extremely toxic to dogs. Brands like Jif, Skippy, and most natural peanut butters that list only peanuts are safe. When in doubt, read that label! I cannot stress this enough—xylitol toxicity in dogs is serious and can be fatal.

The beauty of this recipe is its simplicity. Three ingredients that you probably already have in your pantry, and you can whip up a batch whenever you're running low on treats. I typically make these every two weeks, and Rex gets so excited when he sees me pulling out the silicone molds.
Instructions Overview:
The process of making these treats is wonderfully straightforward and takes minimal effort. You'll start by preheating your oven to 300°F—this lower temperature ensures the treats bake through evenly without burning on the outside. While the oven heats, you'll combine your three ingredients in a food processor or blender and pulse everything together until it forms a cohesive, Play-Doh-like mixture.

The consistency check is important here. You want the mixture to be moldable and hold its shape when pressed into molds, but not so dry that it's crumbly or so wet that it won't hold together. If you need to adjust, add water by the teaspoon if it's too dry, or a few more oats if it's too wet. I've made these enough times that I can usually tell by feel when the consistency is just right.

Once your mixture is ready, you'll press it firmly into silicone molds. I love using paw print and bone-shaped molds because they're adorable, but any shape works. The key is to press the mixture in firmly so there are no air pockets—this ensures the treats hold together during baking. After baking for 25-30 minutes, you'll let them cool completely before giving them to your pup. This cooling step is crucial because hot treats can burn your dog's mouth.

The whole process from start to finish takes about 40 minutes, and most of that is hands-off baking time. I often make these while I'm doing other kitchen tasks or meal prep, and it feels efficient to have something productive baking in the oven. Rex has learned to recognize the smell of these treats baking, and he camps out in the kitchen waiting for them to be ready.
FAQ:
**Are these actually safe for my dog?**
Yes, absolutely! As long as you use peanut butter with ONLY peanuts as the ingredient (no xylitol or other additives), these three ingredients are all completely safe and even beneficial for dogs. Oats provide fiber, pumpkin is great for digestion, and peanut butter provides healthy fats and protein. I've been making these for Rex for years with zero issues. Just always check that peanut butter label!

**How long do these treats last?**
They'll keep at room temperature in an airtight container for about a week, in the refrigerator for up to 2 weeks, or you can freeze them for a month or longer. Frozen treats can actually be a nice cool snack on hot days—just make sure they're not too hard for your dog's teeth. I usually make a batch and keep half in the fridge and half in the freezer.

**Can humans really eat these too?**
Absolutely! They're essentially baked oatmeal with pumpkin and peanut butter. They're pretty bland for human taste buds without added sweetness, but if you add 2 tablespoons of maple syrup to the mixture before baking, they make a perfectly nice human snack. I've made the maple syrup version for myself when I want something sweet but wholesome, and they're actually quite tasty with a cup of coffee.
Related Recipes:
- Cozy Chunky Oatmeal
- High Protein Cookie Dough
- Overnight Chia Seed Tiramisu
Author: Holistic Bravo
Calories: 15-25 per treat (dogs), 35-45 per treat (humans with maple syrup)
Recipe Card Ingredients:
**For Dogs:**
- 1 cup rolled oats (old-fashioned oats work best)
- 1/4 cup pumpkin puree (100% pure pumpkin, not pie filling)
- 1/3 cup creamy peanut butter (ONLY peanuts in ingredients—absolutely no xylitol!)

**For Humans (optional):**
- Same as above, plus 2 tablespoons maple syrup (or honey)

**Equipment:**
- Food processor or high-powered blender
- Silicone treat molds (paw prints, bones, or any shape)
- Baking sheet
Recipe Card Instructions:
1. Preheat your oven to 300°F (this lower temperature prevents burning).
2. Add 1 cup rolled oats, 1/4 cup pumpkin puree, and 1/3 cup creamy peanut butter to your food processor.
3. Double-check that your peanut butter contains ONLY peanuts—no salt, xylitol, or additives (this is crucial for dog safety).
4. Pulse the mixture 10-15 times until well combined into a Play-Doh-like consistency that's moldable but not wet.
5. Check the consistency—if too dry, add water 1 teaspoon at a time; if too wet, add more oats.
6. Press the mixture firmly into silicone molds, packing it down well to eliminate air pockets.
7. Place filled molds on a baking sheet for easier handling and transport to the oven.
8. Bake for 25-30 minutes until treats are firm to the touch and lightly golden.
9. Remove from oven and let cool completely (at least 20 minutes) before giving to your dog—hot treats can burn!
10. Pop the cooled treats out of the silicone molds (they should release easily).
11. Store in an airtight container at room temperature (1 week), refrigerate (2 weeks), or freeze (1+ months).
12. Give to your pup as training rewards or special treats—Rex recommends breaking larger treats in half for portion control!
Nutrition:
Per treat (small bone/paw print size):
- Calories: 15-25 (dog version), 35-45 (human version)
- Protein: 1-2g
- Carbohydrates: 3-4g
- Fat: 1-2g
- Fiber: 0.5-1g

Name: McGriddle-Style Breakfast Sandwich
Short Description: Pancake 'buns' with eggs and cheese for a sweet and savory breakfast sandwich that's ready faster than a drive-through run.
Introduction:
Good morning! Have you ever woken up with an incredible urge for a McDonald's McGriddle but didn't want to actually go to McDonald's? That's exactly what happened to me one morning, and this homemade version was born. Funny thing is, I'd never actually had a McDonald's McGriddle before making this, but the craving was real!

This homemade version is so good that it was nearly gone before I remembered to take a picture. I had to rotate the plate because the other side already had a large bite taken out of it—that's how delicious these are! The magic is in the combination of sweet pancake 'buns' and savory eggs with cheese. That sweet-and-savory breakfast combo is unbeatable and keeps you satisfied all morning.

The whole thing comes together in less than 15 minutes from start to finish, which is faster than actually going to McDonald's when you factor in getting dressed, driving, waiting in line, and coming back home. Plus, you can customize everything exactly how you like it. I want to experiment with chocolate chip pancakes as the 'buns' for an extra sweet version, which sounds absolutely amazing. You could also add bacon, sausage, or keep it vegetarian like I usually do.

What I love about this recipe is how it transforms a simple breakfast into something that feels indulgent and special, even on busy weekday mornings. The thick pancake batter is key—it creates sturdy 'buns' that actually hold together like bread, not floppy pancakes that fall apart. Trust me, the technique makes all the difference.
Why You'll Love This:
- Ready in under 15 minutes from start to finish—faster than a fast food run
- Sweet and savory combination satisfies both breakfast cravings at once
- I use thick pancake batter so the 'buns' hold together and don't fall apart
- Provides 18-22g of protein per sandwich from eggs and cheese to keep you full
- Completely customizable with different proteins, cheese, or pancake flavors
- You'll feel accomplished making a copycat version that rivals the original
Tips:
- Keep the pancake batter thicker than regular pancake batter so it holds up as a bun—this is the most important tip
- I use silicone molds or egg rings to get perfect circular shapes that look like the real thing
- The steam technique (adding water to the pan and covering) helps eggs set faster without overcooking
- Cook on medium to low heat to prevent burning the pancakes before they cook through
- You can meal prep the pancake 'buns' ahead and store in the fridge to assemble quickly on busy mornings
- Future variation I want to try: chocolate chip pancakes as buns for an extra sweet version
- Add bacon, sausage, or veggie sausage for more protein and flavor options
- Use parchment paper circles in your molds for even easier release
Ingredients Overview:
The pancake 'buns' are where the magic happens. You'll start with one cup of all-purpose flour, one teaspoon of baking powder for a bit of lift, and a pinch of salt. Then you add one whole egg (not just egg whites—the whole egg helps with structure and richness), one tablespoon of olive oil for moisture, and one tablespoon of monk fruit sweetener. I love monk fruit because it's a zero-calorie natural sweetener that makes these taste sweet without adding sugar, but you can absolutely use regular sugar if you prefer. The sweetness level is subtle, not overpowering, which is perfect for balancing the savory eggs and cheese.

For the liquid, you'll add water or milk of choice one tablespoon at a time. The key here is to make the batter THICKER than regular pancake batter. You want it thick enough to hold together as a bun structure, not thin and runny like typical pancakes. This is crucial for them to work as sandwich buns. I usually use regular water to keep it simple, but milk (dairy or plant-based) makes them slightly richer and more tender if you want to elevate it.

For the filling, you'll need eggs—one or two per sandwich depending on how protein-packed you want it—and cheese slices. American cheese is classic for that authentic fast-food vibe and melts beautifully, but I often use cheddar or pepper jack when I want more flavor. Use whatever cheese makes you happy.

And you'll need round silicone molds or egg rings to create those perfect circular shapes. This is what makes them look like actual McGriddles instead of just pancakes with eggs on top. I picked up a set of silicone molds on Amazon for about $10, and I use them constantly for everything from these breakfast sandwiches to English muffins.
Instructions Overview:
The process is beautifully efficient because everything cooks in one pan at the same time. You'll start by making your thick pancake batter, then heating a large pan over medium-low heat. Place your silicone molds or egg rings in the pan—you'll need at least four molds total (two for the pancake buns and one or two for the eggs, depending on how many eggs you're using per sandwich).

Pour the pancake batter into the molds first, filling them about two-thirds full. Then crack your eggs into separate molds. Cover the pan with a lid—this is where the magic happens. The covered environment creates steam that helps everything cook evenly. The pancakes get fluffy and cooked through without burning, and the eggs set perfectly without needing to flip them.

Here's a pro tip I discovered: add a splash of water to the pan (not in the molds, but around them) and re-cover. This creates even more steam that helps the eggs set quicker and makes flipping everything easier. The whole cooking process takes about 6-8 minutes total—3-4 minutes per side. Once everything is golden and cooked through, you simply assemble: bottom pancake bun, cheese, egg, another slice of cheese (because why not), and top pancake bun.

The result is a perfectly portioned breakfast sandwich that looks impressive and tastes even better. The sweet pancakes complement the savory eggs and melty cheese in a way that's surprisingly sophisticated for such a simple combination. I make these at least once a week, and they never get old.
FAQ:
**How do I get the pancakes to actually hold together like buns?**
The secret is making the batter thicker than normal pancake batter. I add liquid gradually and stop when I reach a thick, spoonable consistency. The batter should hold its shape when poured, not spread out thin. Using molds also helps create uniform, sturdy shapes that work perfectly as buns. If your batter seems too thin, just add a bit more flour until it thickens up.

**What if I don't have silicone molds?**
No problem! Metal egg rings work great and are super affordable—I got mine for like $8 on Amazon. You can even use clean, empty tuna cans with both ends removed as makeshift molds in a pinch. Mason jar lids (just the metal ring part) also work. Just make sure whatever you use can handle the heat of the stovetop and that you can safely remove it after cooking.

**Can I make these ahead of time?**
The pancake 'buns' can definitely be made ahead and stored in the fridge in an airtight container. They'll keep for 3-4 days. When you're ready to eat, just reheat the buns for 20-30 seconds in the microwave, cook fresh eggs, and assemble. This makes busy weekday mornings so much easier! I often make a double batch of pancake buns on Sunday and use them throughout the week for quick breakfasts.
Related Recipes:
- High Protein Bagels
- High-Protein Vegetarian Breakfast
- High-Protein Greek Yogurt Crescent Rolls
Author: Holistic Bravo
Recipe Card Ingredients:
**Pancake Buns:**
- 1 cup all-purpose flour (spooned and leveled for accuracy)
- 1 teaspoon baking powder (make sure it's fresh for best rise)
- 1 whole egg (room temperature works best)
- 1 tablespoon monk fruit sweetener (or regular granulated sugar)
- 1 tablespoon olive oil (or melted butter for richer flavor)
- Pinch of salt (about 1/8 teaspoon)
- Water or milk of choice (add 1 tablespoon at a time until thick batter forms, usually 3-4 tablespoons total)

**Filling:**
- 2-4 eggs (1-2 per sandwich, depending on protein goals)
- 2-4 slices American cheese (or cheddar, pepper jack, or your favorite melting cheese)

**Equipment:**
- 4+ silicone molds or egg rings (round, about 3-4 inches diameter)
- Large pan with lid
- Thin spatula for flipping
Recipe Card Instructions:
1. In a medium bowl, whisk together 1 cup flour, 1 teaspoon baking powder, 1 tablespoon sweetener, and a pinch of salt.
2. Add 1 whole egg and 1 tablespoon olive oil to the dry ingredients. Mix until combined.
3. Add liquid (water or milk) gradually, 1 tablespoon at a time, mixing after each addition until you reach a THICK batter consistency (thicker than regular pancakes).
4. Heat a large pan over medium-low heat (not too hot or the pancakes will burn before cooking through).
5. Place 4 silicone molds or egg rings in the heated pan (2 for pancake buns, 2 for eggs).
6. Pour pancake batter into 2 molds, filling about 2/3 full to allow for slight expansion.
7. Crack 1-2 eggs into the remaining molds (one egg per mold).
8. Cover the pan with a lid to create steam that helps everything cook evenly.
9. Optional but recommended: Add a small splash of water to the pan (around the molds, not in them) and re-cover—this creates extra steam for faster cooking.
10. Cook for 3-4 minutes, then carefully flip both pancake buns and egg molds using a thin spatula.
11. Cook for another 3-4 minutes on the second side until golden and cooked through.
12. Remove from heat and carefully pop everything out of the molds.
13. Assemble your sandwich: bottom pancake bun, slice of cheese, cooked egg(s), another slice of cheese, top pancake bun.
14. Enjoy immediately while the cheese is melty and everything is warm!
Nutrition:
Per sandwich:
- Calories: 350-400
- Protein: 18-22g
- Carbohydrates: 40-45g
- Fat: 12-16g
- Fiber: 1-2g
- Sugar: 2-4g (monk fruit), 8-10g (sugar)

Name: Mushroom Ravioli with Ricotta Filling
Short Description: A therapeutic and grounding homemade pasta dish featuring handmade ravioli stuffed with savory ricotta and mushroom filling.
Introduction:
I make pasta at least once a week because it grounds me. There's something incredibly meditative about kneading dough—I can drown out the world and just focus on the rhythm of my hands working the pasta. It's therapeutic in a way that few other cooking activities are.

This week's creation is mushroom ravioli, which is my favorite type of pasta to make. The process of forming each individual ravioli is hands-on and mindful, requiring just enough attention that your brain can't wander to stress or worries. You're fully present with the dough, the filling, the sealing of each pocket. It's pasta meditation that happens to result in an incredibly delicious dinner.

The filling is a simple but flavorful combination of ricotta cheese mixed with sautéed mushrooms and fresh parsley. For the sauce, simplicity reigns again—just olive oil, more mushrooms cooked down with spices, and pasta water to bring everything together with fresh grated parmesan at the end. This is the kind of recipe you make when you need to take care of yourself, when you need something that nourishes both body and soul through the act of creating it.

I find that the act of making pasta from scratch connects me to a slower, more intentional way of living. There's no rushing through this recipe. You have to commit to the process, and in doing so, you get to step away from the chaos of daily life for an hour. It's a Sunday ritual that helps me reset for the week ahead, and I hope it does the same for you.
Why You'll Love This:
- The hands-on process of making pasta dough and forming ravioli is genuinely therapeutic and grounding for me
- Fresh homemade ravioli has a tender texture that dried pasta simply cannot match
- Ricotta and mushroom filling is rich and flavorful without being heavy or complicated
- Simple mushroom sauce with pasta water creates restaurant-quality results at home
- You'll feel accomplished and connected to the food you're eating
- Perfect for Sunday dinner rituals that help you reset for the week ahead
Tips:
- The dough kneading process is meditative—I never rush it, and you shouldn't either. Enjoy being present in the moment.
- Make sure mushrooms for the filling are fully cooked down with liquid evaporated to avoid watery filling that makes the ravioli soggy
- I use a sandwich bag with the corner cut off as an easy makeshift piping bag for filling—works perfectly
- Keep ravioli edges well-sealed by pressing with a fork to prevent them opening during cooking
- Fresh parmesan makes a huge difference—I always grate it right before serving for best flavor
- You can substitute spinach or sun-dried tomatoes for mushrooms in the filling for variation
- Make extra ravioli and freeze on a baking sheet before storing in bags for quick meals later—I do this often
- Don't skip the pasta water for the sauce; it's what makes everything come together silkily
Ingredients Overview:
For the pasta dough, you only need two ingredients: 200g of all-purpose flour and 2 whole eggs. That's it. This classic pasta dough ratio creates tender, elastic noodles that are perfect for ravioli. The act of kneading this simple dough for 8-10 minutes is where the therapeutic magic happens. I love how something so simple—just flour and eggs—transforms into something so special.

The filling starts with half a cup of ricotta cheese, which provides a creamy, mild base that doesn't overpower the delicate pasta. To that, you'll add sautéed Bella mushrooms (also called cremini)—about 2 mushrooms chopped small. I cook these down with a whole clove of minced garlic, onion powder, garlic powder, and sea salt until they're fully cooked and all the moisture has evaporated. This step is crucial because any excess moisture will make your ravioli filling watery and cause issues during cooking. Fresh parsley adds brightness and color—just a few sprigs chopped up will do. The combination of earthy mushrooms, creamy ricotta, and fresh parsley is simple but incredibly satisfying.

For the sauce, I keep things minimal to let the ravioli shine. Olive oil as the base, more sliced Bella mushrooms (about 4), and seasonings: garlic powder, onion powder, oregano, sea salt, and black pepper. The magic ingredient is pasta water—that starchy, salty water the ravioli cooks in. I reserve 3/4 cup to create a light but flavorful sauce that coats every ravioli perfectly. And finally, fresh grated parmesan cheese to finish—half a cup of the good stuff. Don't skip this step; the parmesan is what ties everything together.

What I love about this ingredient list is how it proves that you don't need dozens of ingredients to create something memorable. Quality ingredients prepared simply always win.
Instructions Overview:
Let's start with the therapeutic part: making pasta dough. I combine the flour and eggs in a large bowl or directly on my clean counter, then use my hands to bring the mixture together. The kneading process that follows is where I find my zen. I fold the dough over itself, push it away with the heels of my hands, rotate, and repeat for 8-10 minutes until the dough is smooth and elastic. This is my meditation—just me, the dough, and the rhythm.

After wrapping the dough and letting it rest for 30 minutes, I prepare the filling. The mushrooms get sautéed with garlic until fully cooked down, then mixed with ricotta and fresh parsley. I transfer this to a sandwich bag with the corner cut off, which creates the perfect makeshift piping bag. Rolling out the pasta dough is another mindful step—I want it thin enough to see my hand through it but not so thin it tears. A pasta roller makes this easier, but a rolling pin works too.

Forming the ravioli is where the real magic happens. I pipe small mounds of filling onto the pasta sheet, fold the dough over, press to seal, and cut into individual ravioli. Each one is like a little gift I'm making for myself or whoever I'm feeding. The care and attention required in this step is what makes pasta-making so special to me.

Finally, cooking and saucing is quick and straightforward. The ravioli boil for just 3-4 minutes, while I sauté mushrooms with seasonings and pasta water to create a simple, elegant sauce. Everything comes together in those final moments, and the result is restaurant-quality pasta that I made with my own hands. That's powerful.
FAQ:
**Can I make the pasta dough ahead of time?**
Absolutely! I do this often. Wrap the kneaded dough tightly in plastic wrap and refrigerate for up to 24 hours. This actually makes it easier to work with because it's well-rested. Just let it sit at room temperature for about 20-30 minutes before rolling so it's not too stiff. Sometimes I make the dough on Saturday and form the ravioli on Sunday.

**How do I know when the ravioli are fully cooked?**
Fresh ravioli will float to the surface of the boiling water when they're done, which typically takes 3-4 minutes. They should be tender but still have a slight bite—al dente. I always taste one to check. Don't overcook them or they'll become mushy and might even burst open.

**Can I freeze uncooked ravioli?**
Yes, and this is a great meal prep strategy that I use all the time! Lay the formed ravioli in a single layer on a baking sheet lined with parchment paper, making sure they don't touch. Freeze until solid (about 2 hours), then transfer to a freezer bag. They'll keep for up to a month. Cook from frozen, just adding 1-2 minutes to the cooking time. I love having homemade ravioli in my freezer for nights when I want something special but don't have time to make it from scratch.

- Tri-Color Ravioli
- Spinach Ravioli
- High Protein Tofu Pasta
Author: Holistic Bravo
Recipe Card Ingredients:
**Pasta Dough:**
- 200g all-purpose flour (about 1.5 cups, but weighing is more accurate)
- 2 whole large eggs (room temperature for easier mixing)

**Filling:**
- 1/2 cup whole milk ricotta cheese (full-fat tastes best)
- 2 Bella mushrooms (cremini), finely chopped (about 1/2 cup chopped)
- Few sprigs fresh parsley, finely chopped (about 2 tablespoons)
- 1 whole clove garlic, minced (or 1/2 teaspoon pre-minced garlic)
- 1/4 teaspoon onion powder
- 1/4 teaspoon garlic powder
- 1/4 teaspoon sea salt (or to taste)

**Sauce:**
- 1 tablespoon extra virgin olive oil
- 4 Bella mushrooms (cremini), thinly sliced (about 1.5 cups sliced)
- 1/2 teaspoon garlic powder
- 1/2 teaspoon onion powder
- 1/2 teaspoon dried oregano
- Sea salt and black pepper to taste
- 3/4 cup reserved pasta water (don't skip this!)
- 1/2 cup fresh parmesan cheese, finely grated

**Equipment:**
- Pasta roller or rolling pin
- Gallon-size plastic bag for piping filling
- Fork for sealing ravioli edges
Recipe Card Instructions:
1. Combine 200g flour with 2 eggs in a bowl or on a clean counter. Use your hands to bring together.
2. Knead the dough for 8-10 minutes, folding and pushing rhythmically until smooth and elastic (this is the meditative part—don't rush it).
3. Wrap the dough tightly in plastic wrap and rest for 30 minutes at room temperature.
4. While dough rests, heat a pan over medium heat. Add finely chopped mushrooms and minced garlic.
5. Sauté mushrooms with onion powder, garlic powder, and sea salt for 5-7 minutes until fully softened and all liquid has evaporated (this is crucial).
6. Transfer cooked mushrooms to a bowl with ricotta and chopped fresh parsley. Mix well to combine.
7. Transfer filling to a plastic bag and cut off one corner to create a piping bag.
8. Roll out rested pasta dough until thin (almost translucent but not tearing). Use a pasta roller or rolling pin with light flour dusting.
9. Pipe small mounds (about 1 teaspoon each) of filling onto the pasta sheet, spacing them 2 inches apart.
10. Fold pasta over filling or place another sheet on top. Press around each mound firmly to seal, pushing out air pockets.
11. Use a fork to press and seal edges very well all around each ravioli.
12. Cut into individual ravioli squares using a knife or pasta cutter (a fluted edge looks pretty).
13. Bring a large pot of salted water to a rolling boil (water should taste like the sea).
14. Gently add ravioli to boiling water. Cook for 3-4 minutes until they float to the surface.
15. Before draining, reserve 3/4 cup of the starchy pasta water in a measuring cup.
16. While ravioli cook, heat 1 tablespoon olive oil in a large pan over medium heat.
17. Add sliced mushrooms and sauté for 3-4 minutes until softened and browning.
18. Season mushrooms with garlic powder, onion powder, oregano, salt, and pepper.
19. Pour reserved pasta water into the pan with mushrooms to create a light sauce.
20. Use a slotted spoon to transfer cooked ravioli directly from pot to pan. Toss gently to coat in sauce.
21. Plate the ravioli with mushroom sauce and top generously with fresh grated parmesan.
22. Serve immediately and enjoy your handmade creation!
Nutrition:
Per serving:
- Calories: 450-550
- Protein: 22-26g
- Carbohydrates: 55-65g
- Fat: 14-18g
- Fiber: 3-4g
- Calcium: High

Name: Tri-Color Ravioli with Spinach-Cashew Pesto Filling
Short Description: An avant-garde Italian dinner featuring three colors of homemade pasta dough stuffed with fresh spinach-cashew filling.
Introduction:
'We have pasta at home.' Join me for an avant-garde Italian dinner that's as beautiful as it is delicious. This was my first time trying three different pasta dough colors in one recipe, and while I'll admit the aesthetics can definitely be improved with practice, the taste was absolutely amazing.

The concept here is to create three separate pasta doughs using natural colorings: matcha powder for a vibrant green, turmeric powder for a golden orange, and classic natural dough with no additions. You can get creative with how you combine the three colors when assembling your ravioli—I used different colors for the top and bottom layers, created patterns, and mixed and matched however I felt inspired. It's pasta as art, which appeals to both my creative side and my love of cooking.

The filling is beautifully simple: blanched spinach mixed with cashews, parmesan cheese, olive oil, salt, and pepper. The result tastes very similar to pesto, but with cashews instead of pine nuts and the addition of spinach for extra nutrients and color. This is one of those recipes where the process is as enjoyable as the final result—experimenting with natural pasta colors and creating something uniquely beautiful made me feel like a real pasta artist.

I love how this recipe pushes me to try new techniques and expand my pasta-making repertoire. Yes, it takes more time than regular ravioli because you're making three separate doughs, but the visual impact is absolutely worth it. This is the kind of dish you make when you want to impress dinner guests or just treat yourself to something special.
Why You'll Love This:
- Three naturally colored pasta doughs create a stunning visual presentation that always impresses guests
- Matcha and turmeric add subtle flavor and health benefits without overpowering the dish
- Spinach-cashew filling tastes like pesto but with added nutrition from the greens
- You'll feel like a pasta artist creating something truly unique
- Perfect weekend project for expanding your pasta-making skills and getting creative
- I can customize color combinations and patterns for truly unique artistic ravioli every time I make it
Tips:
- This was my first attempt—practice makes perfect for aesthetics, so don't expect perfection on your first try
- Thoroughly squeeze blanched spinach to prevent watery filling that ruins the texture (I learned this the hard way)
- I use a pasta roller for more even, consistently thin sheets across all three colors
- Natural food coloring powders add subtle flavor without significantly altering taste
- You can substitute pine nuts for cashews for a more traditional pesto flavor profile
- Keep colored doughs separate until assembly to maintain distinct colors without bleeding
- Experiment with other natural colorings like beet powder (pink) or activated charcoal (black) for even more color options
- The colored doughs may require slightly different amounts of flour due to the added powders—adjust as needed for proper consistency
Ingredients Overview:
Let's talk about the three pasta doughs. Each batch follows the same base formula: 200g of all-purpose flour mixed with 2 eggs. For the natural dough, that's all you need—classic pasta that I've been making for years. For the green dough, I add 1-2 teaspoons of matcha powder to the flour before adding the eggs. Matcha gives you that vibrant emerald color and adds a subtle earthy note plus antioxidants. I love the slight green tea flavor it brings. For the orange dough, I add 1-2 teaspoons of turmeric powder, which creates a beautiful golden-orange hue and brings warm, slightly peppery notes along with anti-inflammatory benefits.

The amount of matcha or turmeric you use will affect both the intensity of the color and the subtlety of flavor. I recommend starting with 1 teaspoon and adding more if you want a more vibrant color. Keep in mind that the color will be more intense in the raw dough than after cooking.

The filling is wonderfully simple but flavorful. I start with fresh spinach—about 2 cups—which gets blanched briefly to wilt it down and make it easier to work with. You'll need to really squeeze out the excess water from the blanched spinach; this is crucial for preventing watery filling. I cannot stress this enough—squeeze it like you're trying to wring out a wet towel. Raw cashews (about half a cup) create a creamy, rich base similar to traditional pesto but without pine nuts, which I appreciate both for cost and flavor. Parmesan cheese (half a cup) adds that nutty, salty depth that makes pesto so addictive, olive oil (2-3 tablespoons) brings everything together with richness, and salt and pepper season to taste.

This filling is so good that I sometimes make extra and use it as a dip or spread for other meals throughout the week. It's incredibly versatile.
Instructions Overview:
This recipe is definitely more involved than my standard ravioli because you're making three separate pasta doughs instead of one. But I promise the process is the same for each—you're just doing it three times. For each dough, you'll knead until smooth and elastic (about 8-10 minutes each), then wrap and let them rest while you prepare the filling. I usually tackle the doughs one at a time to avoid confusion.

Making the filling is straightforward: blanch the spinach briefly, squeeze it very well to remove all excess water, then blend it with cashews, parmesan, olive oil, salt, and pepper until you reach a pesto-like consistency. The key is getting it relatively smooth while still maintaining some texture. You're not looking for baby food smoothness, just a nice, spreadable consistency.

The fun part comes when you roll out your colored doughs and start getting creative with assembly. I roll each color out into thin sheets separately, then experiment with color combinations. You can use one color for the bottom layer of ravioli and a different color for the top. You can alternate colors randomly. You can create patterns or sections. This is your art project, and there's no wrong way to do it. I love how each ravioli can be different, making the final plated dish look like a gallery of edible art.

Cooking and finishing is the same as regular ravioli—boil in salted water for 3-4 minutes until they float, then toss with a simple sauce. I prefer keeping the sauce minimal with these because the visual impact is so strong. Usually just olive oil and parmesan, or a light butter sauce, lets the colors and flavors shine.
FAQ:
**Will the matcha and turmeric significantly affect the taste?**
Not really! The amounts I use for coloring are quite small, so they add subtle flavor notes without overpowering the dish. The matcha adds a slight earthy undertone that I actually love, and the turmeric brings warmth, but once the ravioli are cooked and sauced, the filling and sauce flavors are much more prominent than the pasta dough colors. You might notice the flavors more if you taste the raw or plain cooked pasta, but in the complete dish, they're very subtle.

**Can I use food coloring instead of natural powders like matcha and turmeric?**
You could, but I don't recommend it. Using natural powders is a more whole-food approach and adds nutritional benefits that artificial food coloring doesn't provide. Matcha provides antioxidants, and turmeric has anti-inflammatory properties. Plus, they add subtle flavor complexity that I appreciate. But yes, technically food coloring would work for just the visual effect if that's all you're after.

**How do I prevent the colors from bleeding together?**
I work with one color of dough at a time and keep them separated until I'm ready to assemble the ravioli. When cutting, I use a sharp cutter to get clean edges. The colors are in the dough itself, so they won't bleed during cooking as long as you maintain distinct layers. Just don't let the raw doughs touch each other for extended periods before forming the ravioli.

Author: Holistic Bravo
Recipe Card Ingredients:
**Pasta Dough (3 separate batches):**
- Natural dough: 200g all-purpose flour + 2 large eggs
- Green dough: 200g all-purpose flour + 2 large eggs + 1-2 teaspoons matcha powder (start with 1 tsp for lighter color)
- Orange dough: 200g all-purpose flour + 2 large eggs + 1-2 teaspoons turmeric powder (start with 1 tsp for lighter color)

**Filling:**
- 2 cups fresh spinach (about 60g, will wilt down significantly when blanched)
- 1/2 cup raw cashews (about 70g)
- 1/2 cup freshly grated parmesan cheese (about 50g)
- 2-3 tablespoons extra virgin olive oil
- 1/2 teaspoon sea salt (or to taste)
- 1/4 teaspoon black pepper (or to taste)

**For Serving:**
- Extra virgin olive oil and freshly grated parmesan
- OR light butter sauce
- OR more of the spinach-cashew pesto thinned with pasta water
Recipe Card Instructions:
1. Make three separate pasta doughs using the same technique for each: combine flour (with matcha or turmeric if making colored dough) and eggs.
2. Knead each dough separately for 8-10 minutes until smooth and elastic. Wrap each in plastic wrap and label which is which.
3. Let all three doughs rest for at least 30 minutes at room temperature while you make the filling.
4. Bring a pot of water to a boil. Blanch fresh spinach for just 30-60 seconds until wilted and bright green.
5. Drain spinach in a colander and let cool enough to handle (run under cold water to speed this up).
6. Squeeze the spinach VERY thoroughly to remove all excess water—really wring it out like a wet towel. This is crucial.
7. Add squeezed spinach to a food processor along with cashews, parmesan, olive oil, salt, and pepper.
8. Blend for 30-60 seconds until mixture reaches a pesto-like consistency—relatively smooth with some texture remaining.
9. Taste and adjust seasoning as needed (I usually add a bit more salt and parmesan).
10. Roll out each colored dough separately using a pasta roller or rolling pin until thin (you should almost see your hand through it).
11. Get creative with color combinations! Use one color for the bottom layer and a different color for the top, or mix and match as you like.
12. Place small spoonfuls (about 1 teaspoon) of spinach-cashew filling on your pasta sheets, spacing them about 2 inches apart.
13. Brush edges and areas around the filling with a bit of water to help seal.
14. Place your second sheet of pasta (same or different color) on top of the filling.
15. Press firmly around each mound of filling to seal, making sure to push out all air pockets (these can cause bursting during cooking).
16. Use a knife, pasta cutter, or ravioli stamp to cut into individual squares or circles.
17. Bring a large pot of salted water to a rolling boil (taste it—it should be salty like the sea).
18. Gently add your tri-color ravioli to the boiling water. Cook for 3-4 minutes until they float to the surface.
19. While they cook, prepare simple sauce: heat olive oil in a pan, or melt butter, or thin some of the pesto filling with pasta water.
20. Use a slotted spoon to transfer cooked ravioli to your sauce and toss gently to coat.
21. Plate your colorful creation and top with extra parmesan and black pepper.
22. Admire your pasta art before eating!
Nutrition:
Per serving:
- Calories: 480-580
- Protein: 20-24g
- Carbohydrates: 60-70g
- Fat: 16-20g
- Fiber: 4-5g
- Iron: Moderate

Name: Hot Chocolate Protein Oatmeal with Spicy Eggs
Short Description: A high-protein vegetarian breakfast featuring chocolate protein oatmeal paired with spicy eggs for 53g of total protein.
Introduction:
Fifty-three grams of protein in one breakfast. Who says you can't hit your protein goals as a vegetarian? This powerhouse breakfast is proof that plant-based eating can be incredibly protein-rich when you know how to layer your protein sources strategically.

The star of the show is the hot chocolate protein oatmeal, which uses a clever technique: blending oats into flour before cooking them into a creamy, almost pudding-like porridge. Then I stir in plant-based chocolate protein powder—I specifically love Momentous brand for this—which transforms the oatmeal into something that tastes like dessert for breakfast. Top it with frozen raspberries, sliced almonds, hemp seeds, and a drizzle of honey, and you've got texture, antioxidants, healthy fats, and even more protein.

Pair that decadent chocolate oatmeal with some spicy eggs cooked just how you like them, and you've got a complete breakfast that keeps you satisfied for hours. This is the kind of breakfast I make when I have an active day ahead and need sustained energy, or after a workout when my body is craving protein for recovery. It's become one of my go-to breakfasts when I'm training or just want to start the day feeling fueled and satisfied.

What I love about this breakfast is how it combines the best of both worlds: the sweet, indulgent chocolate oatmeal satisfies my morning sweet tooth, while the spicy eggs provide savory balance. Together, they create a breakfast that's both delicious and incredibly functional for my fitness goals.
Why You'll Love This:
- Delivers 53g of protein from multiple vegetarian sources so you can easily hit your protein goals
- Blending oats into flour creates a smoother, creamier texture that's almost like pudding—I love this technique
- Tastes like chocolate dessert but it's actually a nutritious, balanced breakfast
- Frozen raspberries, almonds, and hemp seeds add antioxidants, healthy fats, and omega-3s
- Ready in just 15 minutes making it perfect for busy mornings when you need something substantial
- You'll feel energized and satisfied for hours without any mid-morning crash
Tips:
- I blend oats into flour for ultra-smooth texture, but you can leave whole for chunkier traditional oatmeal if you prefer
- Can substitute any flavor of plant-based protein powder—vanilla, strawberry, or unflavored all work (though I love chocolate best)
- Top with any berries you have on hand—blueberries, strawberries, or mixed berries are all great
- Add chia seeds or flax seeds instead of or in addition to hemp seeds for extra omega-3s and fiber
- Can make this sweeter with maple syrup instead of honey for a vegan version
- I sometimes substitute dairy milk for water to make it even creamier and more indulgent
- Can prepare as overnight oats by soaking oat flour with protein powder overnight in the fridge—just add liquid and stir
- The spice level on the eggs is totally customizable—I go pretty heavy on the hot sauce, but use as much or as little as you like
Ingredients Overview:
The base of this recipe is oat flour, which I create by blending whole rolled oats in my blender until they reach a flour consistency. You can also buy pre-made oat flour, but I prefer making it fresh because it's cheaper and I can control the texture. Using oat flour instead of whole oats creates a much smoother, creamier porridge that's almost pudding-like. I use half a cup of oat flour cooked with one cup of water, though you can use milk for even more richness and protein.

The protein powerhouse is the plant-based chocolate protein powder. I specifically recommend Momentous brand—their chocolate flavor is absolutely perfect for this, not chalky or artificial tasting like some protein powders. One scoop adds a significant protein boost (typically 20-25g depending on the brand) while creating that rich, chocolatey flavor that makes this feel like dessert. If you can't find Momentous, any quality plant-based chocolate protein powder will work, though you may need to adjust sweetness accordingly.

For toppings, frozen raspberries provide antioxidants, fiber, and a tart contrast to the sweet chocolate. I love how they start to thaw and create little pockets of fruity flavor throughout the oatmeal. Sliced almonds add crunch, healthy fats, and a bit more protein. Hemp seeds are nutritional superstars—packed with protein, omega-3 fatty acids, and minerals, they add a subtle nutty flavor. And a drizzle of honey brings everything together with natural sweetness (use maple syrup for a vegan version).

For the eggs, I use 2-3 eggs cooked however I prefer (usually scrambled or over easy), seasoned generously with spices or hot sauce for that spicy kick that balances the sweet oatmeal. The combination of sweet and spicy, creamy and fluffy, makes this breakfast incredibly satisfying on multiple levels.
Instructions Overview:
The process is quick and straightforward, which I appreciate on busy mornings. If you're starting with whole rolled oats, you'll blend them into flour first. This takes maybe 30 seconds in a high-powered blender. Then you'll cook the oat flour with water (or milk) over medium heat, stirring frequently to prevent sticking and lumps. This takes about 5-7 minutes until the mixture thickens and becomes creamy.

Once the oatmeal is cooked to your desired consistency, you'll remove it from heat and stir in the chocolate protein powder. This is important to do off-heat so the protein powder doesn't clump or cook weirdly. Mix it thoroughly until completely incorporated and you have this beautiful chocolate-colored porridge.

Transfer the chocolate oatmeal to a bowl and top with frozen raspberries, sliced almonds, hemp seeds, and a drizzle of honey. The frozen raspberries will start to thaw from the heat of the oatmeal, creating these perfect little pockets of tart, juicy fruit throughout. While the oatmeal is cooking, I'm usually preparing my spicy eggs in a separate pan—multitasking for efficiency.

I cook my eggs however I'm in the mood for that day—sometimes scrambled, sometimes over easy if I want that runny yolk situation. I season them generously with whatever spices sound good (usually garlic powder, paprika, black pepper, and a good amount of hot sauce). Serve the chocolate protein oatmeal and spicy eggs together, and you've got this amazing sweet-and-savory breakfast that delivers on both flavor and nutrition. The whole process from start to finish is about 15 minutes, which makes this very doable even on weekday mornings.
FAQ:
**Can I use regular oats instead of oat flour?**
Absolutely! You can keep the oats whole if you prefer a chunkier texture. Blending them into flour just creates that ultra-smooth, creamy, almost pudding-like consistency that I personally love. Both ways are delicious, so it's really about personal preference. Cooking time might be slightly longer with whole oats.

**What if I don't have plant-based protein powder?**
You can use any chocolate protein powder you have on hand, including whey-based protein if you're not strictly plant-based. The protein count might vary slightly depending on the brand and type, but it will still be a high-protein breakfast. Just note that different protein powders have different sweetness levels, so you might need to adjust your honey accordingly. Some are quite sweet on their own; others need more help.

**How do you actually get to 53g of protein total?**
Great question! Here's the breakdown: Plant-based protein powder typically provides 20-25g per scoop, the oats contribute about 5g, hemp seeds add roughly 3g, almonds contribute about 2g, and 2-3 eggs provide 18-21g. Add it all up and you're right around that 53g mark. It's all about layering multiple protein sources instead of relying on just one. This is how I hit my protein goals as a vegetarian—strategic stacking.

Author: Holistic Bravo
Recipe Card Ingredients:
**Chocolate Protein Oatmeal:**
- 1/2 cup oat flour (or 1/2 cup whole rolled oats blended into flour)
- 1 cup water (or milk of choice for creamier result—dairy or plant-based)
- 1 scoop plant-based chocolate protein powder (I use Momentous brand, about 20-25g protein per scoop)
- Handful of frozen raspberries (about 1/4 cup)
- 2 tablespoons sliced almonds
- 1 tablespoon hemp seeds (don't skip these—they add protein and omega-3s!)
- Honey or maple syrup to taste (about 1 tablespoon)

**Spicy Eggs:**
- 2-3 large eggs (2 eggs for 12g protein, 3 eggs for 18g protein)
- Hot sauce to taste (I use quite a bit—Cholula or Tapatio are my favorites)
- Seasonings: garlic powder, paprika, black pepper, salt (amounts to your preference)
- Cooking spray or small amount of butter/oil for the pan
Recipe Card Instructions:
1. If using whole oats, add 1/2 cup to your blender and blend on high for 20-30 seconds until they reach a fine flour consistency (or skip this if using pre-made oat flour).
2. In a small pot, combine oat flour with 1 cup water (or milk for creamier result).
3. Place pot over medium heat and cook, stirring frequently to prevent sticking and lumps, for 5-7 minutes until mixture thickens into creamy oatmeal.
4. Once oatmeal is thick and creamy, remove from heat (important: don't add protein powder while still on heat).
5. Stir in 1 scoop chocolate protein powder, mixing thoroughly until completely incorporated with no lumps. The oatmeal should turn a rich chocolate color.
6. Transfer chocolate protein oatmeal to your serving bowl.
7. Top with a handful of frozen raspberries (they'll start to thaw and release juices).
8. Sprinkle 2 tablespoons sliced almonds over the top for crunch.
9. Add 1 tablespoon hemp seeds generously (these are protein powerhouses!).
10. Drizzle with honey or maple syrup to taste (about 1 tablespoon).
11. While oatmeal cooks, heat a small pan over medium heat with cooking spray or small amount of butter.
12. Crack 2-3 eggs into the pan and cook to your preference (scrambled, over easy, etc.).
13. Season eggs generously with garlic powder, paprika, black pepper, salt, and hot sauce to taste (don't be shy with the hot sauce!).
14. Serve chocolate protein oatmeal and spicy eggs together as a complete breakfast.
15. Enjoy this sweet-and-savory, protein-packed breakfast that will keep you full for hours!
Nutrition:
Per serving:
- Calories: 550-650
- Protein: 53g (protein powder ~20-25g, oats ~5g, hemp seeds ~3g, almonds ~2g, eggs ~18-21g)
- Carbohydrates: 55-65g
- Fat: 18-24g
- Fiber: 10-12g
- Sugar: 10-15g

Name: Quick High-Protein Quinoa Bowl with Steamed Vegetables
Short Description: A simple, healthy meal for when you don't feel like cooking but still need to hit your protein and calorie goals.
Introduction:
Sometimes I wake up after a 12-hour shift and the last thing I want to do is order takeout or spend an hour in the kitchen. That's exactly when this quick high-protein quinoa bowl becomes my best friend. I created this recipe for those exhausted evenings when I have goals to achieve but zero energy to cook something complicated.

What makes this bowl so brilliant is how it comes together in just 20 minutes with mostly hands-off cooking. The quinoa and edamame simmer together, the vegetables steam themselves while I clean up or vacuum (or convince my dog not to attack the vacuum), and everything finishes at roughly the same time. It's the kind of meal that lets me multitask my way to better nutrition without feeling like I'm spending precious energy on cooking.

The simple Asian-inspired sauce with red wine vinegar, soy sauce, hot honey, and cilantro flakes is what takes this from basic to crave-worthy. I'm not exaggerating when I say this is my new favorite sauce—I make big batches of it and keep it in the fridge to use on everything from rice bowls to roasted vegetables. Plus, with quinoa and edamame providing complete plant-based protein, and eggs adding even more, I'm hitting those protein goals without even trying.

This bowl has become my go-to for those nights when I'm too tired to think but still want to fuel my body properly. It's proof that healthy eating doesn't have to be complicated or time-consuming.
Why You'll Love This:
- Only 20 minutes from start to finish, perfect for exhausted evenings after long work shifts
- Steaming the vegetables gives me free time to do other tasks around the house—I love this efficiency
- Packed with plant-based protein from quinoa and edamame, plus eggs for even more
- The simple sauce is my new favorite—I make a big batch and keep it in the fridge for everything
- Only 450-500 calories but keeps me full and satisfied for hours
- No takeout needed, which means I'm saving money and staying on track with my goals
Tips:
- While the vegetables steam, I use that free time to clean up, load the dishwasher, or do other quick tasks
- Make extra quinoa and edamame at the beginning of the week for faster meal prep throughout—this is my usual strategy
- Adjust the amount of egg whites based on your specific protein goals (I sometimes add extra)
- The sauce works on everything, so I usually double or triple the batch and keep it in a jar
- If you don't have hot honey, mix regular honey with a pinch of red pepper flakes or sriracha to taste
- Substitute tamari for soy sauce if you need it gluten-free
- Any steamed vegetables work here—broccoli, snap peas, or carrots would all be delicious
- You can make this vegan by omitting the eggs and adding baked tofu instead
Ingredients Overview:
The beauty of this bowl is how simple the ingredient list is. Quinoa forms the base—it's one of the few plant foods that's a complete protein containing all nine essential amino acids. That means it keeps me full and provides sustained energy without the crash. I cook it with just water and a pinch of sea salt, nothing fancy. Edamame (young soybeans) gets added to the quinoa pot during the last 5 minutes of cooking, which saves me a dish to wash later. It adds even more plant-based protein and fiber, making this bowl seriously satisfying.

For the vegetables, I use bok choy and mushrooms because they steam quickly and provide vitamins without adding many calories. Bok choy gives me vitamins A, C, and K, while mushrooms bring umami flavor and a meaty texture. But honestly, you can use whatever vegetables you have on hand or need to use up. The key is steaming them so they cook with minimal attention required—that hands-off time is precious when I'm exhausted.

The sauce is where the magic happens and why this bowl never gets boring. Red wine vinegar provides acidity and brightness, soy sauce brings saltiness and umami depth, hot honey adds that perfect sweet-spicy balance that I'm obsessed with, and cilantro flakes tie it all together with herbaceous notes. It's such a simple combination, but the flavors are so well-balanced that I literally use this sauce on everything. I've started making big batches and keeping it in a jar in my fridge.

For extra protein, I add eggs—usually 2 whole eggs plus additional egg whites depending on my protein goals for the day. The runny yolk situation when you break it over everything is perfection.
Instructions Overview:
The process is designed for efficiency and minimal active cooking time, which I appreciate when I'm tired. I start by getting the quinoa going in a pot with water and a pinch of sea salt. It needs to simmer for 12-15 minutes, so I set a timer and mostly forget about it. About 5 minutes before the quinoa finishes, I add edamame right into the same pot. This way everything cooks together and I save myself a dish to wash later.

While the quinoa and edamame are cooking, I prepare my vegetables by washing them thoroughly, then placing them in a steaming pot with a little water. I cover and let them steam for 5-8 minutes until tender but still crisp. This is the beautiful part—the vegetables are steaming on their own while I'm doing other things. Sometimes I load the dishwasher, sometimes I vacuum, sometimes I just sit on the couch and decompress. The food is cooking itself.

I make the sauce by mixing together red wine vinegar, soy sauce, hot honey, and cilantro flakes in a small bowl. This takes maybe 30 seconds. I taste it and adjust if needed—sometimes I want it more acidic, sometimes sweeter. Then I heat a pan over medium heat and cook my eggs to preference. I like mine with runny yolks because breaking that yolk over everything is the best part.

When everything is ready, I assemble: quinoa and edamame go in the bowl first (sometimes I use a small bowl to mold the quinoa into a pretty shape for presentation), then I add the steamed vegetables around the sides, and top with the cooked eggs. Drizzle the sauce over everything or serve it on the side for dipping. The whole process from start to finish is 20 minutes, and most of that is hands-off time. It's perfect for exhausted me.
FAQ:
**Can I meal prep this bowl?**
Yes! This is actually perfect for meal prep, which is how I usually do it. I cook the quinoa, edamame, and vegetables ahead of time and store them separately in the fridge in airtight containers. I make a big batch of sauce and keep it in a jar. When I'm ready to eat, I reheat everything (takes 2-3 minutes in the microwave) and cook my eggs fresh each day for the best results. The eggs are the only thing I don't make ahead.

**What if I don't have hot honey?**
No problem! I didn't have it the first time I made this either. Just mix regular honey with a pinch of red pepper flakes or a small squirt of sriracha. Start with less heat than you think you need—you can always add more, but you can't take it away. I usually do 1 tablespoon honey mixed with 1/4 teaspoon red pepper flakes and adjust from there.

**How can I make this even higher in protein?**
A few options that I rotate through depending on my goals: add more egg whites to your eggs (I sometimes do 2 whole eggs plus 1/2 cup egg whites), increase the edamame portion to a full cup, or include some baked tofu cubes in the bowl. You could also swap the quinoa for a higher-protein grain like amaranth, though quinoa is already pretty high.

Author: Holistic Bravo
Recipe Card Ingredients:
**Bowl Base:**
- 1/2 cup quinoa, dry (white, red, or tri-color all work)
- Pinch of sea salt (about 1/8 teaspoon)
- 1 cup water for cooking quinoa
- 1/2 cup frozen edamame (shelled, no pods)
- 1-2 baby bok choy, washed and halved (about 2 cups)
- 4-6 mushrooms, sliced (cremini or button mushrooms, about 1 cup sliced)

**Protein:**
- 2 whole large eggs
- Additional egg whites to preference (I usually add 2-3 egg whites for extra protein)
- Small amount of cooking spray or oil for eggs

**Asian Sauce (my favorite!):**
- 1 tablespoon red wine vinegar
- 1 tablespoon soy sauce (or tamari for gluten-free)

**Equipment:**
- Steaming pot with lid for vegetables
- Small pot for quinoa
- Small pan for eggs
Recipe Card Instructions:
1. In a small pot, combine 1/2 cup dry quinoa with 1 cup water and a pinch of sea salt. Bring to a boil.
2. Once boiling, reduce heat to low, cover, and simmer for 12-15 minutes until quinoa is cooked and fluffy.
3. About 5 minutes before quinoa finishes, add 1/2 cup frozen edamame directly to the quinoa pot (saves a dish!).
4. While quinoa cooks, prepare your vegetables: wash bok choy and halve lengthwise, slice mushrooms.
5. Place prepared vegetables in a steaming pot with about 1 inch of water in the bottom. Cover with lid.
6. Steam vegetables over medium heat for 5-8 minutes until tender but still crisp (this is hands-off time—go do something else!).
7. While everything steams, make your sauce: in a small bowl, whisk together red wine vinegar, soy sauce, hot honey, and cilantro flakes. Taste and adjust to your preference.
8. Heat a small pan over medium heat with a bit of cooking spray or oil.
9. Crack 2 whole eggs (plus additional egg whites if using) into the pan. Cook to your preference—I love runny yolks for this!
10. When quinoa and edamame are done, fluff with a fork.
11. To assemble: Place cooked quinoa and edamame in your serving bowl (use a small bowl to mold it into a pretty shape if you want).
12. Arrange steamed vegetables around the quinoa.
13. Top with your cooked eggs (that runny yolk is about to make everything amazing).
14. Drizzle the sauce over everything or serve on the side for dipping vegetables.
15. Break that yolk and enjoy your 20-minute high-protein bowl!

Per Serving: Approximately 450-500 calories

Name: Lazy Girl Pumpkin Gnocchi
Short Description: Quick and easy homemade gnocchi made with leftover pumpkin and flour in a food processor—no kneading required!
Introduction:
This is the recipe that proves homemade gnocchi doesn't have to be intimidating or time-consuming. I came home from work at 3:15 and challenged myself to make dinner in under 20 minutes—I was shooting for 15. And I nailed it with this genius lazy girl method that requires no kneading, no rolling out, and barely any cleanup.

The secret is using leftover pumpkin puree (or really any vegetable 'binder') mixed with flour in a food processor. No fancy pasta-making skills required, no long kneading sessions, no complicated techniques. I literally just cut pieces of dough directly into boiling water and watch them float to the surface when they're done. It's the kind of cooking hack that feels almost too easy to be real, but the results are absolutely delicious.

What makes this recipe even better is how versatile it is. Have leftover sweet potato? Use that. Found some spinach in the back of the fridge that needs to be used? Perfect for gnocchi. Leftover roasted butternut squash? Absolutely works. It's my ultimate leftover-rescue recipe that turns yesterday's vegetables into tonight's impressive dinner. I love recipes that reduce food waste while also being incredibly convenient.

This lazy girl method has become my go-to for weeknight pasta when I want something homemade but don't have the time or energy for traditional pasta-making. It's proof that shortcuts can still result in delicious, impressive food.
Why You'll Love This:
- Ready in just 15 minutes from start to finish, perfect when I don't want to spend hours cooking
- No kneading or rolling required—the food processor does all the work for me
- Uses up leftover vegetables that might otherwise go to waste (I hate wasting food)
- I literally just cut pieces of dough into the water, no shaping needed—so easy
- The simple garlic and olive oil sauce with pasta water creates a silky coating that's restaurant-quality
- Can substitute any vegetable puree I have on hand for endless variations
- Makes me feel like a pasta-making genius without requiring any actual pasta-making skills
Tips:
- Don't rush the texture check—dough should be slightly springy to the touch, not sticky or dry (this is the only critical step)
- I weigh my ingredients when I can for more consistent results, but cups work fine too
- For cup measurements, I use 1 cup binder to slightly more than 1 cup flour as a starting point
- Incorporate flour slowly until you reach the right consistency—you can always add more but can't take it away
- Reserve plenty of pasta water—it's essential for creating the silky sauce that makes this special
- I sometimes add 1 tablespoon corn starch or tapioca starch to the pasta water if I want a thicker, more luxurious sauce
- Try other vegetable purees like sweet potato, regular potato, or butternut squash—they all work
- Make a double batch and freeze the uncooked gnocchi on a baking sheet before transferring to freezer bags for quick future meals
Ingredients Overview:
The genius of this recipe is how minimal the ingredient list is. I need leftover pumpkin puree (the recipe uses 162g, which is about a cup, but I'm flexible with amounts), all-purpose flour (175g or slightly more than a cup), and a teaspoon of salt. That's literally it for the gnocchi itself. No eggs, no ricotta, no complicated ingredients—just vegetable puree, flour, and salt.

The key is the ratio between your 'binder' (the pumpkin or other vegetable puree) and your flour. Generally, I want slightly more flour than binder in grams. But I don't stress about exact measurements—I add flour slowly until I get a dough that's springy to the touch. It should feel slightly bouncy when you poke it, not sticky or wet, but also not dry or crumbly. This texture check is the only critical step, and it's pretty intuitive once you do it a couple times.

For the sauce, I keep things simple to let the gnocchi shine. Olive oil, minced garlic, salt, pepper, and reserved pasta water create a classic Italian coating. The starchy pasta water is key here—it emulsifies with the olive oil to make everything silky and cohesive instead of greasy. I learned this technique from traditional Italian cooking, and it works beautifully. Top with fresh parmesan and parsley for the finishing touch, and you've got restaurant-quality gnocchi in 15 minutes.

What I love about this recipe is how it proves that fancy food doesn't require fancy ingredients or techniques. It's all about understanding a few key principles (like the magic of pasta water) and being flexible with what you have on hand.
Instructions Overview:
The process is ridiculously simple, which is why this has become one of my most-made recipes. I add my pumpkin puree, flour, and salt to my food processor and pulse until everything comes together into a cohesive ball of dough. This takes maybe 30 seconds. While the food processor is working, I'm bringing a large pot of salted water to a rolling boil—multitasking for efficiency.

Here's where it gets fun and almost laughably easy: I take my dough ball directly to the pot of boiling water. Using a knife or bench scraper, I cut small pieces of dough and drop them directly into the boiling water. No rolling into ropes, no shaping with a fork or gnocchi board, no fancy techniques. Just cut and drop. This is as easy as cooking gets, and it's why I call this the lazy girl method.

The gnocchi sink at first, then float to the surface when they're cooked, usually about 2-3 minutes. I use a slotted spoon to scoop them out as they finish. Before I drain anything, I make sure to reserve about a cup of that starchy pasta water—this is crucial for the sauce. In a large pan, I sauté minced garlic in olive oil, add some of that magical pasta water, then toss in the cooked gnocchi. Everything gets coated in this silky, garlicky sauce that's way better than any heavy cream sauce.

Plate it up, top with freshly grated parmesan and chopped parsley, and that's it. From start to finish, including boiling water time, this takes about 15 minutes. It's the perfect weeknight dinner when I want something homemade and impressive but don't have the energy for anything complicated.
FAQ:
**Do I really not need to knead or roll out the dough?**
Correct! The food processor does all the work of bringing the dough together. I literally just cut pieces directly into the water. It's the lazy girl method for a reason—maximum results with minimum effort. The texture is slightly different from traditional rolled gnocchi, but honestly, once it's sauced, no one can tell the difference.

**What if my dough is too sticky?**
Add a bit more flour, one tablespoon at a time, and pulse again until you reach the right consistency. The dough should be slightly springy to the touch, not wet or sticky. I've learned that it's better to add flour gradually because you can always add more but you can't take it away. Humidity and the moisture content of your pumpkin can affect this, so just adjust as needed.

**Can I use canned pumpkin?**
Yes! Just make sure it's pure pumpkin puree, not pumpkin pie filling with added sugars and spices. Pure pumpkin puree from a can works perfectly and is actually what I use most often because it's convenient. The 15-ounce can of pumpkin will make enough for about 3-4 servings of gnocchi.

- 175g all-purpose flour (about 1.25-1.5 cups—add gradually until right consistency)
- 1 teaspoon salt (or to taste)

**For the Sauce:**

- 1 tablespoon fresh garlic, minced (about 3 cloves, or use pre-minced jarred garlic for convenience)
- Salt and black pepper, to taste (I'm generous with both)
- 1 cup reserved pasta water (don't skip this—it's what makes the sauce silky!)
- Optional: 1 tablespoon corn starch or tapioca starch for thicker sauce
- 1/4 cup fresh parmesan cheese, grated (get the good stuff!)
- 2 tablespoons fresh parsley, chopped (or 1 teaspoon dried parsley)

- Food processor (this is essential for the lazy girl method)
- Large pot for boiling
- Large pan for sauce
- Slotted spoon
Recipe Card Instructions:
1. Add 162g pumpkin puree (about 1 cup), 175g flour (about 1.25-1.5 cups), and 1 teaspoon salt to your food processor.
2. Pulse 10-15 times until everything comes together into a cohesive ball of dough (takes about 30 seconds total).
3. Check the texture by poking the dough—it should be slightly springy, not sticky or dry. Add more flour if too sticky, or a tiny bit of water if too dry.
4. While food processor works, bring a large pot of salted water to a rolling boil (water should taste salty like the sea).
5. Here's the easy part: Take your dough ball to the boiling water.
6. Using a knife or bench scraper, cut small pieces (about 1 inch) directly over the pot, dropping them into the boiling water as you cut. No rolling, no shaping, just cut and drop!

12. Pour some of the reserved pasta water into the pan with the garlic oil (start with 1/2 cup and add more if needed).
13. Optional: If you want a thicker sauce, whisk in 1 tablespoon corn starch or tapioca starch.
