Name: Simple Two-Ingredient Pasta
Short Description: Learn to make fresh pasta at home with just flour and water—no eggs needed!
Introduction:
Have you ever wanted to make pasta at home but thought it was too complicated, required too many ingredients, or needed expensive equipment? I'm here to hold your hand and prove that in the next 60 seconds, you can become a pasta expert. Seriously—this recipe uses just two ingredients and costs about $10 in basic tools from Amazon.

The philosophy behind this recipe is simple: don't over complicate your life. It takes two ingredients to make pasta—flour and water. That's it. No eggs, no fancy machinery, no complicated techniques. Just mix, knead for 2-3 minutes, roll out, cut, and boil. You can use store-bought sauce or make your own simple version with pasta water and parmesan. It's that straightforward.

What makes this water-based pasta special is how accessible it is. It's completely vegan, beginner-friendly, and proves that homemade pasta doesn't have to be intimidating. I love this recipe for teaching people (including kids) about cooking because there's very little that can go wrong. Plus, it's a great way to build your own confidence in the kitchen before tackling more complex pasta recipes.

I make this at least once a month when I want fresh pasta but don't have eggs on hand, or when I'm cooking for vegan friends. It's become one of my go-to recipes for demonstrating that cooking from scratch doesn't have to be complicated or expensive.
Why You'll Love This:
- Only two ingredients needed—flour and water, making it completely vegan and accessible
- No expensive pasta machines required, just a $10 bench scraper and silicone mat from Amazon
- Ready in about 13 minutes from start to finish once you include cooking time
- Perfect for beginners like I was when I first started making pasta—no intimidation factor
- You can shape it with your thumb or the bench scraper—both work great and I switch depending on my mood
- Makes you feel accomplished without requiring complicated techniques
- Great project for teaching kids to cook or building your own kitchen confidence
Tips:
- Don't rush the mixing process—I use a fork first, then the bench scraper helps incorporate everything smoothly
- Knead by hand for 2-3 minutes until everything is nicely incorporated (you'll feel it come together)
- I let the dough rest for at least 5-10 minutes after kneading; it helps with texture and makes rolling easier
- Cut the dough into 4 equal pieces for easier handling—this makes the process less overwhelming
- Use a gnocchi board or the back of a fork to create ridges that help sauce cling to the pasta
- I've found the bench scraper method is faster than using my thumb for shaping
- Boil in plenty of salted water and cook for only 2-3 minutes until pasta floats—fresh pasta cooks quickly!
- Make your own sauce with starchy pasta water, parmesan, salt, and pepper, or use store-bought (both are great)
Ingredients Overview:
The entire pasta requires just 100g of all-purpose flour and 50g of water. That's a 2:1 ratio that's easy to remember and scale up or down depending on how many people you're feeding. I joke that there's one extra ingredient required: love. But really, that's optional—the flour and water will get you there regardless.

For the flour, all-purpose works perfectly. Nothing fancy needed, no special pasta flour required. I use whatever all-purpose flour I have in my pantry. For the water, regular tap water is fine. Some recipes call for warm water, but I've found that room temperature works just as well. The simplicity is what makes this recipe so accessible.

When it comes to serving, you have options and I encourage you to use what you have or what sounds good. You can use any store-bought sauce you love, which is perfectly acceptable and something I do often when I'm short on time. Or you can make a simple pasta water sauce by combining the starchy cooking liquid with parmesan cheese, salt, and pepper. The pasta water contains starch that helps create a silky coating when mixed with cheese or oil—it's an Italian technique that I use constantly.

This recipe proves that you don't need a long list of ingredients to make something delicious and satisfying. Sometimes the simplest recipes are the most impressive.
Instructions Overview:
The process is wonderfully straightforward and perfect for pasta-making beginners. I start by combining the flour and water in a bowl, using a simple fork to mix everything together until it starts to come together into a shaggy dough. This initial mixing takes maybe a minute. Then I switch to a bench scraper (or a knife if you don't have one) to help incorporate the mixture more thoroughly. I scrape and fold the dough together, getting all those dry bits mixed in.

Once it's mostly together, I use my hands to knead everything for about 2-3 minutes. I'm not trying to develop tons of gluten here like with bread—just getting everything nicely incorporated. The dough should feel smooth and cohesive. I let it rest while I set up my work area and get water boiling. Even just 5-10 minutes of resting time helps the texture and makes the dough easier to work with.

Shaping is where it gets fun. I cut my rested dough ball into 4 equal pieces, then take one piece and roll it out with my hands into a long rope about the thickness of my finger. Using my bench scraper, I cut the rope into small pieces about 1-2 inches each. Then I shape each piece—either by rolling it down a gnocchi board with my thumb to create grooves, or by using the edge of my bench scraper to roll pieces up quickly. The bench scraper method is actually faster, which I appreciate.

Cooking fresh pasta is quick—just 2-3 minutes in boiling salted water until the pieces float to the surface. Then I toss with sauce and serve immediately. The whole process from mixing dough to eating pasta is about 20 minutes including resting time, which is faster than many boxed pasta dinners.
FAQ:
**Do I really not need eggs?**
Correct! This is a traditional water-based pasta that's completely vegan and works beautifully. No eggs required. I love this version because it's more accessible—eggs can be expensive or you might not have them on hand, but flour and water? Almost everyone has those. Plus it's great for vegan friends.

**What if I don't have a gnocchi board?**
No problem! I use the back of a fork to create ridges, which works great. Or skip it entirely and just cut into pieces—the pasta will still taste great even without the grooves. The ridges are traditional and help sauce cling, but they're not mandatory. I've made this plenty of times without bothering to create ridges when I'm in a hurry.

**Can I make this ahead of time?**
Yes! I do this often. Let the shaped pasta dry on a floured surface for 30 minutes, then store in an airtight container in the fridge for up to 2 days. You can also freeze it—just lay pieces on a parchment-lined baking sheet until frozen, then transfer to a freezer bag. Cook from frozen, adding maybe 1 minute to the cooking time.

Name: Lettuce-Free Salad
Short Description: A lettuce-free, high-protein meal prep wrap loaded with seasoned tofu, black beans, and Mediterranean vegetables.
Introduction:
When I call these 'lettuce-free salad' wraps, I'm onto something genius. These are all the best parts of a loaded Mediterranean salad—the protein, the vegetables, the bold flavors—wrapped up in a tortilla and baked until crispy. No wilted lettuce, no soggy mess by day three of meal prep. Just delicious, portable lunches that get better as the week goes on.

The protein content in these wraps is seriously impressive. An entire block of tofu and a full can of black beans go into one batch, meaning each wrap is packed with plant-based protein that keeps me full for hours. This is the kind of meal prep that supports my strength training, long work days, or any time I'm trying to hit higher protein goals without eating the same boring meal every single day.

What really sets these apart is the Mediterranean flavor profile that I'm obsessed with. Sun-dried tomatoes, kalamata olives, pepperoncini, and feta cheese create layers of sweet, briny, tangy, and creamy flavors in every bite. I spread them with both red pepper hummus and pesto, then bake at 425°F. These wraps emerge from the oven crispy on the outside and flavor-packed on the inside. They're legitimately one of my favorite meal prep recipes.

I make a batch every Sunday and have lunches sorted for the entire week. They reheat beautifully in the oven or toaster oven (microwave works but won't be as crispy), and I never get bored of them because there's so much flavor happening in each wrap.
Why You'll Love This:
- Perfect for weekly meal prep—I make a batch on Sunday and I'm set for lunches all week
- Entire block of tofu and full can of black beans provide serious plant-based protein that keeps me satisfied
- No lettuce means no wilting—these actually keep well and taste great all week long (this is huge for meal prep)
- Mediterranean flavors from sun-dried tomatoes, kalamata olives, pepperoncini, and feta are so good
- Baking makes them crispy and holds everything together better than fresh wraps
- Only 350-400 calories per wrap but incredibly filling and satisfying
- Great post-workout meal or portable lunch that I can eat at my desk without making a mess
Tips:
- I press the tofu really well to remove excess water—this helps it get extra crispy when cooking
- Season both the tofu and beans generously—they need that flavor to shine
- Use any size tortilla, but larger ones obviously hold more filling (I usually use burrito-size)
- Baking the wraps makes them crispy and helps them hold together much better than eating them fresh
- I make a big batch on Sunday for grab-and-go lunches all week—this is my meal prep staple
- Reheat in the oven or toaster oven for best results—microwave works but won't be crispy
- Substitute chickpeas for black beans if that's what you have (I've done both)
- Make it vegan by omitting the feta or using vegan feta (I do this for vegan friends)
Ingredients Overview:
The protein base for these wraps is where the magic happens. An entire block of tofu (14-16 ounces) gets drained, pressed, crumbled or cubed, and seasoned generously. I use garlic powder, onion powder, paprika, cumin, salt, and pepper—basically treating it like taco seasoning. When I cook it until golden and crispy, it becomes the perfect texture and flavor foundation for these wraps. Pressing the tofu well before cooking is crucial for getting it crispy instead of soggy.

Adding an entire can of drained and rinsed black beans boosts the protein content even more while adding fiber that helps keep me full. I season them similarly to the tofu so every component carries flavor. This is important—you can't just throw bland beans in and expect the wrap to taste good. Every element needs to be seasoned.

The Mediterranean mix is what makes these wraps special and what I crave every time. Sun-dried tomatoes add umami sweetness and concentrated tomato flavor, kalamata olives bring brininess and richness, pepperoncini contributes tang and a little heat that wakes everything up, and crumbled feta adds creamy saltiness. Each ingredient plays a specific role in creating complex, satisfying flavors. I've tried making these without one or two of these components, and they're just not as good.

For the spreads, I use red pepper hummus on one side (adds creaminess and a mild pepper flavor) and pesto on the other (brings herbal, garlicky notes). Using both creates more interesting flavor than just one spread alone. It's the kind of detail that elevates these from good to great.
Instructions Overview:
The prep process is straightforward and perfect for Sunday meal prep. I start by preparing the protein components: drain and press the tofu really well (I usually let it press for at least 15 minutes with something heavy on top), then crumble or cube it and season generously. The tofu gets cooked in a pan over medium-high heat until it's golden and crispy on the outside—this takes about 5-7 minutes and is worth doing properly. Crispy tofu is a game-changer for these wraps.

While the tofu cooks, I prepare everything else: drain and rinse the black beans and season them, chop the sun-dried tomatoes if they're whole, slice the olives and pepperoncini, and crumble the feta. Having everything prepped and ready makes assembly quick and efficient. I usually set everything out in small bowls so I can production-line the wraps.

Assembly is where it gets fun. I lay out all my tortillas and spread red pepper hummus on one half of each and pesto on the other half. Then I layer the fillings down the center: cooked tofu, black beans, sun-dried tomatoes, olives, pepperoncini, and feta. I don't be shy here—these need to be packed full. Fold in the sides, roll up tightly, and place seam-side down on a baking sheet.

Baking at 425°F for 20 minutes makes all the difference. The wraps get crispy and golden, everything melts together, and they hold their shape perfectly. Once cooled, I store them in individual containers for grab-and-go lunches. They reheat beautifully and somehow taste even better the next day after all the flavors have melded together.
FAQ:
**How long do these wraps keep in the fridge?**
Up to 5 days when stored in airtight containers, which is why they're perfect for weekly meal prep. The lack of lettuce means they don't get soggy or wilted like traditional wraps. I make them on Sunday and they're still great on Friday. Sometimes I make a double batch and keep half in the fridge, half in the freezer for later.

**Can I freeze these wraps?**
Yes! This is one of my favorite meal prep hacks. Wrap them individually in foil or plastic wrap and freeze for up to 2 months. Reheat in the oven directly from frozen at 350°F for about 20 minutes. I love having these in the freezer for those weeks when I don't have time for meal prep. They're like homemade frozen burritos but way better.

**What if I don't like tofu?**
Substitute tempeh (which I sometimes use for variety), chickpeas, or white beans for similar protein content. The recipe is flexible and works with various plant-based proteins. I've even made these with leftover cooked lentils and they were great. The key is seasoning whatever protein you use really well.

Name: Spaghetti Squash with Bechamel Sauce
Short Description: A brilliant low-calorie pasta alternative featuring roasted spaghetti squash topped with a quick homemade béchamel sauce. Perfect for staying in a calorie deficit while enjoying something creamy and satisfying.
Introduction:
I discovered something game-changing about spaghetti squash: you can eat half of an entire squash for only 100 calories. That's not a typo - half a whole squash is just 100 calories. This means you can pile on the indulgent béchamel sauce, add plenty of parmesan cheese, and still stay well within a calorie deficit while feeling completely satisfied.

This recipe is perfect for those times when you want creamy pasta but need to stay on track with weight loss goals. The spaghetti squash naturally separates into noodle-like strands when roasted, creating the perfect low-calorie base for whatever sauce you're craving. The quick béchamel takes only 4-5 minutes to make and transforms this simple vegetable into a restaurant-worthy dish.

What makes this even better is how versatile it is. Whether you're in a calorie deficit, trying to add more vegetables to your diet, or just want a delicious dinner that happens to be healthier, this recipe works. And because the squash itself is so low in calories, you really can add all the indulgent toppings you want without guilt. I've made this countless times when I'm craving comfort food but still want to hit my nutrition goals - it never disappoints.
Why You'll Love This:
- Half an entire spaghetti squash is only 100 calories, allowing you to add plenty of sauce and cheese
- The béchamel sauce takes only 4-5 minutes to make and tastes incredibly indulgent
- Perfect for staying in a calorie deficit while still enjoying creamy, comforting pasta dishes
- Naturally forms "noodles" when you scrape it with a fork - no spiralizer needed
- Great for meal prep - makes delicious leftovers throughout the week
- Low-carb alternative to traditional pasta without feeling like you're missing out
- Impressive dinner that's secretly healthy and supports weight loss goals
Tips:
- Prefer al dente squash? Take it out around the 40-minute mark instead of 45-50 minutes
- Warm the milk in the microwave for 30-45 seconds before adding it to the sauce - this prevents lumps
- The entire béchamel process takes only 4-5 minutes, so have all ingredients ready before starting
- Keep the heat on LOW when making the sauce to prevent burning the butter or flour
- Because the squash is so low calorie, you can add extra cheese without guilt
- Try marinara sauce instead of béchamel for even lower calories
- Add sautéed mushrooms or cherry tomatoes for extra flavor and nutrition
- Season the squash well before roasting for maximum flavor
Ingredients Overview:
Spaghetti squash is the star here, and it's honestly a miracle vegetable when it comes to calorie density. An entire squash is only about 300 calories, meaning you could eat the whole thing if you wanted to. When roasted face-down at 425°F for 45-50 minutes, it becomes tender and the flesh separates into spaghetti-like strands with a fork. A drizzle of olive oil and some salt and pepper before roasting helps develop flavor.

The béchamel sauce is one of the classic French "mother sauces," made with equal parts butter and flour cooked together, then milk whisked in to create a creamy coating. I use 2 tablespoons each of butter and flour, plus 1 cup of milk. Using unsweetened almond milk reduces calories further while keeping the sauce creamy and delicious.

The key to a smooth béchamel is warming the milk first before adding it to the butter-flour mixture. This 30-45 second step in the microwave prevents lumps and helps the sauce come together quickly. Season with salt and pepper, and finish with freshly grated parmesan and chopped parsley for a restaurant-worthy presentation. Trust me, once you master this simple sauce technique, you'll find yourself making it for all kinds of dishes.
Instructions Overview:
Start by preheating your oven to 425°F and preparing the spaghetti squash - cut it in half lengthwise, scoop out the seeds, drizzle with olive oil, and season generously with salt and pepper. Place the halves face-down on a lined baking sheet and roast for 45-50 minutes until tender. If you prefer a more al dente texture, check it around the 40-minute mark.

During the last 5 minutes of roasting, make your béchamel sauce. This is surprisingly quick and easy once you get the hang of it. In a pan over low heat, melt the butter, then whisk in the flour until fully incorporated. Warm your milk in the microwave for 30-45 seconds (this is the secret to a lump-free sauce), then pour it into the butter-flour mixture while stirring constantly. The sauce will thicken quickly - keep stirring until it reaches a creamy, pourable consistency, then season with salt and pepper.

Once the squash is done, use a fork to scrape the flesh into noodle-like strands. You can leave it in the shell for a pretty presentation or transfer to a bowl. Pour half of the béchamel sauce over the squash and gently toss to coat. Grate fresh parmesan over the top and sprinkle with chopped parsley. The whole process is incredibly satisfying and the result looks and tastes like you spent hours in the kitchen.
FAQ:
**Can I eat an entire spaghetti squash?**
Yes! An entire squash is only about 300 calories, so if you're hungry, go for it. The low calorie count means you can definitely eat more than half. I've done it plenty of times when I'm extra hungry and it's always kept me satisfied.

**What if my béchamel gets lumpy?**
Whisk vigorously or use an immersion blender to smooth it out. The best prevention is warming the milk first before adding it to the butter-flour mixture. I learned this the hard way after making lumpy sauce a few times!

**How do I store leftovers?**
Store the squash and sauce separately in airtight containers in the fridge for up to 4 days. Reheat gently in the microwave or on the stovetop. The sauce may thicken when cold, so add a splash of milk when reheating.

- Fresh parsley, chopped (about 2 tablespoons)
Recipe Card Instructions:
1. Cut spaghetti squash in half lengthwise. Scoop out all the seeds and stringy center bits with a spoon - make sure to get it all out.
2. Drizzle cut sides generously with olive oil. Sprinkle with salt and pepper - don't be shy with the seasoning.
3. Place face-down on a parchment-lined baking sheet. Roast at 425°F for 45-50 minutes (or 40 minutes if you like it more al dente). You'll know it's done when you can easily pierce it with a fork.
4. During the last 5 minutes of roasting, melt 2 tablespoons butter in a pan over LOW heat. This is important - low heat prevents burning.
5. Add 2 tablespoons flour to the melted butter. Stir constantly with a whisk or wooden spoon for 1-2 minutes until fully incorporated and no dry flour remains.
6. Warm 1 cup milk in microwave for 30-45 seconds. This step is crucial for preventing lumps.
7. Pour the warmed milk into the butter-flour mixture while stirring constantly. The sauce will thicken quickly as you stir - keep going until it's creamy and pourable, about 2-3 minutes.
8. Season the béchamel with 1 teaspoon salt and 1 teaspoon pepper. Taste and adjust - you might want more or less depending on your preference.
9. Remove roasted squash from oven. Use a fork to scrape the flesh into noodle-like strands. Leave it in the shell or transfer to a serving bowl.
10. Pour half the béchamel sauce over the squash and gently toss to coat every strand. Grate fresh parmesan generously over the top and sprinkle with chopped parsley. Serve immediately while hot and enjoy!
Nutrition:
Per Serving (1/2 squash with sauce): Approximately 250-300 calories

- Protein: 8-10g
- Carbohydrates: 30-35g
- Fat: 12-15g
- Fiber: 5-7g

Name: High-Protein Greek Yogurt Bagel Sandwiches
Short Description: Homemade high-protein bagels made with Greek yogurt and flour, topped with egg whites, spinach, and smoked gouda. At 650 calories and 50g of protein, these breakfast sandwiches keep you full through 12-hour work shifts.
Introduction:
This recipe represents a complete mindset shift from the old "starve until noon then binge at night" pattern. I used to think eating something like a bagel sandwich with 650 calories and 50g of protein would make me gain weight. But through my journey of losing 38 pounds over two years, I discovered that meal prepping substantial, high-protein breakfasts like this actually helped me achieve my goals - not sabotage them.

These aren't just any bagels. They're made with Greek yogurt and flour, creating a dramatically higher protein content than traditional bagels while maintaining that soft, chewy texture we all love. Topped with egg whites cooked with spinach and melted smoked gouda cheese, these breakfast sandwiches keep you full through 12-hour work shifts and prevent the starvation-binge cycle.

The transformation I experienced goes beyond the scale. I went from barely being able to do one push-up three years ago to taking on 150 push-ups daily challenges. My goal was never to be thin - it was to be strong and resilient, to look like I could take on a bear, and to maintain muscle and bone health as I approach my late thirties. This meal prep supports all of that. These bagel sandwiches became one of my weekly staples because they actually fuel my body properly instead of leaving me starving by mid-morning.
Why You'll Love This:
- 650 calories and 50g of protein per sandwich keeps you full for hours through long work shifts
- Greek yogurt bagels have dramatically more protein than traditional bagels
- Perfect for meal prep - make a batch on Sunday for breakfast all week
- Prevents the starve-and-binge cycle by providing substantial morning nutrition
- The bagels take only 20 minutes in the air fryer with no complicated techniques
- Smoked gouda adds rich, indulgent flavor while still being a healthy choice
- Supports building muscle and strength, not just weight loss
- Lets you eat bread every day while still achieving your fitness goals
Tips:
- Make a full batch of bagels on Sunday for easy grab-and-go breakfasts all week
- The bagels can be baked in a regular oven at 350°F if you don't have an air fryer
- Store uncut bagels in an airtight container at room temperature for up to 5 days
- Freeze extra bagels for longer storage - toast them directly from frozen
- Use a whole egg instead of just egg whites if you're not tracking calories as closely
- Try different cheeses like sharp cheddar, pepper jack, or crumbled feta
- Add tomato slices for extra flavor, nutrients, and moisture
- Cook eggs in a small pan sized to fit perfectly on the bagel for ideal sandwich proportions
Ingredients Overview:
The magic starts with the Greek yogurt bagels, which are made using just four ingredients: 140g (about 3/4 cup) Greek yogurt, 140g (about 3/4 cup) all-purpose flour, 1 teaspoon salt, and 1 teaspoon baking powder. The Greek yogurt is essential - it's what provides the significantly higher protein content compared to traditional bagels. Regular yogurt won't work because it's too thin and doesn't have the same protein density. I've tried it both ways and trust me, Greek yogurt is non-negotiable here.

For the topping, everything bagel seasoning adds that classic bagel shop flavor and appearance. It's optional but highly recommended for authenticity - it really makes these taste like the real deal.

The egg filling is where you can really customize based on your protein goals. I use egg whites as the base, which are pure protein with minimal calories - perfect for staying in a calorie deficit while building muscle. Fresh spinach gets cooked right into the eggs, sneaking in extra nutrients and volume without adding many calories. It's an easy way to get more greens in your diet without even trying.

Smoked gouda is my cheese of choice here, adding a rich, smoky flavor that makes this breakfast feel indulgent even though it's healthy. You could substitute any cheese you love, but the smoked gouda really elevates the whole sandwich and makes it feel special.
Instructions Overview:
Start by making the bagel dough, which is incredibly simple - just mix together Greek yogurt, flour, salt, and baking powder until a cohesive dough forms. It should be slightly sticky but workable. Divide the dough into 4-6 portions depending on how large you want your bagels, then shape each portion into a bagel by forming a ball and poking a hole through the center with your finger. Gently stretch the hole to make it wider, as it will close up slightly during cooking.

If using everything bagel seasoning, brush the tops with a little water or beaten egg to help the seasoning stick, then sprinkle generously. Place the bagels in your air fryer basket at 350°F and cook for 10 minutes, then carefully flip them over and cook for another 10 minutes on the other side. They should be golden brown and sound hollow when tapped.

While the bagels are cooking (or do this step fresh each morning if meal prepping), prepare your eggs. Heat a small pan sized to fit perfectly on your bagel - this way the cooked egg will be the right size for your sandwich. Pour egg whites into the heated pan, add fresh spinach right on top, and season with salt, pepper, and a pinch of chili powder for extra flavor. Cook until the egg whites are set and the spinach is wilted, then place a slice of smoked gouda on top while the eggs are still in the pan so it starts to melt. Slice your bagels in half horizontally and assemble your sandwiches. If meal prepping, you can either assemble all the sandwiches now and wrap them individually, or keep the components separate and assemble each morning for the freshest results.
FAQ:
**Do the bagels really taste like bagels?**
They have a slightly different texture - more bread-like and less chewy than traditional bagels - but they're delicious and satisfying with way more protein. It's a worthwhile trade-off in my opinion. I actually prefer these now because they don't make me feel heavy like regular bagels do.

**Can I use regular yogurt instead of Greek?**
No, Greek yogurt is essential for the thick texture and high protein content. Regular yogurt is too thin and won't provide the same structure or nutrition. I learned this the hard way when I tried to substitute it once - the dough was a mess!

**How should I meal prep these?**
You can either assemble the sandwiches fully and wrap them individually for grab-and-go convenience, or keep the bagels, eggs, and cheese separate and assemble fresh each morning for best texture. I usually do a combination - make all the bagels on Sunday, then cook fresh eggs each morning because it only takes 3-4 minutes.

Name: Three-Ingredient Shortbread
Short Description: Simple, buttery shortbread cookies made with just butter, powdered sugar, and flour. Perfect for pairing with coffee when you want to celebrate the season your way.
Introduction:
Sometimes life is about doing what makes you happy, even when everyone else says it's too early for the holiday season. I embraced this philosophy when I made these classic shortbread cookies to pair with Gingerbread Nespresso coffee, celebrating the start of the season my way. And honestly, when a recipe requires only three ingredients and delivers that melt-in-your-mouth buttery goodness, why wait?

Full transparency: these cookies didn't hold their shape perfectly. I admit I was impatient - I microwaved the butter instead of letting it soften properly, and only chilled the dough for 10 minutes instead of the recommended 30. But you know what? They still turned out buttery and soft on the inside with crunchy edges, and the flavors were excellent. Sometimes imperfect cookies made with joy are better than perfect cookies made with stress.

This recipe proves that even when you don't follow all the rules, simple ingredients can still create something delicious. But if you want cookies that hold their shape beautifully, learn from my mistakes: let the butter soften at room temperature (don't microwave it), and give the dough a full 30 minutes to chill before cutting and baking. I've made these both ways now, and the properly-made version is definitely worth the extra patience - though the rushed version was still pretty darn good!
Why You'll Love This:
- Only three ingredients required - butter, powdered sugar, and flour
- Classic buttery shortbread flavor that melts in your mouth
- Perfect for pairing with coffee or tea during the holiday season
- Simple enough that even beginners can succeed (as long as you don't rush like I did)
- Makes 24-30 cookies, great for gift-giving or having treats all week
- Even when they don't hold perfect shape, they still taste amazing
- No complicated techniques or expensive equipment needed
Tips:
- CRITICAL: Don't microwave the butter to soften it - let it sit at room temperature for 30-60 minutes instead. I learned this lesson the hard way!
- The proper butter texture is softened but still cool - should indent when pressed but not be greasy
- IMPORTANT: Chill the dough for a full 30 minutes (or more) for cookies that hold their shape. This is where I went wrong the first time.
- If the dough is too soft to work with after chilling, pop it back in the fridge for another 10 minutes
- Cut into classic shapes like circles, squares, or holiday themes with cookie cutters
- Add 1 teaspoon vanilla extract for extra flavor if desired
- Dip cooled cookies in melted chocolate for extra indulgence
- Sprinkle with coarse sugar before baking for sparkle and crunch
Ingredients Overview:
The beauty of shortbread is in its simplicity. Real butter is the star - use 1.5 sticks (12 tablespoons) of good quality butter, and never substitute margarine. The butter must be softened properly at room temperature, not microwaved, for best texture and shape retention. This takes patience - about 30-60 minutes sitting on the counter - but it makes all the difference. I know because I've done it both ways and the results are noticeably different.

Powdered sugar (1/2 cup) creates a more tender, delicate texture than granulated sugar. It's what gives shortbread that signature melt-in-your-mouth quality. The fine texture of powdered sugar incorporates more easily into the butter and creates cookies that are less dense and more refined.

All-purpose flour (1.5 cups) provides the structure. The key is not overmixing once you add the flour, or the cookies will be tough instead of tender. Mix just until the dough comes together, then stop. I always remind myself of this because it's tempting to keep mixing, but restraint is key here. These simple ingredients, when handled properly, create something truly special that tastes like it came from a fancy bakery.
Instructions Overview:
Take your butter out of the fridge well in advance - at least 30-60 minutes before you plan to bake. Let it soften at room temperature until it's soft enough to indent with your finger but not greasy or melted. Don't rush this step by microwaving it - patience pays off here with better texture and cookies that hold their shape.

In a bowl, beat together the softened butter and powdered sugar until light and fluffy. This usually takes 2-3 minutes with an electric mixer, or a bit longer by hand. You want to see the mixture lighten in color and increase slightly in volume. Gradually add the flour to the butter-sugar mixture, mixing until just combined and a dough forms. Don't overmix or your cookies will be tough - stop as soon as everything comes together.

Here's the step many people skip but shouldn't: wrap the dough and refrigerate for at least 30 minutes. I only did 10 minutes the first time and regretted it - don't make the same mistake. The chilling helps the cookies hold their shape beautifully. After the dough is properly chilled, roll it out on a floured surface to about 1/2 inch thickness and use cookie cutters to cut into desired shapes. Place cookies on a lined baking sheet with a bit of space between them. Bake at 350°F for 12-15 minutes until the edges just start to turn golden - don't overbake, as shortbread should still be quite pale when done. Let the cookies cool on the baking sheet for 5 minutes before transferring to a wire rack.
FAQ:
**Why didn't my cookies keep their shape?**
Two common reasons: the butter wasn't properly softened at room temperature (microwaving makes it too soft), and/or the dough wasn't chilled long enough before baking. Both steps are crucial. I made both mistakes my first time and the cookies spread, but they still tasted great!

**Can I freeze the dough?**
Yes! Shortbread dough freezes beautifully for up to 3 months. Wrap tightly in plastic wrap and freeze. Thaw in the fridge overnight before rolling and cutting. This is perfect for having dough ready whenever you want fresh cookies.

**What's the proper butter texture for shortbread?**
Softened but still cool - it should indent when you press it with your finger, but shouldn't be greasy, melted, or sliding around. This takes 30-60 minutes at room temperature, not 30 seconds in the microwave. Trust me on this one - I learned the hard way!

Author: Holistic Bravo
Recipe Card Ingredients:
- 1.5 sticks (12 tablespoons / 170g) butter, softened at room temperature (not microwaved!)
- 1/2 cup (60g) powdered sugar (also called confectioners' sugar or icing sugar)
- 1.5 cups (190g) all-purpose flour
Recipe Card Instructions:
1. Let butter soften at room temperature for 30-60 minutes. Do NOT microwave - this is crucial for cookies that hold their shape. The butter should indent when pressed but not be greasy.
2. Beat softened butter and powdered sugar together until light and fluffy, about 2-3 minutes with an electric mixer. The mixture should lighten in color.
3. Gradually add flour to the butter-sugar mixture. Mix until just combined and a dough forms - don't overmix or cookies will be tough. Stop as soon as everything comes together.
4. Wrap dough in plastic wrap and refrigerate for at least 30 minutes (this is essential - don't skip or shorten this time like I did!).
5. Roll out chilled dough on a lightly floured surface to about 1/2 inch thickness. Use a ruler if you want to be precise.
6. Cut into desired shapes with cookie cutters. Gather scraps, re-roll, and cut more cookies until all dough is used.
7. Place cookies on a parchment-lined baking sheet with about 1 inch of space between them. They don't spread much but give them a little room.
8. Bake at 350°F for 12-15 minutes until edges just start to turn golden. The centers should still be quite pale - don't overbake or they'll be hard instead of tender.
9. Cool on baking sheet for 5 minutes (they're fragile when hot), then carefully transfer to a wire rack to cool completely.
10. Serve with your favorite coffee or tea and celebrate the season your way, regardless of what others think about timing!
Nutrition:
Per Cookie (based on 30 cookies): Approximately 80-100 calories

Name: Three Ways to Use Cabbage
Short Description: Transform an entire head of cabbage into three delicious, low-calorie dishes: pickled cabbage for gut health, cabbage noodles with Asian-inspired spiced broth, and eggs in a cabbage basket for breakfast.
Introduction:
Cabbage is the low-carb, low-calorie best friend you didn't know you needed. At only 22 calories per cup, it's an excellent source of vitamins K and C, has anti-inflammatory properties, and improves digestion. I call it one of my secret weapons for staying in a calorie deficit during my weight loss journey, and after you see these three creative recipes, you'll understand why.

The genius of this approach is shredding an entire head of cabbage at once and storing it in the fridge. When it's already prepped and ready to go, you're much more likely to actually use it throughout the week. You can turn it into quick-pickled cabbage for gut health that lasts two weeks, transform it into savory "noodles" with Asian-inspired spiced broth, or use it as a creative base for eggs in a basket.

Before anyone panics, I want to make one thing clear: I'm not demonizing carbs or telling you to replace pasta forever. I love carbs, eat pasta every week, and I'm very vocal about balanced eating. These are just fun, low-calorie alternatives that help add more vegetables to your diet while keeping you satisfied during weight loss. Think of cabbage as a tool for high-volume eating, not a replacement for the foods you love. I still enjoy my regular pasta and bread - these cabbage recipes just give me options when I want to eat a massive portion without using all my calories.
Why You'll Love This:
- At only 22 calories per cup, cabbage lets you eat huge portions without guilt
- Three completely different recipes from one head of cabbage means variety all week
- Pickled cabbage keeps for 2 weeks and gives your metabolism a morning kickstart
- Cabbage noodles with spiced broth are a fun low-calorie alternative when you want volume
- Eggs in cabbage basket is a creative twist on a breakfast classic
- Shredding the whole head at once means it's ready to use all week long
- Perfect for high-volume eating to stay full while in a calorie deficit
Tips:
- Shred the entire head at once and store in an airtight container in the fridge for 5-7 days
- Having it pre-shredded makes you way more likely to actually use it
- The pickled cabbage gives a morning "jolt" when you eat a spoonful - great for kickstarting your day
- For thicker broth on the cabbage noodles, add a cornstarch slurry
- Nutritional yeast is a great vegan substitute for parmesan on the noodles
- Adjust spice levels in the noodles to your preference - make it mild or fiery
- Keep pickled cabbage as a condiment for bowls, tacos, sandwiches, or anything that needs tang
- Use raw shredded cabbage as a salad base or munch on it when you need something crunchy
Ingredients Overview:
For the pickled cabbage, you only need the shredded cabbage itself plus a simple brine made from salt, sugar, apple cider vinegar, and hot water. Pack it into a mason jar, pour the brine over, and let it sit overnight. It lasts about two weeks in the fridge and becomes your go-to condiment for everything. I eat a spoonful in the morning sometimes just for that wake-up tang!

The cabbage noodles are where things get fun and flavorful. You cook the shredded cabbage in a large pot of salted water loaded with spices: curry powder, garlic powder, onion powder, ginger powder, chili powder, red pepper flakes, and cilantro flakes. Red wine vinegar and low-sodium soy sauce add depth and umami. The cabbage soaks in all these flavors for about 30 minutes until fork tender but still slightly al dente. Top with parmesan or nutritional yeast for vegan option - both are delicious.

For eggs in a cabbage basket, you just need a handful of shredded cabbage, a whole egg, some egg whites for extra protein, and seasonings. Serve on toast or with rice. It's the simplest of the three recipes but equally satisfying. The cabbage acts as both vegetable and "vessel" for the egg, creating this fun breakfast that feels special even though it's just a few basic ingredients. I love making this on lazy Sunday mornings when I want something different but don't want to work too hard.
Instructions Overview:
Start by shredding an entire head of cabbage at once - this is key to actually using it all week. Store it in an airtight container in the fridge and it'll be ready whenever you need it.

For pickled cabbage, pack a portion of the shredded cabbage into a clean mason jar fairly tightly. Make a quick brine by combining salt, sugar, apple cider vinegar, and hot water, stirring until the salt and sugar dissolve completely. Pour the brine over the cabbage, making sure it's completely submerged, close the lid, and refrigerate overnight. The longer it sits, the more pickled it becomes, and it will last about 2 weeks in the fridge.

For cabbage noodles, bring a large pot of salted water to a boil and add all your seasonings directly to the water - curry powder, garlic powder, onion powder, ginger powder, chili powder, red pepper flakes, cilantro flakes, red wine vinegar, and low-sodium soy sauce. Add a large portion of your shredded cabbage and let it cook in the spiced water for about 30 minutes until fork tender but still with a slight bite. Scoop the cabbage "noodles" into bowls with some of the flavorful broth and top with parmesan or nutritional yeast.

For eggs in a cabbage basket, add a handful of shredded cabbage to a pan over medium-low heat, cover with a lid, and let it cook until it starts to soften. Make a little well in the middle, crack one whole egg into the center, pour egg whites directly on top of the cabbage around the whole egg, and season with salt and pepper. Cover and cook until the egg is set to your preference, then serve on toast or with rice. It's such a simple but satisfying breakfast!
FAQ:
**Aren't you just avoiding carbs by using cabbage?**
No! I'm very clear that I love carbs and eat pasta every week. This is just a fun, low-calorie alternative for when I want volume and vegetables, not a replacement for carbs. I'm all about balance and having options, not restriction.

**How long does shredded cabbage last in the fridge?**
About 5-7 days in an airtight container. Having it pre-shredded makes you way more likely to actually use it before it goes bad. I find that when I have to shred it fresh each time, I never end up using it.

**Can I use these recipes if I'm not trying to lose weight?**
Absolutely! These are simply delicious, healthy ways to eat more vegetables and add variety to your diet. The pickled cabbage especially has become a staple in my kitchen regardless of whether I'm in a deficit or not - it just tastes good and is great for gut health.

- Quick High-Protein Quinoa Bowl
Author: Holistic Bravo
Recipe Card Ingredients:
**For Pickled Cabbage:**
- Shredded cabbage (about 2-3 cups, or portion of the head)
- 1 tablespoon salt
- 1 tablespoon sugar (white or cane sugar)
- 1 cup apple cider vinegar (any vinegar works but apple cider is my favorite)
- 2 cups hot water

**For Cabbage Noodles:**
- Shredded cabbage (large portion, about 4-5 cups)
- Large pot of salted water (enough to cover the cabbage)
- 1 tablespoon curry powder
- 1 tablespoon garlic powder
- 1 tablespoon onion powder
- 1 teaspoon ginger powder
- 1 teaspoon chili powder (adjust for spice preference)
- 1 teaspoon red pepper flakes (or less if you prefer mild)
- 1 teaspoon cilantro flakes (or fresh cilantro)
- 1 tablespoon red wine vinegar (any vinegar works)
- 1 tablespoon low-sodium soy sauce
- Parmesan cheese or nutritional yeast for topping

**For Eggs in Cabbage Basket:**
- 1 large handful shredded cabbage (about 1-1.5 cups)
- 1 whole egg
- 3-4 egg whites (for extra protein)
- Sea salt and black pepper, to taste
- Toast or rice for serving
Recipe Card Instructions:
**Pickled Cabbage:**
1. Pack shredded cabbage into a clean mason jar fairly tightly but don't crush it.
2. In a bowl, combine 1 tablespoon salt, 1 tablespoon sugar, 1 cup apple cider vinegar, and 2 cups hot water. Stir everything together until salt and sugar dissolve completely.
3. Pour brine over cabbage in the jar, making sure cabbage is completely submerged. If needed, add a little more water.
4. Close lid tightly and refrigerate overnight before using. The longer it sits, the more pickled it becomes. Lasts 2 weeks in the fridge.

**Cabbage Noodles:**
1. Boil a large pot of salted water - you want enough to cover all the cabbage with room to stir.
2. Add all seasonings directly to the boiling water: curry powder, garlic powder, onion powder, ginger powder, chili powder, red pepper flakes, cilantro flakes, red wine vinegar, and low-sodium soy sauce. Stir to combine.
3. Add a large portion of shredded cabbage to the spiced water. Stir it around to submerge.
4. Let the cabbage cook in the spiced water for about 30 minutes, stirring occasionally. You want it fork tender but still with a slight bite, kind of like al dente pasta.
5. Scoop cabbage "noodles" into serving bowls and add some of the flavorful broth. Top generously with shredded parmesan cheese or nutritional yeast. Enjoy while hot!

**Eggs in Cabbage Basket:**
1. Add a large handful of shredded cabbage to a pan over medium-low heat. Cover with a lid and let it cook for 3-4 minutes until it starts to soften and wilt.
2. Make a little well or hole in the middle of the cabbage where your egg will sit. Push the cabbage to the sides.
3. Crack 1 whole egg into the center well you created.
4. Pour 3-4 egg whites directly on top of the cabbage around the whole egg. This holds everything together and boosts the protein.
5. Season with sea salt and black pepper to taste. Keep it simple or add other seasonings you like.
6. Cover the pan and cook until the egg is set to your preference - runny yolk, medium, or fully cooked. This takes about 5-7 minutes for medium.
7. Serve immediately on top of toast or with rice or any grain you like. It's like a deconstructed breakfast with built-in vegetables!
Nutrition:
Cabbage: 22 calories per cup

Per Serving Estimates:
- Pickled Cabbage: 25-30 calories
- Cabbage Noodles: 50-70 calories
- Eggs in Cabbage Basket: 150-200 calories

Name: No-Egg Pasta Pomodoro
Short Description: Simple vegan pasta made with just water and flour, paired with a rustic pomodoro sauce featuring both air-fried and stovetop tomatoes for complex sweet and acidic layers of flavor.
Introduction:
Did you know you can make pasta with just two ingredients? Water and flour. No eggs needed. I actually prefer this water-based pasta over egg pasta because it absorbs more sauce and has what I consider a better texture. It's still slightly al dente but softer and more pliable, making it perfect for soaking up every bit of that delicious pomodoro sauce.

The real star of this dish is the pomodoro sauce made two ways. Half the tomatoes get cooked in the air fryer, where they develop a sweet, jammy quality as their sugars concentrate and caramelize slightly. The other half cook on the stovetop with olive oil, salt, and pepper, maintaining their bright, acidic notes. When you combine both methods, you get a sauce with complex layers of flavor that tastes like it took hours when it really only took about 20 minutes.

This is the kind of recipe that proves you don't need complicated ingredients or techniques to make restaurant-quality food at home. With quality fresh tomatoes as your star ingredient, kept simple with just olive oil, salt, pepper, and pasta water (plus optional parmesan), you get a dish that celebrates the ingredients rather than drowning them in additions. I make this all the time when I have fresh tomatoes that need to be used - it's become one of my favorite simple dinners that feels special every time.
Why You'll Love This:
- Only two ingredients needed for the pasta - water and flour, making it completely vegan
- Water-based pasta absorbs sauce better than egg pasta in my experience
- The dual tomato cooking method creates complex sweet and acidic layers of flavor
- Keeps the sauce incredibly simple - 4 ingredients or less plus pasta water
- Ready in about 35 minutes from start to finish
- Fresh tomatoes are truly the star, not hidden under heavy seasonings
- Optional parmesan helps the sauce emulsify and come together beautifully
- Perfect for using up fresh tomatoes that need to be eaten
Tips:
- I prefer water-based pasta over egg pasta for its texture and sauce absorption
- Air-fried tomatoes add sweet, jammy, almost caramelized notes to the sauce
- If you don't have an air fryer, roast half the tomatoes in the oven at 425°F for 15-20 minutes
- Save that pasta water - the starch is essential for bringing the sauce together
- Keep the sauce simple - don't over-complicate with too many additions
- Fresh basil would be a nice addition but isn't necessary
- The parmesan is optional but really does help the sauce emulsify and taste complete
- Cherry tomatoes, roma tomatoes, or any fresh tomatoes work well
Ingredients Overview:
The pasta itself is just flour and water in a 2:1 ratio. For a single serving, I use approximately 100g flour to 50g water. Scale up or down as needed. All-purpose flour works perfectly - nothing fancy required. The key is mixing and kneading just until everything comes together, then letting it rest while you prepare the sauce. Don't overwork the dough or it will be tough instead of tender.

For the pomodoro sauce, you need one pack of fresh tomatoes that you'll divide for two different cooking methods. Half go in the air fryer where they cook until they develop a sweet, jammy quality - the heat concentrates their sugars and creates almost caramelized notes. The other half cook on the stovetop with olive oil, salt, and pepper, maintaining their fresh acidity and brightness. This combination is what makes the sauce so special - you get both sweet and tangy notes working together.

Pasta water is not optional here - it's essential. The starchy water helps thin the sauce to the right consistency and emulsifies everything together. Add it gradually until you reach the texture you want. I always save at least a cup because it's so important for making the sauce come together properly.

The parmesan is listed as optional, but I'll be honest - it "helps the sauce come together in the best way possible." It adds umami depth and helps create a cohesive sauce that clings to every strand of pasta. I rarely skip it unless I'm making this for vegan friends.
Instructions Overview:
Start by making your pasta dough - combine flour and water in a 2:1 ratio (100g flour to 50g water per serving), mix and knead until smooth for about 2-3 minutes, then let it rest while you prepare the sauce. This resting time helps the gluten relax and makes the dough easier to roll out.

Divide your fresh tomatoes into two portions for two different cooking methods that create complex flavor. Place half the tomatoes in your air fryer and cook them until they start to break down and develop a sweet, jammy quality - you're looking for them to be soft with slightly concentrated flavors. While the air fryer works, heat olive oil in a pan over medium heat and add the remaining tomatoes with salt and pepper to taste. Cook them down on the stovetop until they soften and release their juices, maintaining their bright acidity.

Roll out your rested pasta dough to your desired thickness and cut it into your preferred shape - I like wider noodles for this dish but you can make any shape you like. Bring a pot of salted water to a boil, add your fresh pasta, and cook for 2-3 minutes until al dente. Fresh pasta cooks much faster than dried pasta, so watch it carefully. Before draining, reserve at least a cup of the pasta water - this is crucial for the sauce.

Add some of the reserved pasta water to your stovetop tomatoes - the starch helps thin the sauce slightly and brings everything together. Add the cooked pasta to the pan with the stovetop tomatoes, then add the air-fried tomatoes as well. Toss everything together so the pasta gets coated in both the saucy tomatoes and the jammy sweet ones. If using parmesan, grate it fresh over the pasta now and toss one more time. The whole process is simple but the results are impressive!
FAQ:
**Is water-based pasta as good as egg pasta?**
I actually prefer it! It absorbs sauce better and has what I consider a better texture - still al dente but softer and more pliable. It's definitely worth trying if you've never made it before. I was skeptical at first too, but now I make it this way all the time.

**Do I need an air fryer?**
No, you can roast half the tomatoes in the oven at 425°F for 15-20 minutes instead. You're looking for the same sweet, concentrated effect. The air fryer is just faster and more convenient.

**Can I make this completely vegan?**
Yes! Just omit the parmesan at the end, or substitute nutritional yeast or vegan parmesan for similar umami flavor. The sauce is still delicious without it, just slightly less rich.

Name: Plant-Based Milk Four Ways
Short Description: Learn to make four different plant-based milks at home: hemp (surprisingly flavorful and ranked #1), rice (nostalgic horchata vibes), almond (most versatile for recipes), and cashew (thickest, like heavy cream).
Introduction:
On today's episode of what free will looks like when you're 36: making four different kinds of plant-based milk just because I can. I decided to explore hemp, rice, almond, and cashew milk to discover which one I loved most and what makes each unique. The results were surprising - hemp milk was the most flavorful and the biggest surprise, rice milk gave nostalgic lower-calorie horchata vibes, almond proved its versatility for recipes, and cashew was the thickest and most similar to heavy cream.

Making plant-based milk at home is more cost-effective than buying it, lets you control exactly what goes into it, and creates zero waste if you save the pulp for other recipes. Plus, there's something satisfying about knowing exactly what's in your milk - no weird additives, thickeners, or preservatives. Just nuts or seeds or rice, water, and whatever sweetener or flavorings you choose.

Each milk has its own personality and best uses. Hemp is surprisingly flavorful on its own and I ranked it #1, rice gives you that horchata feel that brings back childhood memories, almond never overpowers other ingredients in recipes which makes it my cooking go-to, and cashew is thick and creamy like dairy which is perfect for specific uses. Once you try them all, you'll know exactly which one to reach for depending on what you're making. I keep at least two varieties in my fridge at all times now.
Why You'll Love This:
- More cost-effective than buying plant-based milk at the store
- Control all ingredients and avoid additives, thickeners, and preservatives
- Zero-waste option when you save the pulp for baking or smoothies
- Hemp milk is surprisingly flavorful and high in omega-3s and complete protein
- Rice milk gives nostalgic horchata vibes and is naturally sweet and lower calorie
- Almond milk is the most versatile and never overpowers other recipe ingredients
- Cashew milk is thick like heavy cream, perfect for creamy soups and sauces
Tips:
- A high-speed blender like a Vitamix is essential for breaking down nuts and seeds thoroughly
- A nut milk bag makes straining much easier and more thorough than cheesecloth
- Always use raw, unsalted nuts and seeds for milk-making - roasted varieties create off-flavors
- Add dates for natural sweetness instead of processed sugar
- Add vanilla extract for classic milk flavor
- Add a pinch of salt to any milk to enhance the natural flavors
- Rice milk is naturally sweet and needs less added sweetener than other milks
- Save the pulp for baking, smoothies, energy balls, or as a thickener in soups
- Fresh homemade milk separates naturally - just shake before using
- Cashew milk doesn't need straining if you prefer it thick and creamy
Ingredients Overview:
For hemp milk, you need raw hemp seeds or hemp hearts plus water. Hemp doesn't require soaking and blends quickly, which makes it super convenient. It's the highest in omega-3 fatty acids and contains complete protein, making it nutritionally impressive. I was shocked by how flavorful it is - there's a distinct hemp taste that's actually delicious and not at all what I expected.

Rice milk requires cooked white rice that's been cooled, plus water. This is the most naturally sweet option because of the rice's carbohydrate content. Add cinnamon and vanilla for authentic horchata flavor that brings back memories. It's also the lowest in calories which is a nice bonus.

Almond milk is made from raw almonds and water. Almonds benefit from soaking for a few hours or overnight, which makes them easier to blend and creates creamier milk. Optional additions include dates for sweetness and vanilla for flavor. This is my go-to for cooking and baking because it has such a neutral flavor that never overpowers other ingredients.

Cashew milk uses raw cashews and water. Cashews can skip the soaking step because they're softer than almonds, though soaking does create even creamier results. This milk has the thickest consistency of all four - it's almost like heavy cream which makes it perfect for certain applications but a bit too thick for everyday drinking in my opinion.

The general ratio for most nut and seed milks is about 1 cup of the base ingredient to 3-4 cups of water, though you can adjust for thicker or thinner milk. Always use a high-speed blender for best results - I can't stress this enough!
Instructions Overview:
The general process is the same for all four milks with slight variations. Start by preparing your base ingredient - for nuts and seeds, soak if needed (almonds benefit from soaking for a few hours or overnight, cashews can skip it but soaking helps create creamier results, hemp doesn't need soaking at all). For rice milk, cook rice first and let it cool completely before blending.

Add your base ingredient and water to a high-speed blender. The typical ratio is about 1 cup base to 3-4 cups water, but this varies - hemp and cashew use less water for creamier results, rice and almond use more for a thinner consistency. Blend on high speed for 1-2 minutes until completely smooth. A high-speed blender like a Vitamix makes a huge difference in the final texture and I highly recommend investing in one if you plan to make milk regularly.

Strain the mixture through a nut milk bag or fine-mesh strainer into a bowl or pitcher. Squeeze the bag thoroughly to extract all the liquid - you'd be surprised how much milk is still in there even when you think you're done squeezing. The pulp that remains can be saved for other recipes like baking, smoothies, or energy balls - don't waste it!

Add any sweeteners or flavorings now if desired - dates, vanilla extract, cinnamon, maple syrup, or a pinch of salt all work beautifully. Transfer to an airtight container and refrigerate. Homemade milk lasts 3-5 days, which is much shorter than store-bought because there are no preservatives, but the flavor is fresher and better. Shake well before each use as natural separation is completely normal.
FAQ:
**Which milk should I start with?**
Start with cashew or almond - they're the easiest and most forgiving for beginners. They blend smoothly and taste familiar to most people. That said, hemp was my personal favorite and biggest surprise, so if you're adventurous, try that one!

**How long does homemade plant milk last?**
3-5 days in the refrigerator. Without preservatives, the shelf life is much shorter than store-bought, but the flavor is fresher and better. I usually make a batch twice a week to always have fresh milk on hand.

**Do I need to strain all of them?**
Cashew milk can skip straining if you like it thick and creamy - I actually prefer it unstrainable for certain uses like creamy soups. The others benefit from straining for a smooth, drinkable texture that's more like what you're used to from store-bought versions.

**What do I do with the leftover pulp?**
Use it in smoothies for extra fiber and nutrients, add it to muffin or cookie batter for moisture and nutrition, make energy balls with dates and nuts, or use as a thickener in soups. Don't waste it - it still has nutrients and fiber! I keep a container in my fridge specifically for collecting pulp to use throughout the week.

- Overnight Chia Seed Tiramisu
- Hot Chocolate Protein Oatmeal
- Cozy Chunky Oatmeal
Author: Holistic Bravo
Recipe Card Ingredients:
**For Hemp Milk (Ranked #1 - Most Flavorful):**
- 1 cup raw hemp seeds/hearts
- 3-4 cups water (use less for creamier milk)
- Optional: 1-2 pitted dates for sweetness, 1 teaspoon vanilla extract, pinch of salt

**For Rice Milk (Ranked #2 - Horchata Vibes):**
- 1 cup cooked white rice, completely cooled
- 4 cups water
- Optional: 1 teaspoon cinnamon (highly recommended for horchata flavor), 1 teaspoon vanilla extract, 1-2 dates or maple syrup

**For Almond Milk (Ranked #3 - Most Versatile for Cooking):**
- 1 cup raw almonds (soaked 4-8 hours or overnight, then drained)
- 4 cups water
- Optional: 1-2 pitted dates for sweetness, 1 teaspoon vanilla extract, pinch of salt

**For Cashew Milk (Ranked #4 - Thickest, Like Heavy Cream):**
- 1 cup raw cashews (soaking optional but creates creamier results)
- 3 cups water (use less for even thicker consistency)
- Optional: 1-2 dates for sweetness, 1 teaspoon vanilla extract, pinch of salt
Recipe Card Instructions:
1. Prepare base ingredient: For nuts, soak if needed (almonds 4-8 hours, cashews optional but helpful, hemp doesn't need soaking). For rice milk, cook 1 cup rice and let it cool completely.
2. Combine base ingredient with water in high-speed blender. Ratio varies: hemp and cashew use less water (3 cups) for creamier milk, rice and almond use more water (4 cups) for thinner consistency. Adjust to your preference.
3. Blend on high speed for 1-2 minutes until completely smooth. Listen for when the sound changes - that means it's fully blended. A high-speed blender is crucial for smooth results.
4. Strain mixture through a nut milk bag or fine-mesh strainer into a bowl or pitcher. Squeeze the bag thoroughly to extract all the liquid - really work it to get every drop.
5. Add sweeteners or flavorings if desired: dates, vanilla, cinnamon (especially for rice milk!), maple syrup, or pinch of salt. Blend briefly again if adding dates to fully incorporate.
6. Transfer to an airtight container (I use glass bottles) and refrigerate for up to 3-5 days. The milk will separate naturally - this is totally normal.
7. Shake well before each use. Enjoy in coffee, smoothies, cereal, or just drink it straight!

**My Personal Rankings:**
1. Hemp - Most flavorful, biggest surprise, highest nutrition
2. Rice - Nostalgic horchata flavor, naturally sweet, lower calorie
3. Almond - Most versatile for recipes, neutral flavor, my cooking staple
4. Cashew - Thickest like cream, great for specific uses, but too thick for everyday drinking

Approximate Calories Per Cup:
- Hemp Milk: 60-80 calories
- Rice Milk: 100-120 calories
- Almond Milk: 30-50 calories (unsweetened)
- Cashew Milk: 130-150 calories

Name: Overnight Chia Seed Tiramisu
Short Description: High-protein overnight breakfast with 52g of protein, featuring chia seeds, Greek yogurt, espresso, and white chocolate layers. Perfect for surviving those 1:00 a.m. wake-ups and 12-hour shifts.
Introduction:
Mama said there'd be days like this - days when you have to wake up at 1:00 a.m. for multiple 12-hour shifts back to back to back. On those extra long days that feel like you're stuck in a time loop, you need something special to look forward to. Something that's already prepared, packed with protein to keep you going, and includes a shot of espresso because you're definitely going to need caffeine when waking up in the middle of the night.

This overnight chia seed tiramisu checks all those boxes. I threw this together quickly the night before my brutal work week, and it gave me life the next morning. With 52g of protein from Greek yogurt and chia seeds, plus a shot of espresso mixed right in, this breakfast is designed for surviving long shifts and busy mornings when you need grab-and-go fuel that actually sustains you.

The tiramisu-inspired layers of coffee-flavored chia yogurt and melted white chocolate create something that looks and smells amazing - hopefully better than you do at 1:00 a.m., as I like to joke with myself in those dark early morning hours. The sweetness is adjustable using monk fruit sweetener for zero calories, or honey and maple syrup if you prefer. Either way, you've got a breakfast that supports you through even the hardest days. This has become my go-to meal prep for those weeks when I know I'm going to be working long hours.
Why You'll Love This:
- Packs 52g of protein to keep you full through 12-hour work shifts
- Made the night before so it's ready to grab and go when you wake up
- Includes a shot of espresso for that essential caffeine boost on early mornings
- Adjustable sweetness using monk fruit, honey, or maple syrup based on preference
- Takes only 10 minutes to prepare before letting it set overnight
- Perfect for meal prep - make several servings for the whole work week
- The white chocolate and coffee layers create tiramisu-inspired flavors you'll actually look forward to
Tips:
- Make multiple servings at once in mason jars for grab-and-go breakfast all week
- Adjust the sweetness to your preference - use more or less monk fruit, honey, or maple syrup
- Dark chocolate instead of white chocolate creates less sweet, more intense flavor
- Add a layer of cocoa powder between layers for authentic tiramisu appearance
- Top with crushed coffee beans for texture and extra coffee flavor
- Greek yogurt is essential for the high protein content - regular yogurt has much less
- Let it set at least 4-6 hours, but overnight is ideal for chia seeds to fully absorb liquid
- Give it a quick stir before eating or enjoy it with the layers still distinct
Ingredients Overview:
The base of this breakfast is 350g of Greek yogurt, which is where most of the impressive 52g of protein comes from. Greek yogurt is essential here - regular yogurt is too thin and doesn't have nearly the protein density you need to get through those long shifts. Mixed with 40g of chia seeds, which absorb liquid overnight to create a pudding-like texture, you get a thick, satisfying breakfast base that keeps you full for hours.

Monk fruit sweetener provides sweetness without calories and won't spike your blood sugar, making it perfect for sustained energy through long shifts. But I want to emphasize you can adjust this to your preference - use honey, maple syrup, agave, or regular sugar instead. The amount is completely flexible based on how sweet you like things. I tend to use less sweetener than most people, but you do you.

A shot of espresso (or very strong coffee) gets mixed right into the chia-yogurt base. This isn't just for flavor - it's the caffeine boost you need when waking up at 1:00 a.m. to go to work. Let it cool slightly before mixing so it doesn't curdle the yogurt - learned that one the hard way!

The white chocolate layer is made with 30g of melted white chocolate mixed with a small amount of Greek yogurt to create a smooth, pourable consistency. This adds the "tiramisu" element with its sweet, vanilla-like flavor that contrasts beautifully with the coffee. It's what makes this feel like a treat instead of just another protein breakfast, and trust me, when you're waking up in the middle of the night, you need something to look forward to!
Instructions Overview:
In a bowl or container (mason jars work great for individual portions and grab-and-go convenience), combine the Greek yogurt and chia seeds, mixing everything together thoroughly so the chia seeds are evenly distributed. Add monk fruit sweetener to taste - start with a small amount and add more if you want it sweeter. Remember you can adjust this based on your preferences.

Brew a shot of espresso or make very strong coffee and let it cool slightly so it doesn't curdle the yogurt - this is important! Mix it into the chia-yogurt mixture and stir well to incorporate. The coffee flavor should be noticeable but not overwhelming.

In a separate small bowl, melt the white chocolate using the microwave in 15-second intervals, stirring between each, until completely melted and smooth. Mix a small amount of Greek yogurt into the melted white chocolate to create a smooth, pourable consistency. You want it thin enough to create layers or swirls but not so thin it just disappears into the chia mixture.

Create layers in your serving container - you can do distinct stripes of the chia mixture and white chocolate yogurt, create swirls for a marbled effect, or whatever pattern makes you happy. Get creative and have fun with it. Cover your container and place it in the refrigerator to let it set overnight or for at least 4-6 hours. The chia seeds need time to absorb the liquid and swell, creating that pudding-like texture.

In the morning when your alarm goes off at an ungodly hour, grab your prepared breakfast from the fridge. It should smell amazing and look even better. You've got 52g of protein and a shot of espresso on your side - you can totally get through that 12-hour shift!
FAQ:
**Can I use regular yogurt instead of Greek?**
You can, but Greek yogurt is really essential for the high protein content that makes this so filling. Regular yogurt has significantly less protein and won't keep you full through long shifts. I need that protein to sustain me, so I always use Greek.

**What if I don't have monk fruit sweetener?**
Use honey, maple syrup, agave, or regular sugar instead. Adjust the amount to your taste preferences. The monk fruit is just a zero-calorie option that I personally like, but any sweetener works. Start with 1-2 tablespoons and adjust from there.

**How long does this last in the fridge?**
Make it on Sunday for the work week - it will last 3-4 days refrigerated in airtight containers. I usually make multiple servings at once in individual mason jars so I just grab one each morning. It's a lifesaver during busy weeks.

Name: High-Protein Greek Yogurt Crescent Rolls
Short Description: Homemade crescent rolls made with Greek yogurt for added protein and a delicious flaky texture - my healthier take on the classic Pillsbury rolls that turned out so much better than expected.
Introduction:
If you've ever craved those buttery, flaky Pillsbury crescent rolls but wished they packed more of a protein punch, this recipe is about to become your new favorite. I've created a brilliant homemade version that uses Greek yogurt to boost the protein content while maintaining all that irresistible flakiness you love. These turned out so much better than I expected - they have all the delicious flaky-ness you could want from a crescent roll.

What makes these special is the lamination technique borrowed from puff pastry making. By incorporating grated butter through a series of folds, you create those signature layers that make crescent rolls so addictive. The Greek yogurt not only adds protein but also keeps the rolls incredibly tender and moist. Whether you're making them for breakfast alongside scrambled eggs or as a side dish for soup, these rolls come together surprisingly quickly in just 30 minutes.

The best part? You can make these in an air fryer for convenience, or bake them traditionally in the oven. Either way, you'll end up with golden, flaky crescents that are actually good for you. I make these at least once a week now because they're so versatile and satisfying - they've completely replaced store-bought crescent rolls in my house.
Why You'll Love This:
- Higher protein content - Greek yogurt adds 5-6g protein per roll compared to traditional crescent rolls
- Simple ingredients - Just five pantry staples create bakery-worthy results
- Quick preparation - Ready in 30 minutes from start to finish
- Versatile cooking methods - Works in both air fryer and conventional oven
- Meal prep friendly - Freeze unbaked crescents and cook fresh whenever you need them
Tips:
- Use full-fat Greek yogurt for the best texture and flakiness
- Keep the butter cold when grating - this is crucial for creating those flaky layers
- Don't overwork the dough or it will become tough instead of tender
- Brush with melted butter before cooking for extra richness and golden color
- Try adding herbs like rosemary or garlic powder to the dough for savory variations
- Store unbaked crescents in the freezer for quick future meals
Ingredients Overview:
Greek yogurt is the star player here, bringing both protein and moisture to create tender, delicious rolls. You'll want to use a thick, full-fat Greek yogurt for the best results - the consistency matters for creating a workable dough. I've tried this with low-fat Greek yogurt and it just doesn't produce the same flaky texture, so it's worth splurging on the full-fat version.

All-purpose flour provides structure, while baking powder gives these rolls their lift without requiring yeast or long rise times. A touch of salt enhances all the flavors. The grated butter is what creates those beautiful flaky layers through the folding technique - keep it cold from the fridge and grate it right before using. Unsalted butter is preferred so you can control the salt level in the dough, but salted works in a pinch. The key to getting those perfect layers is keeping everything cold and not overworking the dough - treat it gently and you'll be rewarded with incredible flakiness.
Instructions Overview:
Start by making the dough base - mix together Greek yogurt, all-purpose flour, baking powder, and salt in a large bowl. Stir until the mixture comes together and forms a cohesive ball of dough. It should be slightly sticky but workable - if it's too wet, add a tiny bit more flour.

Turn the dough out onto a lightly floured surface and flatten it into a rectangle shape using your hands or a rolling pin. Grate cold butter directly onto the center of the dough rectangle, then fold the two long sides over the butter to enclose it completely. Use a rolling pin to gently seal the dough and press out the butter throughout - the goal is to distribute the butter in thin layers without working the dough too much.

Fold the dough in half and roll it out gently again with the rolling pin - you're creating layers with each fold, similar to making puff pastry. Repeat this process one more time for a total of three folds, which creates multiple butter layers for maximum flakiness.

Using a knife or pizza cutter, cut the dough diagonally into triangles - you should get about 8 triangles. Starting from the wide end of each triangle, roll the dough toward the point to create the classic crescent shape. Place each rolled crescent with the point tucked underneath to prevent it from unrolling during cooking.

Place crescents in the air fryer basket at 350°F, making sure they're not touching, and air fry for 15 minutes until golden brown and cooked through. Remove from the air fryer and let cool for just a minute or two before serving - these are best enjoyed warm when the layers are at their flakiest!
FAQ:
**Can I use a different type of yogurt?**
Greek yogurt works best due to its thick consistency and high protein content. Regular yogurt is too watery and will make the dough wet and difficult to work with. If you only have regular yogurt, strain it through cheesecloth for a few hours to thicken it first, but honestly it's just easier to use Greek yogurt.

**Do I need an air fryer to make these?**
Not at all! You can bake these in a conventional oven at 375°F for 12-15 minutes until golden brown. The air fryer is just convenient and a bit faster, but the oven works beautifully too and actually gives you more even browning.

**Can I freeze these crescent rolls?**
Yes! Freeze the unbaked, shaped crescents on a baking sheet until solid, then transfer to a freezer bag. When you're ready to eat them, cook directly from frozen, adding a few extra minutes to the cooking time. This makes them perfect for meal prep - I always have a batch in my freezer for quick breakfasts or dinner sides.
