Author: Holistic Bravo
Recipe Card Ingredients:
- 100g Greek yogurt (full-fat works best, about 1/3 cup)
- 100g all-purpose flour (about 3/4 cup, plus extra for dusting)
- 1 teaspoon baking powder
- 1 teaspoon salt (I use sea salt)
- 2 tablespoons butter, cold from the fridge, unsalted (for grating into dough)
- Optional: 1-2 tablespoons melted butter for brushing before cooking
Recipe Card Instructions:
1. Mix 100g Greek yogurt, 100g flour, 1 teaspoon baking powder, and 1 teaspoon salt in a bowl until a cohesive ball forms. The dough should be slightly sticky but workable.
2. Turn dough onto a lightly floured surface and flatten into a rectangle shape using your hands or a rolling pin. About 8x6 inches works well.
3. Using a box grater, grate 2 tablespoons of cold butter directly onto the center of the dough rectangle. Work quickly so the butter stays cold.
4. Fold the two long sides of the dough over the grated butter to enclose it completely, like folding a letter.
5. Use a rolling pin to gently seal and press the dough, distributing the butter throughout. Don't press too hard - you want layers, not completely mixed butter.
6. Fold the dough in half (like a book) and roll it out gently again with the rolling pin. You're creating layers here.
7. Repeat step 6 one more time: fold the dough in half and roll it out gently. This is your third fold - you now have multiple butter layers for flakiness!
8. Using a knife or pizza cutter, cut the dough diagonally into 8 triangles. Try to make them fairly equal in size.
9. Starting from the wide end of each triangle, roll the dough toward the point to create the classic crescent shape. Place each with the point tucked underneath so it doesn't unroll.
10. Place crescents in air fryer basket at 350°F, making sure they're not touching. Air fry for 15 minutes until golden brown and cooked through. For oven method: bake at 375°F for 12-15 minutes.
11. Optional but recommended: Brush with melted butter right when they come out for extra richness and shine. Serve warm and enjoy the flakiness!
Nutrition: Each roll contains approximately 120 calories, 5-6g protein, 4g fat, 16g carbohydrates, and trace amounts of calcium and B vitamins from the Greek yogurt.

Name: Crispy Tofu Lettuce Wraps
Short Description: Light, flavorful, and protein-packed vegan lettuce wraps featuring crispy air-fried tofu, crunchy carrot slaw, and a savory peanut sauce made with peanut butter powder. At 18-20g protein per serving, these are seriously satisfying.
Introduction:
These plant-based lettuce wraps are proof that vegan meals can be incredibly satisfying and full of flavor without weighing you down. I've created a brilliant combination of textures and flavors that come together with barely any effort - crispy tofu cubes coated in a delicious peanut powder sauce paired with a tangy, sweet carrot salad, all wrapped in cool, crisp lettuce leaves.

The genius here is using peanut butter powder instead of regular peanut butter. This keeps the protein high while cutting down on fat, creating a sauce that's both rich and light at the same time. The carrot salad adds that essential crunch and brightness with its combination of red wine vinegar, hot honey, and fresh lemon juice. It's the perfect counterpoint to the savory, umami-rich tofu.

Whether you're fully plant-based or just looking to incorporate more meatless meals into your rotation, these wraps deliver serious protein (18-20g per serving!) while feeling fresh and light. They're perfect for lunch or dinner when you want something satisfying that won't leave you feeling overly full. I make these at least twice a week because they're so easy and always hit the spot - my non-vegan friends actually request them now!
Why You'll Love This:
- High protein and plant-based - 18-20g protein per serving from tofu and peanut powder
- Quick and easy - Ready in 40 minutes with simple preparation
- Incredible texture contrast - Crispy tofu meets crunchy slaw and tender lettuce
- Meal prep friendly - Components can be made ahead and assembled when ready
- Light but filling - Satisfying without being heavy, perfect for lunch or dinner
Tips:
- Press tofu for 15-20 minutes before cooking for maximum crispiness
- Don't skip the air bubbles removal when making the peanut sauce - it ensures smooth coating
- Make the carrot salad a day ahead to let the flavors develop
- Butter lettuce is ideal for its delicate texture, but romaine works great for extra crunch
- Add sriracha or chili garlic sauce to the peanut sauce if you like heat
- Try adding sliced cucumber, avocado, or fresh herbs like cilantro and mint
- Can pan-fry tofu instead of air frying if you don't have an air fryer
Ingredients Overview:
The magic ingredient here is peanut butter powder, which is a total game-changer for making high-protein, lower-fat sauces. When you mix it with soy sauce, maple syrup, rice wine vinegar, and black sesame seeds, you get this incredibly flavorful coating that clings to the crispy tofu. If you've never used peanut butter powder before, you'll be amazed at how it reconstitutes into a rich, peanutty sauce with just a splash of water. I keep a jar of this in my pantry at all times now.

For the tofu, one block is perfect - make sure to drain it well (or better yet, press it) before air frying to achieve maximum crispiness. I learned the hard way that skipping the pressing step results in softer tofu that doesn't get as golden and crunchy.

The carrot salad is simple but impactful: shredded carrots, thinly sliced red onion, and fresh parsley get dressed with red wine vinegar for tang, hot honey for a sweet kick with subtle heat, fresh lemon juice for brightness, and salt and pepper to bring it all together. Let it sit for at least 15-20 minutes while the tofu cooks so the flavors can meld.

Butter lettuce leaves make the perfect delicate wrap vessels - their soft, pliable texture is ideal for wrapping around the filling without tearing. If you prefer more structural integrity or extra crunch, romaine or iceberg lettuce cups work beautifully too. I like butter lettuce for the elegance, but use whatever you have!
Instructions Overview:
Start by making the carrot salad so the flavors have time to develop while you prepare the tofu. In a medium bowl, combine shredded carrots, thinly sliced red onion, and fresh parsley. Add red wine vinegar, hot honey, fresh lemon juice, salt, and pepper. Toss everything together until well combined and set aside - the longer it sits, the better it gets as the flavors marry.

Drain your block of tofu and cut it into bite-sized cubes. For extra crispy tofu (which I highly recommend), press it first to remove excess moisture - wrap it in a clean kitchen towel or paper towels, place something heavy on top, and let it sit for 15-20 minutes. Place the tofu cubes in your air fryer basket in a single layer and cook at 400°F for 25 minutes until golden brown and crispy on all sides.

While the tofu cooks, make the peanut sauce by whisking together peanut butter powder, soy sauce, maple syrup, rice wine vinegar, and black sesame seeds in a bowl. The mixture will be quite thick at first. Add water one tablespoon at a time, whisking between additions, until you reach a pourable but still thick consistency that will coat the tofu - you want it to cling, not drip off.

Once your tofu comes out of the air fryer all golden and crispy, immediately toss it in the peanut sauce mixture. Use a spoon or spatula to ensure every piece gets well coated. To assemble, take a butter lettuce leaf and lay it flat in your palm, add a generous spoonful of the carrot salad in the center, top with several pieces of the peanut-coated tofu, then fold the lettuce around the filling like a taco or burrito. Eat immediately while the tofu is still warm and crispy!
FAQ:
**Can I use regular peanut butter instead of peanut butter powder?**
Yes, but use about 3-4 tablespoons and thin with more water or soy sauce to reach the right consistency. Keep in mind the texture will be richer and the calorie content higher since regular peanut butter has much more fat than the powder version. I prefer the powder for this recipe specifically because it keeps things lighter.

**How do I press tofu properly?**
Wrap the entire block of tofu in a clean kitchen towel or several layers of paper towels. Place it on a plate and set something heavy on top - a cast iron skillet, a few cans, or a heavy pot works perfectly. Let it sit for 15-20 minutes to remove excess moisture, which helps achieve that extra-crispy texture. It's worth the wait!

**Can I bake the tofu instead of air frying?**
Absolutely! Preheat your oven to 400°F and spread the tofu cubes on a parchment-lined baking sheet. Bake for 30-35 minutes, flipping the pieces halfway through, until they're golden brown and crispy on all sides. The oven method works great - I used it for years before getting an air fryer.

- High Protein Veggie Wraps
- Mediterranean Veggie Wraps
Author: Holistic Bravo
Recipe Card Ingredients:
**Carrot Salad:**
- 5 medium carrots, shredded (about 2-3 cups shredded)
- ¼ red onion, thinly sliced (about 1/4 cup)
- Large handful fresh parsley, roughly chopped (about 1/4 cup)
- 1 tablespoon red wine vinegar (any vinegar works but red wine is my favorite here)
- 1 tablespoon hot honey (regular honey works too, but the spice kick is nice)
- 1 lemon, juiced (about 2-3 tablespoons fresh juice)
- 1 teaspoon salt
- 1 teaspoon black pepper (freshly ground is best)

**Tofu:**
- 1 block firm or extra-firm tofu (about 14-16 oz), drained and cubed
- ½ cup peanut butter powder (I use PB2 or similar brand)
- 1 tablespoon soy sauce (low-sodium works great)
- 1 tablespoon maple syrup (or honey, agave)
- ½ tablespoon rice wine vinegar (or any vinegar)
- 1 tablespoon black sesame seeds (white sesame seeds work too)
- Water, as needed to thin the sauce (about 2-4 tablespoons)

**For Assembly:**
- Butter lettuce leaves (about 8-10 leaves, or 1 head)
- Optional add-ins: sliced cucumber, avocado, fresh cilantro, mint, sriracha
Recipe Card Instructions:
1. Make the carrot salad first: Combine 5 shredded carrots, ¼ sliced red onion, and handful of fresh parsley in a bowl. Add 1 tablespoon red wine vinegar, 1 tablespoon hot honey, juice of 1 lemon, 1 teaspoon salt, and 1 teaspoon pepper. Toss well and set aside to let flavors develop.
2. Drain tofu block and cut into bite-sized cubes (about 3/4 inch pieces). For maximum crispiness, press tofu first: wrap in kitchen towel, place something heavy on top, let sit 15-20 minutes.
3. Place tofu cubes in air fryer basket in a single layer - don't overcrowd or they won't get crispy. Air fry at 400°F for 25 minutes until golden brown and crispy on all sides. Shake basket halfway through if you remember.
4. While tofu cooks, make peanut sauce: In a bowl, whisk together ½ cup peanut butter powder, 1 tablespoon soy sauce, 1 tablespoon maple syrup, ½ tablespoon rice wine vinegar, and 1 tablespoon black sesame seeds. Mixture will be very thick.
5. Add water to the peanut sauce one tablespoon at a time, whisking between each addition, until you reach a thick but pourable consistency. I usually use about 3-4 tablespoons total. You want it to coat the tofu, not drip off.
6. When tofu comes out of air fryer golden and crispy, immediately toss it in the peanut sauce. Use a spoon or spatula to coat every piece well. The residual heat helps the sauce cling.
7. Assemble wraps: Take a butter lettuce leaf and lay it flat in your palm. Add a generous spoonful of carrot salad in the center.
8. Top carrot salad with 4-6 pieces of peanut-coated tofu (or however much you want - I like mine loaded!).
9. Optional: Add sliced cucumber, avocado, fresh cilantro or mint, or a drizzle of sriracha if you like heat.
10. Fold the lettuce around the filling like a taco or burrito. Eat immediately while tofu is warm and crispy - the texture contrast is perfection!
Nutrition: Each serving provides approximately 280 calories, 18-20g protein, 8g fat, 32g carbohydrates, 6g fiber, and significant vitamin A from carrots.

Name: Rosemary Crackers & Fresh Hummus
Short Description: These homemade rosemary crackers paired with creamy hummus are my go-to when I want a wholesome Mediterranean snack that's both simple and satisfying.
Introduction:
There's something incredibly satisfying about making your own crackers and hummus from scratch. When I'm craving a wholesome snack, this combination always hits the spot - and it comes together so much more easily than you might think. These homemade crackers have that perfect crispy snap, infused with fragrant rosemary that makes my kitchen smell amazing while they bake.

The secret to these crackers is using a combination of oat flour and all-purpose flour. This blend gives them better texture and a subtle nutty flavor while adding extra nutrition. And here's a pro tip that'll save you money: just grind your own rolled oats in a food processor or blender to make fresh oat flour. You can get an entire container of whole rolled oats for a few dollars versus paying an upcharge for pre-packaged oat flour.

Paired with creamy, classic hummus made with just four essential ingredients, this snack feels indulgent while being genuinely nutritious. Whether I'm serving these at a party, packing them for lunch, or just enjoying an afternoon snack, these crackers and hummus prove that homemade doesn't have to mean complicated. I love how the earthy rosemary complements the rich, nutty hummus - it's a flavor combination that never gets old.

What I appreciate most about this recipe is how it demystifies homemade crackers. So many people think crackers are complicated or require special equipment, but really, if you can roll out cookie dough, you can make these. The dough comes together in minutes, and the rolling process is actually quite meditative. Plus, there's something deeply satisfying about breaking apart a sheet of perfectly crispy homemade crackers.
Why You'll Love This:
- **Budget-friendly** - I make my own oat flour by grinding whole oats for a fraction of the cost
- **Minimal ingredients** - Simple pantry staples create impressive results
- **Quick preparation** - Ready in 35 minutes from start to finish
- **Great for meal prep** - Crackers stay crisp for days, hummus lasts a week in the fridge
- **Customizable** - Easy to add your favorite herbs and spices
Tips:
- Grind whole rolled oats in a food processor for fresh, inexpensive oat flour
- Roll the dough as thin as possible for extra-crispy crackers
- I keep the dough cold while working with it for easier rolling and crisper results
- Experiment with different herbs like thyme, oregano, or za'atar
- Add garlic powder to the cracker dough for extra savory flavor
- For the hummus, I always add lemon juice for tanginess and brightness
- Try flavor variations like roasted red pepper or sun-dried tomato hummus
- Store crackers in an airtight container at room temperature for up to a week
Ingredients Overview:
The cracker dough starts with a simple but effective flour blend. For the oat flour, seriously, just grind your own rolled oats - it takes 30 seconds and saves you so much money. Combined with all-purpose flour, you get structure and crispness along with that wholesome oat flavor. Olive oil adds richness and helps create those crispy layers. A touch of sugar balances the savory elements, while salt enhances everything. Fresh rosemary is the star herb here, bringing that distinctive pine-like aroma and flavor, though dried rosemary works perfectly too if that's what you have on hand. Cold water brings the dough together - I add it slowly so I don't over-saturate.

For the hummus, you need just four essential ingredients. A can of drained chickpeas forms the creamy base. Tahini (sesame seed paste) is absolutely essential for authentic hummus flavor and that smooth, rich texture. Olive oil adds silkiness and more Mediterranean flavor. Sea salt brings out all the other flavors. If you want, top your finished hummus with a sprinkle of paprika for color, an extra drizzle of olive oil for richness, and some fresh parsley for brightness.

I've learned over time that the quality of your tahini really matters here - don't skimp on it. A good tahini should be smooth, not grainy or bitter. I also like to use a good quality extra virgin olive oil since it's such a prominent flavor in both the crackers and the hummus. And for the rosemary, fresh is wonderful, but honestly, dried works beautifully too. I always have both on hand so I can make these crackers anytime the craving hits.
Instructions Overview:
The process starts with preheating your oven and mixing together your dry ingredients for the crackers. I combine the oat flour (freshly ground if possible), all-purpose flour, sugar, salt, and rosemary in a large bowl. Then I work in the olive oil until the mixture becomes crumbly like coarse sand. This step is important because you want the oil evenly distributed throughout the flour.

Next comes the water addition, which I do gradually and carefully. The key is to add just enough water for the dough to come together into a cohesive ball - too much and your crackers will be tough instead of crispy. Once the dough forms, I roll it out as thinly as possible on a well-floured surface. The thinner you can roll it, the crispier your crackers will be. I usually aim for about 1/8 inch or even thinner.

After cutting the crackers into your desired shapes, they bake at 375°F for about 20 minutes until golden and crisp. While they're cooling (and they do need to cool completely to reach maximum crispiness), I make the hummus. I simply blend together drained chickpeas, tahini, olive oil, and salt in my food processor until completely smooth. If it's too thick, I add water a tablespoon at a time until it reaches that perfect creamy consistency.

The whole process is remarkably straightforward. I find that making the crackers is the perfect weekend afternoon activity - you can listen to music or a podcast while rolling and cutting. And the hummus literally takes two minutes to blend together. Before you know it, you've got a homemade snack that rivals anything you'd buy at the store, and it tastes so much fresher.
FAQ:
**Can I use only all-purpose flour instead of the oat/AP blend?**
Yes, you can use 1½ cups of all-purpose flour instead. However, I find the oat flour adds better texture, a subtle nutty flavor, and more nutrition. The combination is worth it if you have oats on hand.

**How do I store the crackers to keep them crispy?**
I store the completely cooled crackers in an airtight container at room temperature. They should stay crispy for up to one week if the container is properly sealed. Don't refrigerate them or they'll get soft.

**Can I make hummus without tahini?**
Tahini is traditional and provides the authentic flavor and creamy texture that defines hummus. However, if you don't have it, you can substitute with extra olive oil or a nut butter like almond or cashew butter, though the flavor will be noticeably different.

Author: Holistic Bravo
Recipe Card Ingredients:
**Rosemary Crackers:**
- ¾ cup oat flour (I grind rolled oats in my food processor for 30 seconds)
- ¾ cup all-purpose flour, plus extra for rolling
- 3 tbsp extra virgin olive oil
- 1 tsp granulated sugar
- 1 tsp sea salt
- 1 tbsp fresh rosemary, finely chopped (or 1 tbsp dried rosemary)
- Cold water, approximately ⅓-½ cup (add gradually)

**Fresh Hummus:**
- 1 can (15 oz) chickpeas, drained and rinsed
- 2 tbsp extra virgin olive oil, plus more for drizzling
- 2 tbsp tahini (sesame seed paste)
- 1 tsp sea salt
- 2-4 tbsp water for consistency (optional)

**Optional Toppings:**
- Paprika, extra virgin olive oil, fresh parsley (chopped)
Recipe Card Instructions:
**Crackers:**
1. Preheat oven to 375°F and line a baking sheet with parchment paper.
2. In a large bowl, whisk together oat flour, all-purpose flour, sugar, salt, and rosemary until well combined.
3. Add olive oil and mix with a fork or your hands until the mixture becomes crumbly and resembles coarse sand.
4. Slowly add cold water, a tablespoon at a time, mixing as you go. Stop when the dough comes together into a cohesive ball (you may not need all the water).
5. On a generously floured surface, roll the dough out as thinly as possible - aim for ⅛ inch thickness or thinner for maximum crispiness.
6. Cut into desired shapes using a knife, pizza cutter, or cookie cutters. Alternatively, score the dough into squares but leave whole for easier handling.
7. Transfer to prepared baking sheet and bake for 20 minutes until golden brown and crisp. Let cool completely on the sheet - they'll crisp up even more as they cool.

**Hummus:**
1. Add drained chickpeas, olive oil, tahini, and sea salt to a food processor or high-speed blender.
2. Blend on high for 2-3 minutes until completely smooth and creamy, scraping down sides as needed.
3. If too thick, add water one tablespoon at a time while blending until you reach desired creamy consistency.
4. Transfer to a serving bowl and create a shallow well in the center with the back of a spoon.
5. Drizzle with olive oil, sprinkle with paprika, and garnish with fresh parsley if desired.
6. Serve alongside cooled crackers and enjoy!
Nutrition: Each serving provides approximately 180 calories, 6-8g protein, 9g fat, 20g carbohydrates, 4g fiber, and significant iron and B vitamins from chickpeas.

Name: Chinese-Style Steamed Eggs
Short Description: This is my go-to breakfast for slow mornings - silky, custard-like steamed eggs that literally melt in your mouth, nothing like scrambled or fried eggs.
Introduction:
If you've never tried Chinese-style steamed eggs, you're in for a treat. This is my go-to breakfast for slow mornings when self-care is the priority. These eggs have a silky, custard-like texture that literally melts in your mouth - nothing like scrambled or fried eggs. The texture is closer to a savory flan or silken tofu, impossibly smooth and delicate.

This traditional Asian preparation transforms simple eggs into something truly special. The secret is in the gentle steaming method and the precise ratio of eggs to water. While the ingredient list is minimal - just eggs, water, and some flavorful toppings - the technique requires a bit of attention to detail. You'll want to remove all those air bubbles for a perfectly smooth surface, and the steaming needs to happen gently over low heat so the eggs set slowly without becoming rubbery.

My first time making these was a revelation. They're perfect for those mornings when I want something comforting and protein-rich but also light and delicate. Served with soy sauce, rice vinegar, and sesame seeds, with optional additions like kimchi for crunch or chillies for heat, this is a breakfast that feels both nourishing and indulgent.

I love how this recipe represents the best of Asian cooking - taking the simplest ingredients and using technique to transform them into something extraordinary. There's a meditative quality to the preparation, from carefully whisking the eggs to meticulously removing every air bubble. It forces you to slow down and be present, which is exactly what I need on those mornings when I'm making this dish.
Why You'll Love This:
- **Incredibly silky texture** - Unlike any other egg preparation I've tried, truly custard-like
- **Simple ingredients** - Just eggs and water create something extraordinary
- **High protein** - 12-14g protein per serving keeps me satisfied all morning
- **Gentle and comforting** - Perfect for slow mornings or when I need comfort food
- **Customizable toppings** - I add kimchi, chillies, scallions, or keep it simple depending on my mood
Tips:
- Removing ALL air bubbles is the key to a smooth, glossy surface - I take my time with this step
- Don't overcook - eggs should still jiggle slightly in the center when done
- I use low heat for gentle cooking; high heat will create holes and bubbles
- Cover the bowl with foil or plastic wrap with holes poked in it to prevent water from dripping onto the eggs
- Adjust water amount for different textures: less water makes firmer eggs, more water makes softer eggs
- Try adding a splash of dashi or chicken broth to the water for extra umami flavor
- I often serve this over rice for a complete meal
- Top with crispy fried shallots or garlic for textural contrast
Ingredients Overview:
This recipe is deceptively simple with just two main ingredients. You'll need 2 fresh eggs and 1 cup of water - that 1:1 ratio of eggs to water is crucial for achieving the signature silky, custard-like texture. I use warm water (not cold) because it helps the eggs cook more evenly throughout. The magic happens in the steaming technique rather than in complicated ingredients.

For serving, soy sauce and seasoned rice vinegar are essential. The soy sauce provides that savory, umami depth, while the rice vinegar adds a subtle tang that brightens the rich eggs. A sprinkle of sesame seeds adds a touch of nuttiness and visual appeal. Beyond that, I customize with optional additions based on my mood. Kimchi adds crunch and fermented tang. Fresh or crushed chillies bring heat. Green scallions contribute freshness, texture, and a mild onion flavor that complements the eggs beautifully.

I've experimented with this recipe a lot, and I've found that the quality of your eggs really matters here. Since there are so few ingredients, everything is on display. I try to use the freshest eggs I can get. And for the toppings, I love having a variety on hand so I can customize based on what I'm craving that morning. Sometimes I want it simple with just soy sauce and sesame seeds. Other mornings, I load it up with kimchi and chillies for a more robust flavor profile.
Instructions Overview:
The process starts with setting up your steaming apparatus - I bring a pot of water to a boil and place a steamer rack or basket inside. While that's heating, I whisk together the eggs and warm water until well combined. This is where patience becomes important.

The critical step is removing every single air bubble from the egg mixture. I use a spoon to carefully skim the surface, and I take my time with this because those bubbles will create imperfections in the finished dish. Once the mixture is perfectly smooth, I pour it into a heatproof bowl and place it in the steamer.

The steaming technique varies based on your stove. For very hot stoves, I steam on low for just 2 minutes, then turn off the heat completely and let the residual heat finish the cooking for another 7-8 minutes. For regular stoves, I steam on low heat for 8-10 minutes total. The eggs should be set but still have that gorgeous jiggle in the center, similar to panna cotta.

Once they're done, I carefully remove the bowl from the steamer and add my toppings. A drizzle of soy sauce and rice vinegar, a sprinkle of sesame seeds, and whatever optional additions I'm in the mood for that day. The whole process is surprisingly quick once you get the hang of it, and the result is always impressive.
FAQ:
**Why are my steamed eggs not smooth and have lots of holes?**
This happens when air bubbles aren't completely removed before steaming, or if the heat is too high. I make sure to carefully remove ALL bubbles from the surface before steaming, and I keep the heat gentle and low throughout the cooking process.

**Can I use a microwave instead of steaming?**
You can, but the results won't be nearly as silky and smooth. If you must use a microwave, cook on 50% power in 30-second intervals, checking frequently, until just set. The texture won't be quite the same, but it will still be softer than regular scrambled eggs.

**How do I know when the steamed eggs are done?**
The eggs should be just set but still jiggly in the center - think of the texture of silken tofu or panna cotta. If I gently shake the bowl, the surface should have a slight wobble. They'll continue to firm up a bit as they cool.

Author: Holistic Bravo
Recipe Card Ingredients:
- 2 large fresh eggs
- 1 cup warm water (not cold, not hot)
- Soy sauce, to taste (I use about 1 tbsp)
- Seasoned rice vinegar, to taste (I use about 1 tsp)
- Sesame seeds, for garnish (about ½ tsp)

**Optional Toppings:**
- Kimchi (2-3 tbsp chopped)
- Fresh or crushed red chillies (to taste)
- Green scallions, thinly sliced (1-2 tbsp)
Recipe Card Instructions:
1. Fill a pot with water (about 2 inches deep) and bring to a boil. Set up a steamer rack or basket inside the pot.
2. In a bowl, gently whisk 2 eggs with a fork until well beaten but not overly frothy.
3. Add 1 cup warm water to the eggs and whisk together until completely combined.
4. Using a spoon, carefully skim and remove ALL air bubbles from the surface - take your time with this step, it's critical for smooth texture.
5. Pour the egg mixture into a heatproof bowl (one that fits comfortably in your steamer).
6. Place the bowl into the steamer. For very hot stoves: steam on low heat for 2 minutes, then turn OFF heat and let steam covered for 7-8 minutes. For regular stoves: steam on low heat for 8-10 minutes.
7. Check for doneness - eggs should be just set with a gentle jiggle in the center, like panna cotta. Don't overcook!
8. Carefully remove the bowl from the steamer using oven mitts.
9. Drizzle with soy sauce and seasoned rice vinegar to taste.
10. Sprinkle with sesame seeds and add optional toppings if desired.
11. Serve immediately while hot and enjoy the silky texture!
Nutrition: Each serving provides approximately 150 calories, 12-14g protein, 10g fat, 3g carbohydrates, and essential vitamins including D, B12, selenium, and choline.

Name: Spinach Ravioli
Short Description: When I had wilting spinach and arugula, I transformed them into this gorgeous green pasta with a creamy egg-Parmesan filling - it's like eating a salad but so much better!
Introduction:
This is like eating a salad but so much better! When I had a container of baby spinach and arugula starting to wilt, instead of tossing it, I transformed it into this gorgeous green pasta. Homemade ravioli might sound intimidating, but this dish actually comes together in 30 minutes or less - faster than you'd think.

The brilliance of this recipe is threefold. First, the spinach gets used in two ways: blended into the pasta dough for vibrant green noodles, and wilted in the sauce for serving. Second, the filling is made using a clever double-boiler technique that creates a rich, custard-like egg-Parmesan mixture that stays creamy inside the ravioli. Third, you're getting protein from both the eggs and the spinach, all packaged in gorgeous homemade pasta pillows.

Using up greens that are starting to wilt is the perfect application here - the blending and cooking process revives them completely. You'd never know these weren't made with the freshest greens from the farmers market. Whether you have a ravioli mold or just use a glass to cut circles, this recipe proves that homemade pasta is absolutely achievable on a weeknight.

I love this recipe because it's both practical and impressive. Practical because it uses up produce that would otherwise go to waste, and impressive because the vibrant green pasta looks restaurant-quality. The double-boiler technique for the filling was a game-changer for me - it creates this luxurious, custard-like texture that's so much more interesting than a traditional ricotta filling. And honestly, making fresh pasta is one of my favorite kitchen activities. There's something so satisfying about working with the dough and seeing those perfect little ravioli pillows come together.
Why You'll Love This:
- **Uses up wilting greens** - I love that this is the perfect way to rescue spinach, arugula, or mixed greens before they go bad
- **Ready in 30 minutes** - Faster than most people think homemade pasta takes
- **High protein** - 22-24g protein per serving keeps me full for hours
- **Beautiful presentation** - Vibrant green pasta looks restaurant-quality
- **Clever filling technique** - The double-boiler method I use creates custard-like eggs without scrambling
Tips:
- Any leafy greens work in this recipe: spinach, arugula, kale, or mixed greens - I use whatever needs to be used up
- If you don't have a ravioli mold, I just cut circles with a glass and fold into half-moons
- Keep extra flour handy while rolling to prevent sticking
- Don't let the egg mixture scramble - I maintain gentle heat and whisk constantly
- The pasta dough should be elastic and smooth; if too sticky, add more flour
- Can substitute ricotta cheese mixed with the eggs for a more traditional filling
- I often add fresh herbs like basil to the sauce for extra flavor
- Reserve extra pasta water to adjust sauce consistency if needed
Ingredients Overview:
The pasta dough starts with about half a container of baby spinach and arugula mix (or any leafy greens I have on hand) blended with 1 cup of water until bright green and smooth. I strain this to remove chunks, then mix it with approximately 2.5 cups of all-purpose flour - I might need a bit more or less depending on how much liquid I have. A pinch of salt enhances the flavor. The dough comes together quickly and rests while I make the filling.

The egg-Parmesan filling is genius in its simplicity. I crack 4 eggs into a bowl set over simmering water (double-boiler style) and whisk continuously as they gently heat. Adding ½ cup of grated Parmesan cheese while whisking creates this incredible custardy paste that's rich and creamy, perfect for stuffing ravioli. The gentle indirect heat prevents scrambling - it's all about patience here.

For the garlic-spinach sauce, I use the remaining half of the greens along with olive oil, minced garlic, salt, and pepper. Adding pasta water to the sauce creates a silky coating that clings to the ravioli. Extra Parmesan, sea salt, and black pepper finish the dish beautifully.

What I love about this ingredient list is how flexible it is. The greens can be any combination of what you have on hand - I've made this with all spinach, all arugula, a mix of both, and even with kale when that's what needed to be used up. The key is the blending technique which turns any green into a beautiful, vibrant pasta liquid. And that double-boiler egg filling? It's become one of my favorite techniques. The texture is so unique and luxurious, miles better than a standard ricotta filling.
Instructions Overview:
The process begins with making the green pasta liquid. I blend half of my greens with water until bright green and smooth, then strain it to remove any chunks. This liquid becomes the base of the pasta dough. I gradually add flour, mixing and kneading until I have a smooth, elastic dough ball. After letting it rest under a bowl, I move on to the filling.

The double-boiler egg filling is where the magic happens. I set a bowl over simmering water and crack eggs into it, whisking constantly as the steam gently heats them. As they begin to thicken, I add Parmesan cheese and continue whisking until the mixture becomes thick and custardy. The key is maintaining gentle heat and constant whisking - this prevents scrambling and creates that perfect custard-like texture.

Once I have my dough and filling ready, I roll out the pasta dough into thin sheets. If I'm using a ravioli mold, I place the first sheet over it, add spoonfuls of the egg-Parmesan custard into each indentation, then cover with a second sheet and press to seal. Without a mold, I cut circles, add filling, fold in half, and seal with a fork. It's a rhythmic process that I actually find quite meditative.

The final steps happen quickly. I wilt the remaining greens with garlic in a pan, boil the ravioli until they float, then combine everything with pasta water to create a silky sauce. The whole process flows beautifully once you've done it once or twice, and the result is always impressive.
FAQ:
**Can I make the pasta dough ahead of time?**
Yes! I wrap the dough tightly in plastic wrap and refrigerate for up to 24 hours. When I'm ready to use it, I let it come to room temperature for about 30 minutes before rolling - this makes it much easier to work with.

**What if I don't have a ravioli mold?**
No problem! I use a glass or round cookie cutter to cut circles from my rolled pasta sheets. I place a spoonful of filling in the center of each circle, fold it in half to make a half-moon shape, and press the edges together firmly. I use a fork to crimp the edges for a decorative seal.

**How do I prevent the egg filling from scrambling?**
The key is gentle, indirect heat from the double boiler. I keep whisking constantly, and if I notice the eggs cooking too quickly or starting to get grainy, I immediately remove the bowl from the heat while continuing to whisk. The residual heat will continue cooking without scrambling.

Author: Holistic Bravo
Recipe Card Ingredients:
**Spinach Pasta Dough:**
- Half container (about 3 cups) baby spinach/arugula mix or any leafy greens
- 1 cup water
- 2.5 cups all-purpose flour, plus extra for rolling and kneading
- Pinch of salt (about ½ tsp)

**Egg-Parmesan Custard Filling:**
- 4 large eggs
- ½ cup Parmesan cheese, freshly grated (use good quality for best flavor)
- Pinch of sea salt

**Garlic-Spinach Sauce:**
- 1 tbsp extra virgin olive oil
- 1 heaping tbsp fresh garlic, minced (about 3-4 cloves)
- 1 tsp sea salt
- 1 tsp black pepper, freshly ground
- Remaining half of greens (about 3 cups)
- 1 cup pasta cooking water, reserved

**For Serving:**
- Extra Parmesan cheese, grated
- Sea salt and black pepper, to taste
Recipe Card Instructions:
1. Add half the greens and 1 cup water to a blender; blend on high until bright green and completely smooth.
2. Pour through a fine-mesh strainer to remove any chunks; discard solids.
3. Pour green liquid into a large bowl. Gradually add flour (start with 1 cup, then add more), mixing with a spoon then your hands until dough forms.
4. Turn out onto a floured surface and knead for 3-5 minutes until smooth and elastic. Cover with a bowl and let rest.
5. For the filling: Bring a pot of water to boil. Place a heatproof bowl over the pot (bowl should not touch water).
6. Crack 4 eggs into the bowl. Add a pinch of salt. Whisk continuously as the steam heats the eggs.
7. As eggs begin to thicken slightly, add ½ cup Parmesan cheese. Continue whisking constantly until mixture becomes thick and custardy like paste (about 5-7 minutes). Remove from heat and set aside.
8. Divide rested dough into 4 pieces. On a well-floured surface, roll each piece very thin into long sheets.
9. If using ravioli mold: Place first sheet over mold, press to create indentations, spoon egg mixture into each, cover with second sheet, press firmly, and cut. If no mold: Cut circles with glass, place filling in center, fold in half, seal edges with fork.
10. Repeat with remaining dough and filling until all ravioli are formed.
11. Heat 1 tbsp olive oil in large pan over medium heat. Add minced garlic and sauté 30 seconds until fragrant.
12. Add remaining greens, 1 tsp salt, and 1 tsp pepper. Cook until greens wilt, about 2-3 minutes.
13. Bring a large pot of salted water to boil. Gently add ravioli and cook until they float to the surface, about 3-4 minutes.
14. Add 1 cup pasta water to the pan with wilted greens.
15. Using a slotted spoon, transfer cooked ravioli directly to the sauce pan. Toss gently until coated.
16. Plate ravioli with sauce, top with extra Parmesan, sea salt, and black pepper. Serve immediately!
Nutrition: Each serving provides approximately 380 calories, 22-24g protein, 12g fat, 45g carbohydrates, 4g fiber, and significant vitamin A, iron, and calcium.

Name: Crispy Rice Bowl with Sweet Potato Tempura
Short Description: When meal prep gets boring, I transform leftover rice and sweet potatoes into something completely different and exciting with crispy textures and bright chimichurri sauce.
Introduction:
Happy New Year! This recipe is all about giving new life to leftovers. Let's be honest - even with the best meal prep intentions, eating the same foods every day can get boring. I get it, and this recipe is my solution: taking leftover rice and sweet potatoes and transforming them into something completely different and exciting.

This might seem like the long way to make dinner, but here's the thing - taking time to transform the leftovers I already have makes them so much more appealing. The thin layer of rice gets incredibly crispy when baked and broiled, creating texture similar to rice cakes but better. The cooked sweet potato becomes indulgent when cut into wedges, breaded tempura-style, and air-fried until golden. And that fresh chimichurri sauce? It brings everything together with bright, herbaceous flavor that makes me forget I'm eating leftovers at all.

This is the kind of recipe I make when I'm really hungry, want something delicious fast, and don't want to waste the food I already have. Plus, with a fried egg on top, I've got complete protein and a meal that's way more satisfying than reheated meal prep.

I developed this recipe during a particularly intense meal prep phase when I found myself staring at containers of rice and sweet potato thinking, "I just can't eat this plain again." Instead of ordering takeout or letting the food go to waste, I started experimenting. The crispy rice technique was a revelation - who knew that leftover rice could become so texturally interesting? And breading sweet potato tempura-style makes it feel like a treat rather than just another serving of roasted vegetables.
Why You'll Love This:
- **Transforms leftovers** - I make meal-prepped foods exciting again
- **Multiple textures** - Crispy rice, crunchy breading, creamy sweet potato, runny egg all in one bowl
- **Quick and satisfying** - Ready in 30 minutes when I'm really hungry
- **Reduces food waste** - Uses up leftover rice and sweet potatoes perfectly
- **Bright and flavorful** - Fresh chimichurri elevates simple ingredients
Tips:
- I spread rice as thin as possible in the baking dish for maximum crispiness
- Works with any type of leftover rice I have - white, brown, or rice/quinoa mix
- Slightly drier leftover rice crisps up better than freshly cooked
- Can pan-fry sweet potato instead of air frying if I prefer
- I often make chimichurri ahead and store in the fridge for up to one week
- Try this technique with other leftover roasted vegetables like butternut squash
- I love adding toppings like avocado, pickled onions, or hot sauce
- Substitute cilantro for parsley in chimichurri for a different flavor profile
Ingredients Overview:
The beauty here is using what I already have. My leftover rice (whether it's plain white rice, brown rice, or even a rice and quinoa mixture like I used in the video) becomes the crispy base. Any cooked sweet potato works - one large sweet potato cut into thick slices or wedges. For breading the sweet potato tempura-style, I need flour for dredging, liquid egg whites (or whole eggs work fine), and panko breadcrumbs for that extra-crispy coating.

The chimichurri sauce is what really makes this dish sing. It comes together quickly in my food processor with 1 tablespoon extra-virgin olive oil, 2 tablespoons white wine vinegar for tang, 1 teaspoon minced garlic, ½ teaspoon sea salt, and spices including ¼ teaspoon each of dried oregano, red pepper flakes, and smoked paprika. About ½ cup (a good handful) of fresh parsley provides that bright green color and fresh herb flavor. I top everything with a fried egg for protein and I've got a complete, satisfying meal.

What I love about this ingredient list is that it's completely flexible. The rice can be any variety, the sweet potato can be freshly roasted or leftover from days ago, and even the chimichurri can be adapted based on what herbs I have on hand. Sometimes I make it with cilantro instead of parsley for a different flavor profile. The key is the technique - that crispy rice base and crunchy breaded sweet potato - which transforms simple ingredients into something special.
Instructions Overview:
The process starts with getting that leftover rice incredibly crispy. I spread it in the thinnest possible layer on a baking dish and bake it at 400°F for 15 minutes, then finish under the broiler for 5 minutes. The key is pressing it down really flat so every grain gets crispy. While the rice is in the oven, I set up my breading station with three bowls: flour, egg whites, and panko breadcrumbs.

I cut my cooked sweet potato into thick slices or wedges - they don't have to be perfect, irregular shapes are fine. Then I bread each piece by dredging in flour, dipping in egg white, and coating thoroughly in panko. The triple coating creates that tempura-style crunch. Into the air fryer they go at 400°F for 12-15 minutes until golden and crispy.

While everything cooks, I make the chimichurri by pulsing all the ingredients in my food processor. The sauce comes together in less than a minute - just 10-12 pulses until combined but still slightly chunky. I fry an egg for protein, then assemble everything in a bowl: crispy rice base, breaded sweet potato pieces, fried egg on top, and a generous drizzle of that bright green chimichurri.

The whole process is actually quite efficient once you get the rhythm down. While the rice bakes, I prep and bread the sweet potato. While the sweet potato air fries, I make the chimichurri. Everything comes together hot and fresh at the same time, and the result is this incredibly satisfying bowl with multiple textures and bright, bold flavors.
FAQ:
**Can I make this without an air fryer?**
Absolutely! I bake the breaded sweet potato pieces at 425°F for 20-25 minutes, flipping them halfway through, until golden and crispy on both sides.

**What if my rice isn't getting crispy?**
Make sure you've spread it in a very thin layer - thickness is the enemy of crispiness here. Also, slightly drier leftover rice works better than freshly cooked rice. Give it the full broil time, watching carefully to prevent burning.

**Can I use store-bought chimichurri?**
Yes! This recipe is all about convenience and using what you have. If you have jarred chimichurri sauce, use it and save yourself some time. The dish will still be delicious.

- Quick Vegetable Fried Rice
- Leftover Pasta with Pesto Mushrooms
- Spaghetti Squash with Bechamel
Author: Holistic Bravo
Recipe Card Ingredients:
**Crispy Rice:**
- 2-3 cups leftover cooked rice (any variety - white, brown, rice/quinoa mix)

**Sweet Potato Tempura:**
- 1 large cooked sweet potato (about 8-10 oz)
- ½ cup all-purpose flour (for dredging)
- ½ cup liquid egg whites (or 2 whole eggs, beaten)
- 1 cup panko breadcrumbs (Japanese-style for extra crispiness)

**Chimichurri Sauce:**
- 1 tbsp extra-virgin olive oil
- 2 tbsp white wine vinegar
- 1 tsp garlic, minced (about 1 clove)
- ½ tsp sea salt
- ¼ tsp dried oregano
- ¼ tsp red pepper flakes
- ¼ tsp smoked paprika
- ½ cup fresh parsley, packed (about 1 small bunch)

**For Assembly:**
- 1 large egg (for frying)
- Oil or cooking spray
Recipe Card Instructions:
1. Preheat oven to 400°F.
2. Spread leftover rice in the thinnest possible layer on a baking dish, pressing down firmly to flatten.
3. Bake at 400°F for 15 minutes, then switch to broil and cook for an additional 5 minutes, watching carefully to prevent burning. Remove and set aside.
4. Cut cooked sweet potato into thick slices or wedge shapes (about ½-inch thick).
5. Set up breading station: Place flour in first bowl, egg whites in second bowl, panko breadcrumbs in third bowl.
6. Bread each sweet potato piece: Dredge in flour (coating all sides), dip in egg white (let excess drip off), coat thoroughly in panko breadcrumbs. Place in air fryer basket.
7. Air fry breaded sweet potato at 400°F for 12-15 minutes until golden brown and crispy. (Oven method: Bake at 425°F for 20-25 minutes, flipping halfway.)
8. While everything cooks, make chimichurri: Add all chimichurri ingredients to food processor or blender. Pulse 10-12 times until combined but still slightly chunky, not completely smooth. Set aside.
9. In a small pan, fry an egg to your preference (I like mine with a runny yolk).
10. Assemble bowl: Place generous portion of crispy rice in a bowl. Add several pieces of sweet potato tempura. Top with fried egg.
11. Drizzle everything generously with chimichurri sauce.
12. Serve immediately while hot and crispy. Enjoy!
Nutrition: Each serving provides approximately 420 calories, 14-16g protein, 15g fat, 58g carbohydrates, 7g fiber, and significant vitamin A and C.

Name: Leftover Pasta with Pesto Mushrooms
Short Description: I don't waste food in my kitchen - after making fresh pasta, I transform the scraps into second dinner with crispy mushrooms and pesto, and honestly it often turns out better than the first dinner.
Introduction:
We don't waste food in this household! After I make fresh pasta - whether it's ravioli, tri-color pasta, or any other shaped creation - there are always scraps left over. The edges, the odd pieces, the trimmings that don't make it into the final dish. Instead of tossing them, I transform them into second dinner, which honestly often turns out better than the first dinner.

This recipe is the perfect example of resourceful cooking. Those irregular pasta scraps might not look picture-perfect, but they taste incredible. The beauty of homemade pasta is that anything you add to it enhances its flavor, and you really can never go wrong with the combination of crispy oyster mushrooms and pesto. The earthy, slightly sweet oyster mushrooms get wonderfully crispy when fried, then get tossed with leftover pesto and starchy pasta water to create this glossy, cohesive sauce that clings to every irregular piece of pasta.

If you've been intimidated by the idea of making fresh pasta because you worry about waste, this recipe should ease your mind. Every scrap has a purpose, and sometimes those scraps become the most satisfying meal of the week.

I love how this recipe represents my whole cooking philosophy: nothing goes to waste, and resourcefulness often leads to the most delicious results. Some of my favorite meals have come from combining leftovers in creative ways. And there's something particularly satisfying about eating pasta I made myself, even if it's the "ugly" scraps. They taste just as good, if not better, than the perfect ravioli I served the night before.
Why You'll Love This:
- **Zero waste cooking** - I use every scrap from my pasta-making projects
- **Quick and easy** - Ready in 20 minutes with minimal effort
- **Incredible flavor** - Crispy mushrooms and pesto are a perfect combination
- **Works with any pasta** - Scraps, store-bought, or intentionally made pasta all work in my kitchen
- **Starchy pasta water magic** - Creates restaurant-quality glossy sauce
Tips:
- Works with any pasta scraps I have - ravioli edges, colored pasta trimmings, irregular shapes
- Don't crowd the pan when frying mushrooms - they need space to crisp up properly
- I salt the pasta water generously (until my arm hurts!)
- Reserve at least 1 cup of pasta water before draining - I'll need it for the sauce
- Any mushroom variety works: oyster, cremini, shiitake, or button mushrooms
- If I don't have leftover pesto, I use jarred pesto or make fresh
- I often add red pepper flakes for heat, or top with freshly grated Parmesan
- Finish with a drizzle of good olive oil for extra richness
Ingredients Overview:
The star here is my leftover pasta - those scraps from ravioli-making, the trimmed edges from colored pasta sheets, or any irregular pieces from pasta projects. Don't have scraps? Any fresh or dried pasta works perfectly too. For the mushrooms, oyster mushrooms are featured in the video (lucky leftovers from a hot pot night with friends), but any variety works great. Oyster mushrooms have a delicate, slightly sweet flavor and become wonderfully crispy when fried.

I need minced garlic - as much as I like, since garlic makes everything better. Leftover pesto sauce is ideal (waste not!), but jarred pesto or freshly made pesto all work beautifully. The secret ingredient is the starchy pasta water, which I reserve before draining the pasta. This starchy water is liquid gold - it helps the pesto thin out and cling to the pasta, creating a glossy, cohesive sauce instead of separated, dry noodles. Olive oil for frying the mushrooms and salt for the pasta water round out this simple but satisfying ingredient list.

What I love about this ingredient list is how forgiving it is. The pasta can be any shape or variety - those irregular scraps I was going to throw away work just as well as pristine store-bought pasta. The mushrooms can be whatever looks good at the market or whatever I have leftover from another meal. And the pesto? Leftover homemade is fantastic, but honestly, a good quality jarred pesto works beautifully too. This is resourceful cooking at its best.
Instructions Overview:
The process starts with bringing a large pot of water to a rolling boil and salting it generously - I'm talking really salting it until my arm hurts. This is where all the seasoning for the pasta happens, so I don't skimp here. While the water heats, I fry the mushrooms in olive oil over medium-high heat, letting them get nice and crispy with golden-brown edges.

Once the mushrooms are beautifully crispy, I add minced garlic and sauté just until fragrant - about 30 seconds to 1 minute, being careful not to let it burn. Then I add my leftover pasta to the boiling water. If using fresh pasta scraps, they cook quickly, usually 2-3 minutes. If using dried pasta, I follow the package directions but cook to al dente.

Here's where the magic happens: I add my leftover pesto to the pan with the mushrooms and garlic, then pour in some of that precious starchy pasta water. The water thins the pesto and helps it become saucy. Before draining the pasta, I reserve at least 1 cup of the cooking water - this is my insurance policy for adjusting the sauce consistency.

I transfer the cooked pasta directly from the pot to the pan using tongs or a spider strainer, letting a little extra water cling to the pasta. Then I toss everything together enthusiastically, adding more pasta water as needed to create a glossy, cohesive sauce that coats every piece of pasta. The whole process is quick, satisfying, and results in second dinner that often outshines the first.
FAQ:
**Can I use store-bought pasta instead of scraps?**
Absolutely! This recipe is fantastic with any pasta. I use whatever shape I have on hand - penne, spaghetti, farfalle, whatever. Just cook it according to package directions and proceed with the recipe.

**What if I don't have leftover pesto?**
No problem! I use jarred pesto from the store (very convenient), make fresh pesto if I have basil and pine nuts on hand, or substitute with olive oil, garlic, and freshly grated Parmesan for a simple aglio e olio style.

**Why is pasta water so important?**
The starch released into the pasta cooking water acts as an emulsifier, helping the sauce cling to the pasta and creating that restaurant-quality glossy coating. It also helps thin out thick sauces like pesto without adding more oil. I never skip reserving that pasta water!

Author: Holistic Bravo
Recipe Card Ingredients:
- 2-3 cups leftover fresh pasta scraps (ravioli edges, colored pasta pieces, or any pasta shape - or substitute with 8 oz store-bought pasta)
- 8 oz package oyster mushrooms (or mushrooms of choice - cremini, shiitake, button)
- 2-3 cloves garlic, minced (about 1 tbsp)
- ¼ cup leftover pesto sauce (or jarred/fresh pesto)
- 1 cup pasta cooking water, reserved (minimum - reserve extra for adjusting)
- Salt for pasta water (about 2 tbsp or "until your arm hurts")
- 2-3 tbsp extra virgin olive oil (for frying mushrooms)

**Optional:**
- Red pepper flakes (for heat)
- Freshly grated Parmesan cheese
- Extra drizzle of olive oil for finishing
Recipe Card Instructions:
1. Bring a large pot of water to a rolling boil. Salt it generously - really salt it until your arm hurts from shaking the container. This is critical for flavorful pasta!
2. While water heats, heat 2-3 tbsp olive oil in a large pan over medium-high heat.
3. Add mushrooms to the hot oil. Fry without moving them too much, allowing them to get crispy with golden-brown edges, about 5-7 minutes.
4. Once mushrooms are beautifully crispy, add minced garlic to the pan. Sauté for 30 seconds to 1 minute until fragrant. Don't let it burn!
5. Add leftover pasta to the boiling salted water. For fresh pasta scraps: cook 2-3 minutes until they float and are tender. For dried pasta: follow package directions, cooking to al dente.
6. While pasta cooks, add ¼ cup pesto to the mushroom and garlic pan. Pour in about ½ cup of the starchy pasta water. Stir to combine and thin the pesto.
7. Before draining pasta, reserve at least 1 cup (or more) of the cooking water. Set aside.
8. Using tongs or a spider strainer, transfer cooked pasta directly from the pot to the pan with the mushroom-pesto sauce. A little extra water clinging to the pasta is good!
9. Toss everything together vigorously, adding more reserved pasta water as needed to create a glossy, cohesive sauce that coats every piece of pasta. The sauce should cling, not pool.
10. Taste and adjust seasoning if needed. Add red pepper flakes for heat if desired.
11. Transfer to plates or bowls while hot. Top with freshly grated Parmesan and a final drizzle of olive oil if desired.
12. Enjoy your delicious "second dinner"!
Nutrition: Each serving provides approximately 340 calories, 12-14g protein, 14g fat, 42g carbohydrates, 4g fiber, and significant B vitamins and selenium.

Name: Quick Vegetable Fried Rice
Short Description: Rice is only boring if you make it boring - this is my absolute favorite way to transform leftover rice into something elevated and delicious in less than 10 minutes.
Introduction:
Rice is only boring if you make it boring! That's my philosophy, and this recipe proves it. When faced with leftover rice (in this case, a mixture of rice and quinoa), instead of just reheating it plain in the microwave, I transform it into something elevated and delicious in less than 10 minutes. This is my absolute favorite thing to do with leftover rice, and once you try it, it'll become yours too.

The beauty of making fried rice at home is that I control everything - the amount of oil, the sodium level, the vegetables, all of it. Restaurant fried rice can be loaded with excess oil and salt, but my homemade version gives me all that delicious flavor while being genuinely healthier. Plus, with the addition of eggs and vegetables, I'm creating a complete protein meal that's way more nutritious than plain rice.

This recipe is incredibly forgiving and adaptable. I use whatever leftover grain I have - rice, quinoa, or a mixture. I grab any bag of mixed vegetables from my freezer. I adjust the seasonings to my taste. The formula is simple, the execution is quick, and the results are always satisfying. It's the kind of recipe that makes me excited about leftovers.

I make this at least once a week, sometimes more. It's become my go-to quick dinner when I don't feel like cooking something elaborate but still want something nourishing and delicious. And I love how customizable it is - sometimes I add extra vegetables, sometimes I throw in some tofu for more protein, sometimes I make it spicy with extra chili sauce. The base recipe is so solid that you can riff on it endlessly.
Why You'll Love This:
- **Super quick** - Ready in less than 10 minutes from start to finish
- **Uses up leftovers** - Perfect for leftover rice or grain mixtures I have in the fridge
- **Complete protein** - Eggs and grains together provide all essential amino acids
- **Healthier than takeout** - I control oil and sodium while maximizing nutrition
- **Infinitely customizable** - I use any vegetables, proteins, or seasonings I like
Tips:
- Leftover rice works much better than fresh - it's drier and fries without getting mushy
- Day-old rice from the fridge is ideal because the grains separate better
- I use any frozen or fresh mixed vegetables I have on hand
- Add extra protein like cubed tofu, edamame, or cooked chicken
- Substitute tamari or coconut aminos for gluten-free option
- I love adding a drizzle of sesame oil at the end for extra authentic flavor
- Top with sliced green onions, sesame seeds, or sriracha
- Try with cauliflower rice for a low-carb version
- Add minced garlic or fresh ginger for even more flavor
Ingredients Overview:
This recipe works with any leftover grain I have - plain rice, quinoa, or a mixture of both like in the video. The drier texture of day-old rice makes it perfect for frying. I need 2 eggs for protein, which I scramble separately first to ensure they don't become overcooked or rubbery later. A bag of mixed vegetables (any variety works) adds color, nutrition, and texture. Frozen veggie mixes are super convenient, but fresh vegetables work great too.

The magic is in the seasoning combination. Low sodium soy sauce (or coconut aminos for a gluten-free option) provides that essential umami and salty depth. Seasoned rice vinegar adds tanginess and brightness. Turmeric gives the rice a beautiful golden color and anti-inflammatory benefits. Umami mushroom spice (if you can find it) amps up that savory depth even more. Ginger paste or powder brings warmth and authentic fried rice flavor. Just a light spray or drizzle of oil keeps everything from sticking to the pan.

What I've learned about fried rice is that the seasonings are where you can really play. The base combination I use here is my tried-and-true formula, but sometimes I add a splash of sesame oil at the end for nuttiness, or a squirt of sriracha for heat, or some oyster sauce if I want a more traditional flavor. The leftover grain and vegetables are just a canvas - the seasonings are where you make it your own.
Instructions Overview:
The process is wonderfully simple and fast. I start by heating a pan or wok over medium heat with just a light spray or drizzle of oil. First, I scramble the eggs directly in the pan until completely cooked, then set them aside. This ensures they stay tender and don't overcook when I add them back later.

In the same pan, I add my mixed vegetables and cook them until slightly tender but still with some bite. Then I add the leftover rice, using my spatula to break up any clumps and incorporate it with the vegetables. Once the rice is heated through and mixed with the veggies, I add all my seasonings: soy sauce, rice vinegar, turmeric, umami mushroom spice, and ginger. I mix everything thoroughly until the rice is beautifully colored and all the seasonings are distributed.

Finally, I toss the scrambled eggs back into the pan and give everything one last stir to distribute them evenly throughout the rice. The whole process takes less than 10 minutes, and the result is this satisfying, flavorful fried rice that's so much better than plain reheated leftovers.

I love how straightforward this is. There's no complicated technique, no special equipment needed. Just a pan, some leftover rice, whatever vegetables I have on hand, and a handful of pantry seasonings. It's the kind of recipe that's so simple and reliable that I can make it on autopilot, even on my most tired weeknights.
FAQ:
**Can I use fresh rice instead of leftover rice?**
Fresh rice can work, but it tends to get mushy and sticky because of the extra moisture. If I must use fresh rice, I spread it on a sheet pan and refrigerate it for at least 30 minutes to dry it out first. Day-old refrigerated rice is really ideal for fried rice.

**What vegetables work best in fried rice?**
Honestly, anything! Frozen mixed vegetable bags are super convenient. Fresh options like carrots, peas, bell peppers, broccoli, edamame, corn, or green beans all work beautifully. I use whatever I have or whatever needs to be used up.

**Can I make this vegan?**
Yes! I skip the eggs entirely and add extra vegetables, or substitute with crumbled firm tofu or extra-firm tofu cubes for protein. I could also add edamame or other plant-based protein sources.

Author: Holistic Bravo
Recipe Card Ingredients:
- 2-3 cups leftover cooked rice (or rice and quinoa mixture - day-old from the fridge works best)
- 2 large eggs
- 1 bag (12-16 oz) frozen mixed vegetables (or 2 cups fresh mixed vegetables)
- 2 tbsp low sodium soy sauce or coconut aminos (for gluten-free)
- 1 tbsp seasoned rice vinegar
- 1 tsp turmeric powder (for color and nutrition)
- 1 tsp umami mushroom seasoning (or substitute with garlic powder)
- 1 tbsp ginger paste or ginger powder
- Oil or cooking spray (for pan)

**Optional additions:**
- 1 tsp sesame oil (drizzle at the end)
- Sliced green onions (for garnish)
- Sesame seeds (for garnish)
- Sriracha or chili sauce (for heat)
Recipe Card Instructions:
1. Heat a large pan or wok over medium heat. Lightly spray or drizzle with a small amount of oil.
2. Crack 2 eggs directly into the hot pan. Scramble with a spatula until completely cooked through, about 2-3 minutes.
3. Remove scrambled eggs from pan and set aside on a plate.
4. In the same pan (no need to wash), add frozen or fresh mixed vegetables. Cook over medium heat for 3-4 minutes, stirring occasionally, until vegetables are slightly tender but still have some bite.
5. Add leftover rice (or rice/quinoa mixture) to the pan with the vegetables. Use your spatula to break up any clumps and mix the rice with the veggies, stirring well to combine.
6. Once rice is heated through and incorporated with vegetables (about 2-3 minutes), add all seasonings: 2 tbsp soy sauce, 1 tbsp rice vinegar, 1 tsp turmeric, 1 tsp umami mushroom spice, and 1 tbsp ginger.
7. Mix everything thoroughly, stirring constantly for 1-2 minutes until all seasonings are evenly distributed and the rice is nicely colored.
8. Toss the scrambled eggs back into the pan with everything else.
9. Give everything a final stir for 1 minute until the eggs are evenly distributed throughout the rice.
10. Taste and adjust seasonings if needed. Add sesame oil for extra flavor if desired.
11. Transfer to bowls or plates. Top with green onions, sesame seeds, or sriracha if using.
12. Serve immediately and enjoy! This makes enough for about 2 servings or 2 days of meals.
Nutrition: Each serving provides approximately 310 calories, 12-14g protein, 8g fat, 48g carbohydrates, 5g fiber, and significant vitamin A, C, iron, and B vitamins.

Name: Classic Shortbread Cookies
Short Description: I'm a self-proclaimed sugar addict, and these simple three-ingredient shortbread cookies are perfect for those moments when I need just a little something sweet at the end of the day.
Introduction:
I'm a self-proclaimed sugar addict, and honestly, who can blame me? Sometimes I just need something sweet at the end of the day - not anything big or elaborate, just a little something to signal to my body that I'm done eating. These shortbread cookies are perfect for exactly that moment.

I made these gingerbread-man-shaped shortbread cookies again, and this time they turned out so much better. The secret? Patience. By taking the time to let the dough rest in the fridge for a whole hour, the butter firms up and the gluten relaxes, creating that perfect tender, crumbly, melt-in-your-mouth texture that makes shortbread so addictive.

True shortbread is magnificently simple - just three ingredients create these buttery, delicate cookies that are somehow both crispy and tender at the same time. They're perfect for holiday baking, gift-giving, or just keeping a batch around for those moments when I need a sweet treat. The simplicity is the point - no fancy add-ins needed, just pure buttery perfection.

I love these cookies because they're so versatile. During the holidays, I cut them into festive shapes and decorate them with royal icing. For everyday treats, I just cut them into simple rounds or squares. Sometimes I dip them in melted chocolate for an extra special touch. And because the dough freezes beautifully, I can make a big batch and bake them fresh whenever the craving strikes.
Why You'll Love This:
- **Only three ingredients** - Butter, sugar, and flour create perfection
- **Melt-in-your-mouth texture** - That signature crumbly, tender shortbread texture I crave
- **Perfect for any occasion** - Holidays, gifts, tea time, or everyday treats
- **Easy to customize** - I add vanilla, lemon, or spices for variations
- **Great for gift-giving** - Beautiful when decorated with icing
Tips:
- Don't skip the chilling time - it makes a huge difference in texture and prevents spreading
- Room temperature butter is essential - I leave it out for 30-60 minutes before baking
- Don't overbake - cookies should be pale with just barely golden edges
- I roll dough between two sheets of parchment paper to prevent sticking
- Add 1 teaspoon vanilla extract for classic vanilla shortbread
- Mix in lemon zest for bright citrus shortbread
- I love dipping cooled cookies in melted chocolate for an elegant touch
- Add spices like cinnamon or ginger for spiced shortbread variation
- Press dough into a pan and cut into squares after baking for easier preparation
Ingredients Overview:
Classic shortbread requires only three ingredients, and the quality really matters since there's nowhere to hide. I need 1½ sticks of butter - it must be at room temperature, not cold from the fridge and not melted. I leave it out on the counter for 30-60 minutes before I start baking. Room temperature butter creams properly with the sugar and creates the right texture. I use unsalted butter so I can control the salt level, though a pinch of salt can be added if desired.

Powdered sugar (also called confectioners' sugar or icing sugar) is crucial here - I never substitute granulated sugar. The powdered sugar dissolves completely into the butter and creates that fine, delicate crumb that makes shortbread so tender. I use ½ cup. Finally, 1½ cups of all-purpose flour provides structure. The ratio of these three ingredients is what creates that signature shortbread texture - high butter content for richness and tenderness, just enough sugar for sweetness without being overpowering, and flour to hold it all together.

I've tried variations over the years - adding vanilla extract, lemon zest, or spices - and they're all delicious. But there's something beautiful about the simplicity of the classic three-ingredient version. The butter flavor really shines through, and that tender, crumbly texture is just perfect. Sometimes I add a pinch of salt to enhance the butter flavor, but even that's optional.
Instructions Overview:
The process begins with creaming room temperature butter and powdered sugar together until fluffy and lightened in color. I use a hand mixer or stand mixer for this, though a wooden spoon and some elbow grease work too. The mixture should be smooth and well incorporated.

Next, I add the flour and mix just until a soft dough forms. The dough will start crumbly but will come together as I continue mixing. The key is not to overmix once it forms a cohesive dough, or the cookies will be tough. I shape the dough into a ball or disk, wrap it tightly, and refrigerate for at least 1 hour. This chilling step is crucial and not optional - it firms up the butter and relaxes the gluten for the best texture.

After chilling, I roll the dough out to about ¼ inch thickness on a floured surface and cut it into shapes. I transfer the cut cookies to a parchment-lined baking sheet and bake at 325°F for 12-15 minutes. The relatively low temperature ensures gentle baking for tender cookies. I watch them carefully and remove them when they're barely golden at the edges but still pale in the center.

The cookies will be very soft when hot, so I let them cool on the baking sheet for 5 minutes before transferring to a wire rack. They firm up as they cool, developing that perfect tender yet crisp texture that makes shortbread so irresistible.
FAQ:
**Why do I need to chill the dough for so long?**
Chilling serves two important purposes. First, it firms up the butter, which prevents the cookies from spreading too much during baking and helps them hold their shape. Second, it allows the gluten in the flour to relax, which results in more tender, less tough cookies. It really does make a huge difference!

**Can I freeze the dough?**
Yes! Shortbread dough freezes beautifully. I shape it into a log, wrap tightly in plastic wrap, and freeze for up to 3 months. When I'm ready to bake, I either thaw it in the fridge and roll it out, or slice frozen dough into rounds and bake, adding 1-2 extra minutes to the baking time.

**Why are my cookies too crumbly and falling apart?**
This usually means they were overbaked. I always remove shortbread from the oven when barely golden at the edges. If they weren't overbaked, the dough might not have been mixed enough to properly bring the butter and flour together - it needs to form a cohesive dough.
