Author: Holistic Bravo
Recipe Card Ingredients:
- 1½ cups all-purpose flour
- 1½ sticks (¾ cup or 12 tbsp) unsalted butter, at room temperature (leave out 30-60 minutes before baking)
- ½ cup powdered sugar (also called confectioners' sugar or icing sugar)

**Optional additions:**
- Pinch of salt (¼ tsp, for enhancing butter flavor)
- 1 tsp vanilla extract (for vanilla shortbread)
- 1 tbsp lemon zest (for citrus shortbread)
- ½ tsp cinnamon or ginger (for spiced shortbread)
Recipe Card Instructions:
1. In a large bowl, combine 1½ sticks room temperature butter with ½ cup powdered sugar.
2. Using a hand mixer, stand mixer, or wooden spoon, cream together until well incorporated and fluffy. The mixture should be smooth and lightened in color, about 2-3 minutes.
3. Add 1½ cups all-purpose flour to the butter-sugar mixture.
4. Mix just until a soft dough forms. The dough will start crumbly but will come together as you mix. Don't overmix once it forms a cohesive dough or the cookies will be tough.
5. Shape the dough into a ball or disk. Wrap tightly in plastic wrap or cover the bowl.
6. Refrigerate for at least 1 hour (this step is crucial - don't skip it!).
7. About 15 minutes before baking, preheat oven to 325°F. Line a baking sheet with parchment paper.
8. Remove chilled dough from refrigerator. If too hard to roll, let sit at room temperature for 5-10 minutes to soften slightly.
9. On a generously floured surface (or between two sheets of parchment paper), roll the dough out to about ¼ inch thickness.
10. Use cookie cutters to cut dough into desired shapes (gingerbread men, stars, circles, etc.).
11. Place cut cookies on prepared baking sheet, spacing about 1 inch apart. They won't spread much.
12. Bake at 325°F for 12-15 minutes. Watch carefully - cookies should be barely golden at the edges but still pale in the center. Don't overbake!
13. Remove from oven and let cookies cool on the baking sheet for 5 minutes. They'll be very soft when hot but will firm up as they cool.
14. Transfer cookies to a wire rack to cool completely before decorating or serving.
15. Store in an airtight container at room temperature for up to 2 weeks.
Nutrition: Each cookie provides approximately 90 calories, 1g protein, 6g fat, 9g carbohydrates, and trace amounts of calcium and iron.

Name: Pan-Cooked Eggs with Tomatoes
Short Description: During the busy holiday season, I created this quick, nutritious breakfast using tomatoes close to expiration - eggs cooked on halved tomatoes with fresh microgreens and pickled cabbage for gut health.
Introduction:
Tis the season! During the busy holiday season, I was having fun with my Nespresso advent calendar (day three officially when I made this!), and it's great having a little surprise each morning. But even with holiday excitement, breakfast still needs to be simple and nourishing. This recipe came together because I had some tomatoes that were close to their expiration date - and instead of letting them go to waste, I transformed them into this beautiful, simple breakfast.

The concept is brilliantly simple: halve the tomatoes and cook them cut-side down in a pan to release their natural juices, then crack fresh eggs directly on top, add some bok choy microgreens, and cover it all to steam gently. The tomatoes create a flavorful, juicy base for the eggs, and the microgreens add a fresh, peppery element. Serving it with pickled cabbage on the side adds probiotics to support gut health and digestion - perfect for the indulgent holiday season.

This is the kind of breakfast that looks impressive but requires almost no effort. It's wholesome, uses up produce before it goes bad, and provides plenty of protein to fuel my holiday activities. Whether it's Christmas morning or just a regular Tuesday, this simple pan-cooked egg dish delivers.

I love how this recipe represents mindful, waste-free cooking even during the chaos of the holidays. Those tomatoes were getting soft and would have ended up in the compost, but cooking them this way actually makes their softness an advantage - they break down and create this gorgeous, flavorful base for the eggs. And the pickled cabbage on the side? That's my little gut health insurance during a season of indulgent foods.
Why You'll Love This:
- **Saves produce from waste** - Perfect for using tomatoes close to expiration
- **Ready in 15 minutes** - Quick enough for busy holiday mornings or any day
- **High protein** - 18-21g protein from three eggs keeps me satisfied
- **Gut health support** - Pickled vegetables provide beneficial probiotics
- **Minimal cleanup** - One pan is all I need
Tips:
- I use any type of tomatoes I have - cherry, roma, beefsteak, or heirloom
- Any microgreens work beautifully: radish, sunflower, pea shoots, or arugula
- If I don't have microgreens, I use baby spinach, arugula, or chopped kale
- Substitute sauerkraut or kimchi for pickled cabbage
- I often add minced garlic or herbs to the pan with the tomatoes for extra flavor
- Top with hot sauce, salsa, or pesto after cooking
- Covering the pan helps eggs cook evenly; alternatively, I baste with tomato juices
- Serve over toast or with crusty bread for a heartier meal
- Add cheese on top of the eggs for richness
Ingredients Overview:
This recipe is all about simplicity and using what I have. I need 2-3 tomatoes of any variety - this is the perfect way to use up tomatoes that are starting to get a bit soft and would otherwise go to waste. Cooking them cut-side down in the pan releases their natural juices and intensifies their sweet-savory flavor, creating a delicious base for the eggs.

Three fresh eggs provide plenty of protein to keep me satisfied through the morning. Bok choy microgreens (or any microgreens I have) add a fresh, peppery note and look beautiful on top, plus they're packed with nutrients. If I don't have microgreens, regular greens work just fine. Salt and pepper season everything simply. A little oil or cooking spray keeps things from sticking.

The pickled cabbage served on the side is optional but recommended, especially during the holidays when my digestion might need some support. Any fermented vegetables - pickled cabbage, sauerkraut, or kimchi - provide beneficial probiotics that support gut health and aid digestion.

What I appreciate about this ingredient list is its flexibility. The tomatoes can be any variety, any level of ripeness (softer is actually better for this preparation). The greens can be microgreens, baby greens, or chopped mature greens. And the fermented vegetables on the side can be whatever I have in the fridge - pickled cabbage, sauerkraut, kimchi, or even pickled onions.
Instructions Overview:
The process starts with heating a medium pan over medium heat and lightly oiling it. I halve my tomatoes - enough halves to cover most of the bottom of the pan - and place them cut-side down in the hot pan. I let them cook for 2-3 minutes without moving them, which allows them to soften and release their delicious juices.

Once the tomatoes have created this flavorful base, I crack three fresh eggs directly on top of them, spacing them out evenly. The tomatoes hold the eggs in place. I season with salt and pepper, then scatter microgreens over everything. Placing a lid on the pan traps the steam and helps cook the tops of the eggs evenly. I cook for 5-7 minutes depending on how I like my eggs - 5 minutes for runny yolks, 7 or more for fully set yolks.

When the egg whites are completely set and opaque, I carefully transfer everything to a plate using a spatula, trying to keep the eggs and tomatoes together. I serve with pickled cabbage on the side for that probiotic boost and tangy contrast to the rich eggs.

The whole thing is incredibly simple and comes together in one pan in about 15 minutes. It's the kind of breakfast I can make even when I'm half-asleep on a holiday morning, but it looks and tastes like I put in way more effort than I actually did.
FAQ:
**Can I use regular greens instead of microgreens?**
Absolutely! Baby spinach, arugula, or chopped kale all work great. Microgreens are lovely because they're tender and packed with nutrients, but regular greens are just as delicious and nutritious.

**What if I don't have pickled vegetables?**
The main dish is still complete and delicious without them. I can serve with fresh vegetables, avocado, or just skip the side entirely. The fermented vegetables are a nice addition for gut health but certainly not required.

**Do I need to cover the pan?**
Covering the pan creates steam that helps cook the tops of the eggs evenly, especially the whites. If I prefer, I can leave it uncovered and baste the eggs with the tomato juices using a spoon, though this requires more attention.

Author: Holistic Bravo
Recipe Card Ingredients:
- 2-3 medium tomatoes (any variety - cherry, roma, beefsteak, or heirloom work great)
- 3 large fresh eggs
- 1 handful bok choy microgreens (or any microgreens, baby spinach, arugula, or chopped kale)
- ¼ cup pickled cabbage (or substitute sauerkraut or kimchi), for serving
- Salt and pepper, to taste (about ½ tsp each)
- 1 tsp oil or cooking spray

**Optional additions:**
- 1 clove garlic, minced (add with tomatoes)
- Fresh herbs like basil or parsley
- Hot sauce, salsa, or pesto (for topping)
- Crusty bread or toast (for serving)
Recipe Card Instructions:
1. Place a medium-sized pan (8-10 inch) over medium heat. Lightly oil the pan or spray with cooking spray.
2. Halve 2-3 tomatoes (you should have enough halves to cover most of the bottom of your pan).
3. Place tomato halves cut-side down in the hot pan. Let cook for 2-3 minutes without moving them. This allows them to soften and release their juices into the pan.
4. Crack 3 fresh eggs directly on top of the tomatoes in the pan, spacing them out evenly. The tomatoes will hold the eggs in place.
5. Season eggs with salt and pepper to taste (about ½ tsp each).
6. Scatter bok choy microgreens (or your choice of greens) over everything in the pan.
7. Place a lid on the pan to trap steam. This helps cook the tops of the eggs evenly.
8. Cook covered for 5-7 minutes: For runny yolks, aim for 5 minutes; for fully set yolks, cook for 7 minutes or more. The egg whites should be completely set and opaque.
9. Check doneness - whites should be fully cooked and no longer translucent.
10. Carefully transfer everything to a plate using a spatula, keeping the eggs and tomatoes together as much as possible.
11. Serve immediately with pickled cabbage (or sauerkraut/kimchi) on the side.
12. Add optional toppings like hot sauce, herbs, or cheese if desired.
13. Enjoy while hot!
Nutrition: Each serving provides approximately 280 calories, 18-21g protein, 16g fat, 14g carbohydrates, 3g fiber, and essential vitamins D, B12, and A plus probiotics from fermented vegetables.

Name: Chinese Souffle Steamed Eggs
Short Description: If you're bored of traditional scrambled eggs, try this - silky smooth Chinese-style steamed eggs with a flan-like texture that took me 5-10 tries to perfect, but now I've cracked the code.
Introduction:
If you're bored of traditional scrambled eggs, it's time to try something new and completely different. These Chinese soufflé eggs are actually very easy to make - the hardest part is simply having the patience to let the eggs cook at a slow rate. But trust me when I say: you can do this too. Just trust the process.

The texture of these eggs is similar to a flan or panna cotta - very smooth, silky, and absolutely delicious on the palate. It took me probably 5 to 10 times of trying this dish before I got it right, before I achieved this perfect flan-like consistency. I kept overcooking them at first, and they'd end up turning into an omelette with a bunch of bubbles on the top instead of that beautifully smooth surface.

But I finally cracked the code (pun intended), and now you can benefit from all that trial and error. The recipe itself is deceptively simple - just eggs, boiling water, and love. The technique is where the magic happens. This is for those mornings when you have 20 minutes to kill and want a truly decadent breakfast. The result is impossibly tender eggs with that perfect little jiggle when you cut into them.

I share this recipe with you knowing full well that your first attempt might not be perfect - mine certainly wasn't! But stick with it. The technique is simple once you understand the key principles: remove ALL the air bubbles, turn OFF the heat before steaming, and have patience. Those three things will get you that perfect, silky, flan-like texture. And once you nail it, you'll be making these every week like I do.
Why You'll Love This:
- **Unique texture** - Like flan or panna cotta, completely different from any other egg dish I've tried
- **Simple ingredients** - Just eggs, water, and love (seriously!)
- **Impressive results** - Restaurant-quality technique I can master at home
- **High protein** - 12-14g protein per serving keeps me satisfied
- **Worth the learning curve** - Once I nailed the technique, I make these again and again
Tips:
- Removing ALL air bubbles is absolutely critical for that smooth, silky surface - I take my time with this
- Turn OFF the heat before adding the egg bowl - this is the key to avoiding overcooking
- Patience is essential - I resist the urge to increase the heat or check too early
- The eggs should jiggle like panna cotta when done, not be completely firm
- If I don't have a steamer rack, I use a heat-safe bowl elevated on something in a pot with less water
- I love adding toppings like green onions, sesame oil, or crispy fried shallots for extra flavor
- For savory variation, I mix in a bit of chicken broth instead of plain water
- I often serve this with congee (rice porridge) for a traditional Chinese breakfast
- Can be served warm or at room temperature
Ingredients Overview:
This is minimalist cooking at its finest. I need just 2 eggs and ⅓ cup of boiling water. The ratio of eggs to water is crucial - this 2:⅓ proportion creates that signature custard-like texture. The water must be boiling when I add it to the eggs to start the gentle cooking process. And then there's the most important ingredient: LOVE. I insist on this, and honestly, the patience and care I put into this dish really do show in the results.

For serving, I drizzle the finished eggs with soy sauce for that essential savory, umami flavor and rice vinegar for a subtle tang that brightens the rich eggs. These two simple seasonings are traditional and perfect - they don't overwhelm the delicate egg flavor but enhance it beautifully. If I want to get fancy, I add green onions, a drizzle of sesame oil, or crispy fried shallots, but the eggs are stunning with just soy sauce and vinegar.

The ingredient list might seem almost too simple to work, but that's the beauty of this dish. It's all about technique rather than ingredients. Those two eggs and that precise amount of boiling water, combined with the right steaming technique, create something that's so much more than the sum of its parts. It's the kind of dish that teaches you that cooking is as much about how you treat ingredients as what ingredients you use.
Instructions Overview:
The process begins with preparing a steamer - I fill a pan with water and bring it to a rolling boil with the steamer rack ready. While that's heating, I crack 2 eggs into a heatproof bowl and measure ⅓ cup of the boiling water, whisking it into the eggs until very well combined. The boiling water will slightly cook the eggs as I whisk, which is normal and good.

Then comes the critical step: removing every single air bubble. I look at my egg mixture and see lots of little bubbles floating on top and throughout. I take a spoon and meticulously remove ALL of these bubbles, scooping them off and popping them until the surface is perfectly smooth. This step is what gives me that gorgeous, smooth, silky top without holes or pockmarks, and I never rush it.

Here's where the magic happens: I turn OFF the heat completely and move the pan away from the burner before placing the bowl of eggs onto the steamer rack. This is essential! If the heat is too high, I'll end up with an omelette instead of silky steamed eggs. I cover tightly with the lid and then... I wait. The residual heat and steam will cook the eggs slowly and gently for 15-20 minutes.

Patience is everything here. I resist the temptation to peek or check. After 15-20 minutes, I carefully remove the lid. The eggs should be just set but still have a beautiful jiggle in the center, similar to panna cotta or silken tofu. That perfect jiggle means they're done! I drizzle with soy sauce and rice vinegar and enjoy my perfectly silky steamed eggs.
FAQ:
**Why did my eggs turn into an omelette instead of the smooth texture?**
The heat was too high or the cooking was too fast. This is the most common mistake! I always remember to turn OFF the heat completely before adding the bowl of eggs, and I let the residual heat and steam do the cooking slowly. Patience is absolutely essential here.

**Why does my steamed egg have holes and bubbles throughout?**
I probably didn't remove enough air bubbles before steaming, or the heat was too high causing the eggs to bubble during cooking. I take my time removing every single bubble from the surface before steaming, and I make sure the heat is off.

**How do I know when they're perfectly done?**
The eggs should be just set but still jiggle significantly in the center when I gently shake the bowl. I think of the texture of silken tofu or panna cotta - there should be that beautiful wobble. They'll firm up very slightly as they cool, so I don't overcook them trying to make them completely firm.

Author: Holistic Bravo
Recipe Card Ingredients:
- 2 large fresh eggs
- ⅓ cup boiling water (must be boiling, not just hot)
- LOVE ❤️ (seriously, patience and care are essential!)

**For Serving:**
- 1 tbsp soy sauce (for drizzling)
- 1 tsp rice vinegar (for drizzling)

**Optional toppings:**
- 1 tbsp green onions, thinly sliced
- ½ tsp sesame oil
- Crispy fried shallots or garlic
- Fresh cilantro or herbs
Recipe Card Instructions:
1. Fill a pan with water (about 2 inches deep) and bring to a rolling boil. Have your steamer rack ready.
2. Crack 2 eggs into a heatproof bowl that will fit in your steamer.
3. Measure ⅓ cup of the boiling water and whisk it into the eggs until very well combined. The boiling water will slightly cook the eggs as you whisk - this is normal and good!
4. Now the CRITICAL step: Look at your egg mixture - you'll see lots of little bubbles floating on top and throughout. Take a spoon and meticulously remove ALL of these bubbles. Scoop them off, pop them, do whatever it takes to get rid of every single one. Don't rush this step - it's what creates that perfectly smooth surface!
5. Place your steamer rack over the pot of boiling water.
6. Now here's the KEY: Turn OFF the heat completely and move the pan away from the burner. This is essential!
7. Carefully place your bowl with the egg mixture onto the steamer rack.
8. Put the lid on the pot securely. Make sure it's tightly sealed.
9. Be patient! Let it steam undisturbed for 15-20 minutes. Don't peek! The residual heat and steam will cook the eggs slowly and gently.
10. After 15-20 minutes, carefully remove the lid. The eggs should be just set but still have a beautiful jiggle in the center, similar to panna cotta or silken tofu. If you gently shake the bowl, the surface should wobble slightly.
11. Drizzle with 1 tbsp soy sauce and 1 tsp rice vinegar.
12. Add optional toppings like green onions, sesame oil, or fried shallots if desired.
13. Use a spoon to cut into the eggs and enjoy that perfect, silky, smooth texture while warm!
